title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 2
text:
Mission Statement 
 
 
The Environmental Management Authority is committed to protecting and 
conserving the natural environment to enhance the quality of life by 
promoting: 
 
 Environmentally responsible behaviour 
 Development and enforcement of environmental legislation 
 Encouragement of voluntary compliance 
 The use of economic and other incentives 
 
This is to be achieved in an atmosphere of mutual respect, professionalism, 
accountability, transparency, collaboration and social responsibility.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 4
text:
CHAIRMAN’S MESSAGE 
 
For the 2005 reporting period the EMA’s Board of Directors 
operated under the Chairmanship of Dr. John Agard. On behalf 
of the current Board of Directors it is my pleasure to present the 
2005 Annual Report. This report reflects the EMA’s ongoing 
commitment to continually improve environmental protection and 
sustainable resource management in Trinidad and Tobago. On 
behalf of the Board of the Environmental Management Authority 
(EMA) it is my pleasure to present the 2005 Annual Report. We 
achieved a milestone in this reporting year: The EMA completed 
public consultations on the revision of the National Environmental 
Policy (NEP) and forwarded its recommendations to the 
Government. The revised NEP was submitted to the Trinidad and 
Tobago Cabinet in September 2005. 
 
The EMA remains committed to using the best tools available to 
report on the state of the nation’s environment. This 2005 Report departs from the single theme focus of past 
reports and examines the state of several aspects of the environment, which allows reporting of a complex 
topic in a more appropriate manner. Significant effort continues to be made towards the acquisition of data 
that can be used to monitor and track changes in the environment. 
Trinidad and Tobago is the most industrialised country in the Commonwealth Caribbean, and through the use 
of the EMA’s subsidiary legislation, we have continued to work towards our goal of healthy ecosystems and 
protection of our rich biological diversity through the designation of three Environmentally Sensitive Species 
and one Environmentally Sensitive Area. 
While we celebrate our successes in environmental management, we realise that our environment is 
being affected by accelerated development activities and there is much more work to be done. During 
2005 the EMA committed to pursuing the required subsidiary environmental management legislation, 
including the necessary amendments to the Water Pollution Rules, the draft Air Pollution Rules and the 
draft Deposit Refund Bill. These pieces of legislation will ensure a strong system of environmental and 
human health protection. 
Mr. Shyam Dyal 
Deputy Chairman 
January 2013

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 5
text:
MESSAGE FROM THE 
MANAGING DIRECTOR/CHIEF EXECUTIVE OFFICER 
 
 
I am pleased to present the Environmental Management Authority’s 
(EMA) 2005 Assessment of the State of the Environment Report 
(ASOE). 
It is my belief that closer collaboration and more effective communication 
with our internal and external stakeholders are crucial to the delivery of 
the environmental targets established for the Authority. In 2005, the 
EMA explored new and creative ways to achieve its targets and worked 
towards them by establishing systems to ensure access to the best 
available scientific and technological information; determining which 
problems pose the most immediate and critical risks to our natural 
environment, human health and ultimately the quality of life available to 
citizens. 
On the operational front, the EMA established a new office in La 
Romain allowing it to better serve the public of south Trinidad. Members 
of the public became able to access the EMA’s services at locations in 
north Trinidad, south Trinidad and Tobago. 
The EMA continued to record steady progress in monitoring and protecting the environment. Trinidad and 
Tobago’s first ambient air quality monitoring station was commissioned at Pt. Lisas. The station is 
equipped to continuously monitor carbon monoxide, sulphur and nitrogen oxides, ground level ozone, 
particulate matter and meteorological parameters. This project represented the preliminary phase in 
establishing a National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Network and has enabled technical capacity 
building in the field of air quality monitoring for those involved. The result was and continues to be the 
collection of continuous time-series ambient air quality data from the most industrialized part of Trinidad 
and Tobago. 
The EMA continued its groundwater quality-monitoring programme at 32 wells and boreholes 
throughout Trinidad and Tobago. This field survey revealed contamination of groundwater from 
gasoline stations in Maraval, in Charlieville in Central Trinidad as well as Scarborough and Canaan in 
Tobago. Some of the Water and Sewerage Authority's potable water producing catchments also showed 
signs of contamination. Those catchments were in Four Roads in the Diego Martin Producing Field; in 
Curepe in the Valsayn Producing Field and at Don Miguel Road, which is part of the El Socorro Gravels. 
By designating the Manatee, Pawi and Sabrewing Hummingbird as Environmentally Sensitive 
Species, the EMA continued to advance its mandate to protect key species. Legal Notices were signed 
on World Environment Day - June 5, and gazetted on June 17, Legal Supplement Part B – Vol.44, No. 
98 – June 2005. 
The EMA has also been very busy in fulfilling its mandate to educate the public about the nation’s 
environmental issues through awareness programmes. In 2005, there were many collaborative projects 
undertaken with our sister agencies. These included an Anti-Litter campaign with TIDCO; a Forest Fire 
Awareness and Prevention programme with the Forestry Division; the Prime Minister’s Best Village 
Environment and Sanitation Competition with the Ministry of Community Development and Gender Affairs 
and Tidy T&T.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 6
text:
The management and staff committed to work assiduously to achieve results to make tangible differences 
to our fellow citizens, offering them a safer, healthier environment and creating stronger partnerships with 
all sectors of society. The environment is everyone’s business and we see our role in changing attitudes, 
behaviours and ultimately our culture, to one of respect for our environment, as critical. 
 
 
Dr. Joth Singh 
Managing Director/CEO

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 20
text:
xiv 
 
 
Executive Summary 
 
 
GENERAL 
Section 14(1) of the Environmental Management Act (Act 3 of 2000) mandates the Environmental 
Management Authority (EMA) to report annually on the state of the national environment. The 
Assessment of the State of Environment (ASOE) provides a public forum for informed discourse on the 
state of some aspects of the natural environment, the human actions or natural phenomena which impact 
negatively on the environment and actions taken to reduce or ameliorate the negative ones. This ASOE 
which responds to the EMA’s mandate is organised around eight chapters, of which the first two deal with 
the new approach taken in the presentation of its contents and the main drivers influencing environmental 
change. Each of the remaining six chapters deals with a theme identified as critical to the natural 
environment. The themes include the atmosphere; land; coasts and oceans; inland waters; biodiversity; 
and economy, industry and resource use. These thematic areas represent the utilisation of resources that 
are being impacted by human action either in their use in building human civilization or as by-products of 
human activities. 
For the most part, previous ASOEs prepared by the EMA have had a single focus or a single 
theme. This 2005 Report departs from this trend in that several aspects of the environment are dealt 
with simultaneously. 
This approach allows reporting of a complex topic in a more appropriate manner. It is also the base 
for development of a system which, over time, will enable the EMA to track changes continuously in the 
environment, and monitor and report nationally and internationally on these changes. The approach is 
therefore more consistent with the spirit of national legislation, as well as with Trinidad and Tobago’s 
international obligations under the several conventions on aspects of the natural environment to which it 
is signatory. 
The Report recognises the manipulation of the natural environment over time and acknowledges 
the well-known relationship between environmental change and socio-economic activities. It notes a list of 
factors or drivers identified by the Millennium Ecosystem Assessment (MA) which aid in the destruction of 
the natural environment. These drivers are both direct (e.g. physical, biological and chemical drivers) and 
indirect (e.g. demographic, economic, socio-political, scientific and technical, as well as religious drivers). 
The Report focuses on the natural resource component of the environment as defined in the 
Environmental Management Act of 2000, but it also includes social, economic and cultural elements as 
important components. Thus, one of the six themes is economy and industry. 
The model adopted in this Report which also will be utilized in the future, is known as the 
Pressure–State–Response (PSR) Model which is used by a large number of countries for national and 
international reporting on the natural environment. The usefulness of the PSR Model for Trinidad and 
Tobago lies in the fact that it reflects the key environmental issues which are of concern to stakeholder 
agencies. It also reflects indicators on the key issues which pose the greatest risk to the environment. 
Perhaps more importantly, many of the key issues such as pollution, flooding, urbanisation, and forest 
fires are widely known and understood nationally. The model will allow for more detailed examination of 
these as they affect the natural environment and the natural resources of the two islands. 
The issues were derived from the Environmental Committee with responsibility for preparation of 
the ASOE, interviews with representatives of stakeholder agencies and a review of plans and 
environmental reports. Relevant issues were identified for each thematic area and guided the Report’s 
preparation.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 21
text:
xv 
 
 
The Report proposes a set of indicators for use with the PSR Model and separates them into actions 
which can be described as pressure on the resource, state of the resource or response to the pressure or 
state of the resource in each of the thematic areas described in the report. The environmental indicators are 
intended to be used to collect relevant information on environmental health and quality, provide greater 
insights into their condition and trends. 
The Report points out the different sources of data and notes that there are major gaps in data 
availability and reporting. It proposes that agreement be reached among stakeholder agencies on both 
the number and type of indicators to be used across thematic areas now and in the future, and 
appropriate methodologies to be developed in collection and reporting. 
Each of the six thematic areas is elaborated on in a separate chapter on the basis of the PSR Model. 
The overview generally describes the nature of the resource, the role it plays in the global or local environmental 
system and its utilisation in national development. The issues are explored in terms of determining some of the 
explicit human actions impacting on the resource (the pressure) which are aiding in the change of the resource. 
The current condition of the resource (the state) is also assessed, along with the direction of change in human 
action or in the resource (the trend), and the actions taken or being pursued through private or public 
intervention to mitigate or ameliorate the change in the resource, or to prevent or decrease continued human 
action (the response). 
FINDINGS 
Atmosphere 
The atmosphere is described as a blanket of gas surrounding the earth. While it consists of several 
layers, the two layers closest to the earth’s surface (the troposphere and the stratosphere) are crucial in 
the maintenance of environmental conditions necessary for continued life on earth. However, human 
activities (which give rise to vehicular emissions, bush fires and other pollutants) are compromising the 
ability of these two layers of the atmosphere to function effectively. 
The main issues identified in respect of the atmosphere are climate variability and change; 
enhanced green house effects and global warming; air quality (vehicle emissions and particulate 
pollutants); stratospheric ozone depletion; and Sahara dust. The pressures exerted on the atmosphere 
were identified as increased concentrations of ozone depleting material; increased atmospheric 
concentration of anthropogenic greenhouse gases (GHGs) such as carbon dioxide, methane and nitrous 
oxide; and increased concentration of tropospheric ozone. 
The changing state or condition of the resource as a consequence of human actions is seen in 
increased flooding, sea level rise, increase in extreme rain events, increased coastal erosion, and global 
warming (increased air and ocean temperatures) among others. 
General reactions or responses to the changing conditions of the atmosphere include both national 
legislation and ratification of international protocols. At the local level, draft legislation designed to effect 
the reduction in air pollutants discharged into the atmosphere from stationary sources such as industrial 
enterprises, have been developed. At the international level, Trinidad and Tobago will formulate cost-
effective national programmes designed to improve the quality of local emissions and reduce the 
emission of greenhouse gases, consistent with the objectives of the Kyoto Protocol, which this country 
has ratified. In addition, this country will formulate and implement measures to facilitate adequate 
adaptation to climate change. 
Land 
Land is described in Chapter 4 as the “… terrestrial bio-productive system that comprises soil, 
vegetation, other biota, and the ecological and hydrological processes that operate within the system”. It

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 22
text:
xvi 
 
 
provides for services and products which make human existence possible, and especially for regulation of 
the atmospheric and water cycles. Of Trinidad and Tobago’s 5,128,000 hectares of land, agriculture 
including forestry accounts for 333,000 hectares while land for mining exploitation and reserve accounts 
for approximately 5,435 hectares. Urbanisation consumes more than 127,000 hectares. 
The main issues identified in respect of the land are mining for the supply of oil, asphalt and 
aggregates; agriculture including forestry for food, shelter and industrial materials; urbanisation or human 
settlement including residential, commercial and industrial spaces; and pollution from the disposal of solid 
and liquid waste as by-products of human effort and physical development. 
The country has deposits of asphalt, oil and natural gas, which constitute its hydrocarbon sector as 
well as mineral deposits, mostly aggregates and clays. These have been exploited over the years, the 
former mostly for export and the latter principally for usage in the local construction industry. The 
demand for aggregate has outstripped the local industry's capacity to supply. This demand is fuelled by 
increasing urbanisation, principally an expansive housing construction programme, construction of office 
buildings, industrial estates and road infrastructure. 
Small landholder agricultural production is the main agricultural activity practised since plantation 
agriculture, such as sugar cane and coconuts, has been in decline over the decades. Nonetheless, 
agriculture, including forestry, is the single largest user of the resource. 
Pressure brought to bear on the land includes oil spills from ruptured, leaking and abandoned oil 
lines, pumps and processing facilities; squatting, slash and burn land clearance methods, bush fires and 
increasing use of agro-chemicals; change in land use from agriculture to housing, industry and roads; and 
indiscriminate disposal of solid, liquid and hazardous waste. 
Changes in the resource as a consequence of human action include de-vegetated hillsides and 
modification of the land form, which in turn will result in greater frequency of flooding in the floodplains; siltation 
of streams and rivers; and pollution of land and waterways. 
Responses to changes in resources and/or human actions include legislation, and programmes and 
training to modify behaviour. Existing laws have been amended to increase penalties for illegal or unauthorised 
occupation or use of State lands for mining, agricultural or residential squatting. Additional legislation is 
pending regarding changes in rules and regulations governing mining operations, especially relevant to the 
restoration of played-out or abandoned sites. A programme of regularisation of squatters on State lands is 
being pursued by the Land Settlement Agency to upgrade the physical environment and relocate those that 
are occupying environmentally sensitive areas. Training programmes for vegetable farmers in the proper use 
of agricultural chemicals are organised by the Ministry of Agriculture, Land and Marine Resources. Finally, the 
major oil companies all implement health, safety and environmental programmes to protect employees and the 
natural environment. 
Coasts and Oceans 
Trinidad and Tobago is an archipelagic state and has claimed a marine area of 75,000 square 
kilometres and a 370.4 kilometre (200 nautical mile) Exclusive Economic Zone. The country's marine 
environment is influenced by its proximity to South America since it sits on the shoulder of that continent and 
shares its continental shelf. The country is also situated at the mouth of the Orinoco River. Trinidad and 
Tobago’s 704 kilometres of coast support wetlands comprising some 25,000 hectares of swamps and 
marshes, along with ports, piers and other coastal protection structures, while its marine areas contain rather 
extensive areas of seagrass beds, algal growth, coral reefs and oil and gas deposits. The country's waters 
are nutrient rich, support a large fish population and are sufficiently shallow to accommodate easy exploration 
and mining of hydrocarbons.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 23
text:
xvii 
 
 
The main issues identified in respect of the marine environment are hydrocarbon mining, marine 
transportation, fisheries resources exploitation, coastal erosion and coastal development. 
Exploitation of the nation’s coastal and marine resources has been long and continuous and has 
placed increasing pressure on the resource. These include construction and operation of both coastal and 
marine facilities for winning oil and gas as well as for high income residential and commercial uses; 
dredging and reclamation works in the near shore and dumping of dredged spoils off shore; over fishing 
and destructive fishing practices; and discharge of oil, ballast water and waste from marine vessels. 
Changes in the condition of the resource as a consequence of human actions include an increase in 
marine and land-based pollution and modification of the shape of the coastline, particularly on Trinidad’s west 
coast and Tobago’s north-west coast. Other changes include destruction of fish habitats in the Gulf of Paria 
and elsewhere; resource depletion including declining fish populations and loss of beaches particularly along 
the south and west coasts of Trinidad; destruction of seagrass beds and coral reefs; and localised changes in 
inshore water quality and currents. 
Responses to human activity or resource changes include enactment of local legislation, accession to 
international conventions and protocols and institutional capacity building to monitor and regulate use of the 
resource. Local legislation such as the Shipping (Marine Pollution) Bill, No. 2, 2004 (this bill lapsed in 
September, 2005), the Marine Areas (Preservation and Enhancement) Act Chapter 37:02, (Act No. 1 of 
1970, amended in 1996) and the Territorial Sea Act, Chapter 1:51 (Act No. 38 of 1969, amended in 1986) 
were enacted to extend control over the local marine environment and regulate and control its use. The 
Water Pollution Rules, once implemented, are expected to control effluent discharge into the environment. 
The United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, the Convention for the Protection and 
Development of the Marine Environment of the Wider Caribbean Region, the Protocol Concerning Co-
operation and Development in Combating Oil Spills in the Wider Caribbean Region, the Protocol 
Concerning Pollution from Land-based Sources and Activities and the International Convention for the 
Prevention of Pollution from Ships are the marine-based international conventions and protocols to which 
the nation has acceded. 
Institutional capacity building includes preparation of a Draft Port Plan intended to modernise the port 
and port activities, along with on-going programmes of support such as subsidies to the fishing industry; 
construction and maintenance of fisheries infrastructure in several locations by the Municipal Corporations; 
and construction of coastal defence structures by the Ministry of Works and Transport, private developers 
and property owners. 
Inland Waters 
The resource, inland waters, consists of drainages and impoundment systems, both natural and 
artificial. Natural systems include rivers, wetlands and other naturally occurring standing water bodies and 
artesian well systems, while artificial systems comprise man-made ponds and lakes, reservoirs, and irrigation 
and drainage canals. The islands’ topography, principally the Main Ridge in Tobago and the Northern, 
Southern and Central Ranges in Trinidad, determines the location and characteristics of the drainage and 
water impoundment systems. The highlands provide the source of the river systems while the lowlands or 
basins offer areas of water impoundment. 
Many of the nation’s natural wetlands are located along the coasts of both islands, and its river 
systems run from the highlands to the Gulf of Paria, Caribbean Sea or Atlantic Ocean. While most of the 
natural water systems have been identified and catalogued, the characteristics and locations of many of the 
reservoirs, weirs and other manmade water impoundment structures remain largely unknown to the public. 
Surface water sources provide 75 % of the nation’s water supply while the remainder comes from ground 
water sources. The aquifers are recharged by direct rainfall infiltration, streambed infiltration and subsurface

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 24
text:
xviii 
 
 
flows. 
The resource is utilised by many living things. It supports human activities by providing a source of 
potable water. It also offers ecosystems which encourage the development of biodiversity, including a 
refuge for migratory species of birds, and ensures appropriate sites for the impoundment of surface water 
run-off. The three issues dealt with in respect of inland waters are surface water availability and quantity, 
ground water availability and quality, and aquatic ecosystems-wetland health. 
Human action has generated pressure on the resource. Surface and ground water resources and 
wetlands receive a range of pollutants from agriculture (agro-chemicals), industry (chemicals including 
some of the heavy metals), faecal coliform (raw sewage from malfunctioning treatment plants) and solid 
and hazardous waste from urban areas and particularly residential commercial activities. Wetlands are 
impacted by all of the above, but are further stressed by land reclamation and physical development 
especially port, industrial activities and resort/residential development. 
As a consequence of the pressure, there are some changes in the resource. Abstraction and 
diversion have resulted in reduced volume of water in our rivers, while increased sedimentation has reduced 
their widths and depths. Other changes include a decrease in the quality of both surface and ground water; 
increasing cost of water production; and reduction in size and quality of water recharge areas. The country 
has sustained more than 50 % loss of wetlands over the years and wetland loss continues as corporate and 
individual developers transform the wetland resources into physical development. 
Responses have been at both the national and international levels. At the national level the Water 
Pollution Rules will make continued practices of pollution much more difficult and costly for the polluters. The 
National Reforestation and Watershed Rehabilitation Programme which is intended to replant some 33,000 
hectares of denuded land over a 10-year period is being implemented, and a National Wetlands Committee 
prepared a National Wetlands Policy which was accepted by the Government. At the international level, the 
Government acceded to the Convention for Wetlands of International Importance. 
Biodiversity 
Trinidad and Tobago has a rich variety of biological resources, given its geological relationship to 
the South American mainland. Although the review of taxa in Trinidad and Tobago is far from complete, at 
latest count the fauna for the archipelago exceeds 1,000 species and the flora, 2,500 species. A number of 
challenges face Trinidad and Tobago in its task to maintain species biodiversity and to conserve the 
country’s wildlife habitats. The chapter on Biodiversity looks at the issues of habitat conservation and 
protection, species protection, and biodiversity and climate change. 
The major pressures on habitat conservation and protection are loss, fragmentation and 
degradation - due mainly to deforestation, expansion of human settlement and continued urbanisation, 
forest degradation, forest fires, pollution, and the physical alteration of habitat - all of which ultimately 
affect species’ numbers in terrestrial, riverine and marine environments. The chapter looks at forest 
degradation and loss, and decreasing water quantity and quality as major pressures acting on the 
environment. In an effort to conserve and protect the country’s biodiversity, there are some 36 forest 
reserves, 13 wildlife sanctuaries, 1 national park, 3 beaches where entry is restricted seasonally (i.e., 
during the sea turtle nesting season) and 1 marine protected area. However the proportion of 
ecosystems represented in the various protected areas is not known. The reforestation of degraded 
forests is being undertaken as part of the National Reforestation and Watershed Rehabilitation 
Programme (NRWRP). Between 2003 and 2005, 1,825 hectares of forest lands have been replanted 
and maintained and an additional 14,042 hectares of forest protected.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 25
text:
xix 
 
 
The section on species protection considers the vulnerability of local flora and fauna to the 
conditions in Trinidad and Tobago and reports on the over-exploitation of certain species, including some 
timber species, tree ferns, orchids, freshwater fish species, prawns, marine demersal fish species, sea 
turtles, cage bird species, and game animals, such as deer, lappe, quenk, ducks and other waterfowl. 
Responses to these pressures include the declaration of the 13 wildlife sanctuaries, which prohibits 
hunting in these ‘sacred’ areas; the designation of Environmentally Sensitive Species (ESS); the 
development and implementation of recovery plans for ESS; and initiatives undertaken by State agencies, 
non-governmental organisations and community-based organisations in Northeast and Southeast Trinidad 
and in Tobago in managing the country’s biodiversity. 
The issue of biodiversity and climate change is a critical one given recent predictions of rising 
ambient air and water body temperatures, changing rainfall patterns, and sea level rise. The chapter 
summarises climate change predictions and observations in Trinidad and Tobago and proposes how 
such changes are likely to affect terrestrial, coastal and marine ecosystems and species. There is need 
for research and for monitoring the impact of climate change on the country’s ecosystems and biological 
resources. 
Economy, Industry, Resource Use 
The natural resources of Trinidad and Tobago have been and continue to be the foundation for 
its socio-economic development. The exploitation of oil and natural gas resources, and moreso natural 
gas, drives the economy. Coastal and marine resources are important for both the fishing and tourism 
industries, particularly to the economy of Tobago where both are the mainstay. 
Urban consumption of natural resources is also considerable. The official government target of 10,000 
additional housing units annually has consequences for increased demand for land space, the densification of 
existing neighbourhoods, and the expansion into green areas. Domestic users account for the largest 
freshwater withdrawals, and total electricity use is also high. The chapter focuses on three issues, namely 
energy use, water use, and road travel and transportation. 
The energy sector accounted for 40.5 % of total GDP in 2004 and 41.2 % in 2005 with an overall 
projected growth of 20.6 % in the sector. However, as the country’s economic future remains entrenched in the 
energy sector and the number of proposed energy-based industries and electricity generation increase, the 
pressure on the oil and natural gas reserves can be tremendous. In 2004, oil and gas reserves stood at 756 
million proven barrels of oil and 18.8 trillion cubic feet of proven gas reserves, respectively. Already Trinidad and 
Tobago is among the top ten per capita producers of carbon dioxide emissions in the world, although in terms of 
absolute carbon emissions, the country produces less than 1 % of the global total. 
There has been a recent call by the South Chamber of Industry and Commerce and the Caribbean 
Association of Industry and Commerce for the development of a Common Caribbean Energy Policy which 
focuses on mitigating the negative environmental impacts of the energy industry and on encouraging 
energy conservation. 
The availability of freshwater in Trinidad and Tobago is affected by the occurrence and distribution of 
rainfall as well as the occurrence, movement and quality of surface water and groundwater. As a water-rich 
country, Trinidad produces some 4.005 billion cubic metres/year, while Tobago produces about 0.206 billion 
cubic metres/year. The actual abstractions of surface and groundwater for Trinidad and Tobago in 2000 were 
278 million cubic metres/year and 81.1 million cubic metres/year respectively. The main pressures on water 
use reported in this chapter are the high demand for water for domestic and industrial uses, water wastage 
and pollution. One response to the water shortage problem is the installation and use of high-volume water 
storage tanks by householders and businesses. In addition, the Water and Sewerage Authority improved and 
upgraded the water infrastructure and reduced unaccounted for water in many areas to increase the water 
supply to the population.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 26
text:
xx 
 
 
Trinidad and Tobago is a very mobile society with a significant proportion of the population 
traveling outside of their communities to key urban centres to access jobs, schools and government 
services. Most travel within the islands involves private cars and taxis (cars, small and large maxi-taxis), 
while movement between Trinidad and Tobago is achieved via air and sea travel. The number of vehicles 
on the nation’s roads has increased dramatically. Between 1960 and 2000 the level of motorisation 
increased almost four-fold from 63 registered vehicles per 1,000 persons in 1960, to 250 vehicles per 
1000 persons in 2000. By 2005, the number of vehicles per 1,000 persons had increased further to 312. 
The current rates of vehicle ownership and traffic growth have placed great demands on the national 
transportation infrastructure resulting in gridlock in many of the country’s main roadways and access roads. 
In response the State continues to expand and upgrade the road infrastructure. A comprehensive National 
Transportation Plan was commissioned in 2004 which attempts to address the long term sea, air and land 
transportation needs of the country. There are also plans for a light rail mass transit system. 
CONCLUSION 
Reporting on the state of the environment arose out of the recognition internationally that such 
systems are required as part of the sustainable system of managing and conserving our natural resources 
which are increasingly under threat. This 2005 report represents a new system for reporting on the state of 
the Trinidad and Tobago environment. It provides a more comprehensive outlook of the condition and trends 
of our natural resources, the pressures that affect these resources and society’s responses to our 
environmental challenges. The report provides information in a form that we hope makes it valuable to 
decision makers, policy makers, planners and the general public alike.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 27
text: xxi

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 28
text:
1 
 
 
Chapter 1: New Approach to Reporting on the State of the 
Environment 
 
1.1 Purpose of Reporting 
In the face of continued degradation of the natural environment, the Government of the Republic of 
Trinidad and Tobago (GORTT) long ago recognised the need for a single agency to take charge of 
management of the environment. Its job was to deal with the multiplicity of disparate pieces of legislation 
relating to different aspects of the environment and administered by a plethora of government agencies 
engaged in its management and protection. However, it was not until 1995 that the Government enacted 
the Environmental Management Act (Act 3 of 1995) which provided a framework for engaging natural 
resource exploitation through environmentally sensitive approaches and created the legal, financial, and 
institutional mechanisms to guide and oversee the development of the environment. Five years later, this 
Act was repealed and replaced by the Environmental Management Act (Act 3 of 2000) (the EM Act). 
Of the many vehicles identified in the EM Act to effect policy direction, coordination, 
environmental controls, information dissemination, reporting and compliance, one was the preparation 
and submission of the Annual Report. 
The Assessment of the State of the Environment (ASOE) serves several purposes. First, as 
previously mentioned, it is a requirement of the EM Act (Section 14[1]), and the Environmental 
Management Authority (EMA) is therefore mandated to produce an annual report each year, a segment of 
which is devoted to an assessment of the state of the Trinidad and Tobago’s environment. Secondly, it 
provides an opportunity to measure environmental performance and critically examine the pressures 
being placed, through actions or lack thereof, on various components of the environment. 
The ASOE therefore offers a focal point for the systematic acquisition and application of scientific 
knowledge to the national development process and, through public education, informs the public of the 
state of the environment and provides guidance to all stakeholders in the pursuit of sustainable 
development. Finally, it meets the requirements of the GORTT’s international obligations to agreements 
such as the United Nations Convention on Biological Diversity (UNCBD) which emerged from the 1992 
United Nations Conference on Environment and Development (the Earth Summit). 
1.2 Past Approach to Reporting 
With two exceptions, past reports on the state of the environment dealt primarily with a single 
thematic environmental issue. The EMA’s 1996 State of the Environment Report assessed the nation’s 
Biodiversity (EMA 1998a), and this was followed by Freshwater (EMA 1998b), Air Pollution (EMA 2001a), 
and Noise Pollution (EMA 2004). For the years 2001 and 2002, a biennial report (2001/2002) was 
prepared focusing on an Environmental Vulnerability Index (EVI) developed for Trinidad and Tobago 
(Agard and Gowrie 2003). 
The 2004 State of the Environment Report (Northern Range Assessment 2005) was the most 
marked departure from the traditional reporting approach. Drawing on an assessment of the Northern 
Range which was conducted as part of a global process known as the Millennium Ecosystem 
Assessment (MA), this report focused on the Northern Range as an integrated ecosystem, thereby 
allowing for an examination of the cross-sectoral approach that is required for effective management. The 
process of preparing this report also focused on participation of all relevant stakeholders and users. 
The reporting processes to date have allowed the EMA to test different approaches for producing 
the ASOE, and this has lead to a decision to streamline the methodology so that the ASOE can now

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 29
text:
2 
 
 
better address the multi-dimensionality of the links between environment and development, thus providing 
a more informed basis for decision-making. 
 
 
1.3 New Approach to Reporting 
The 2005 ASOE seeks to provide a benchmark for continued, systematic reporting on 
environmental and developmental issues. Several steps have been taken in this report to lay the 
groundwork for future reports, and these are as follows: 
• 
A framework for reporting on the state of the environment, known as the Pressure-State-Response 
(PSR) Model, is introduced. This model was developed by the Organisation for Economic Co-
operation and Development (OECD) in 1993, and is currently utilised by a large number of 
countries for international and national reporting on the environment (OECD 2003). Section 1.4 
describes the PSR Model in more detail. The section also explains how the PSR model will be 
used in conjunction with other frameworks (such as the conceptual MA framework) in order to 
capture the interconnection between humans and the environment. 
• 
On the basis of the PSR Model, and through stakeholder consultations, the 2005 ASOE began the 
development of a national set of environmental indicators for Trinidad and Tobago. Indicators are tools 
that can be used to obtain information on issues of relevance to human development, thereby allowing 
for analyses of societal change (Segnestam 2002). In the case of assessing the state of environment, 
indicators can be used to collect useful information on environmental health and quality and provide 
better insights on: conditions and trends; impacts on any given component of the environment; the 
pressures acting on the environment; and outcomes of human responses to environmental change. 
The full list of core indicators developed is included in Annex 1. 
• 
It has been recognized however that Trinidad and Tobago will have great difficulty obtaining data in 
the short to medium term to utilise the full set of core indicators proposed by the OECD. A realistic 
approach would be for the EMA, in collaboration with stakeholders, to identify and select locally 
relevant and achievable indicators from the full OECD set. In this manner, a comprehensive 
subset of indicators capable of immediate use for ongoing monitoring and evaluation of Trinidad 
and Tobago’s environmental performance can be derived. Trinidad and Tobago can then develop 
and implement the necessary data acquisition systems to allow eventual expansion to the full set of 
core OECD indicators or theoretically define indicators for our unique situation. It should also be 
noted that national indicators are important for allowing comparisons across national boundaries, so 
that Trinidad and Tobago can keep abreast of its obligations to international environmental 
conventions (for example, UNCBD, United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification [UNCCD], 
and United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change [UNFCCC]). 
• 
The process for preparation of the 2005 ASOE attempted to bring together a group of relevant 
public sector agencies and research institutions and organisations in order to make the crucial 
links amongst issues and between sectors related to environment and development. Having 
King’s Wharf, San Fernando continues to be a 
location where seabirds are observed

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 30
text:
3 
 
 
established such a network, the EMA will continue to promote this type of multi- stakeholder 
involvement in the preparation of the ASOE so as to increase saliency and legitimacy.

character to the scenic Manzanilla/Mayaro Road 
The goat race, an annual event held in Buccoo,

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 32
text:
5 
 
 
While the PSR Model makes a positive link between the environment and humans through 
resource use, what this model does not do is elaborate on the ways in which various aspects of our well-
being are enhanced or affected by different environmental services. 
In the 2004 State of the Environment Report which focused on the Northern Range, the MA 
conceptual framework was used. Although principally designed for the assessment of the state of 
ecosystems through an examination of ecosystem services, this conceptual framework introduced for the first 
time a breakdown of the constituents of human well-being and made the linkages between these, ecosystem 
services, and the drivers of environmental change (human and natural, both directly and indirectly affecting 
ecosystem change). 
Since its introduction, the MA conceptual framework has been found to be extremely useful in 
environmental reporting, and is gaining credibility for reporting not only from an ecosystem standpoint. A 
more in-depth explanation of this framework is provided in the 2004 State of the Environment Report and 
can also be accessed at www.maweb.org. 
Although the 2005 ASOE focuses on the PSR Model, future reports will combine the information in 
the PSR Model and MA conceptual framework to more effectively make the links to human well-being. In 
addition, elements of the United Nations Environment Programme’s (UNEP) Driving Force-Pressure-
State-Impact-Response (DPSIR) model will also be incorporated (UNEP/Grid-Arendal 2008). 
1.5 Report Preparation 
A Steering Committee was established to provide technical guidance and input into the preparation 
of the 2005 ASOE. This Committee was convened at the beginning of the process, and before finalisation 
of the Report, members were required to review the report for scientific accuracy and rigour. 
The Committee was comprised of members drawn from several government agencies such as: the 
Department of Natural Resources and the Environment (DNRE) of the Tobago House of Assembly (THA); the 
Ministry of Public Utilities and the Environment (MPUE); the Institute of Marine Affairs (IMA); the EMA; the 
Water and Sewerage Authority (WASA)/Water Resources Agency (WRA); and the Ministry of Trade and 
Industry (MTI). One non-government organisation (NGO), the Cropper Foundation, was also involved. 
Data and information used to compile this report were collected from a range of sources, including 
published and unpublished documents, and through interviews with experts and national stakeholders. 
Annex 2 provides a breakdown of sources of information from various agencies and ministries. 
1.6 Reporting Themes 
Six thematic areas have been selected in reporting on the Trinidad and Tobago environment: 
Atmosphere; Land; Coasts and Oceans; Inland Waters; Biodiversity; and Economy, Industry and 
Resource Use. 
These themes are defined as follows: 
Atmosphere 
The sum total of all gases surrounding the earth, extending several hundred km above the surface in 
a mechanical mixture of various gases in fluid-like motion (EMA 2001a). The atmosphere is artificially 
divided into layers with the troposphere (closest to the earth) and the stratosphere (found above the 
troposphere) being the areas of greatest environmental influence. 
Land 
The terrestrial bio-productive system that comprises soil, vegetation, other biota, and the ecological 
and hydrological processes that operate within the system (MPUE 2006).

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 33
text:
6 
 
 
Coasts and Oceans 
The coast is the part of the land adjoining or near the ocean/sea. It is often described as the 
land-water interface. Oceans are defined as the areas of sea or seabed which are under the jurisdiction 
of Trinidad and Tobago and include its exclusive economic zone (EEZ). 
Inland Waters 
Surface and subterranean waters (rivers, streams, lakes, dams, aquifers) and their ecosystems. 
Biodiversity 
The number and variety of different organisms in the ecological complexes in which they naturally 
occur. Organisms are arranged at many levels, ranging from complete ecosystems to the biochemical 
structures that are the molecular basis of heredity. Thus, the term encompasses different ecosystems, 
species and genes that must be present for a healthy environment. 
Economy, Industry, Resource Use 
This Chapter discusses socio-economic trends and activities as they relate to natural resource 
uses. 
1.7 Format of the 2005 ASOE 
The 2005 ASOE comprises eight chapters. This chapter – Chapter 1 - is an introductory chapter 
which provides the rationale for the approach used in this report. 
Chapter 2 provides an overview of some of the underlying causes or driving forces for 
environmental change in Trinidad and Tobago. This is done in an attempt to unearth some of the societal 
conditions driving human actions and their consequential impact on the environment. 
Chapters 3 through to 8 report on the various thematic areas. Each component identifies a number 
of issues which are explored in some depth through appropriate indicators to arrive at the state of the 
resource, the pressures placed on it, and the societal response to its condition.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 34
text:
7 
 
 
Chapter 2: Introduction to the Main Drivers of Change 
Affecting Trinidad and Tobago 
 
2.1 Introduction 
The Central Statistical Office’s 2005 mid-year estimates puts the population of Trinidad and 
Tobago at over 1.29 million (CSO 2005a), Trinidad and Tobago is a mix of ethnicities and cultures, 
predominantly of African and Indian ancestry. As the southernmost islands in the Caribbean archipelago, 
the country consists of 2 larger islands with a total land area of 5,128 km2, and 21 smaller islands and 
islets, with the more notable 5 islands off Trinidad’s north-western coast and the 3 key islands off 
Tobago’s south-central coast. Its economic jurisdiction goes beyond its terrestrial boundaries with a 
claimed EEZ in 2003 extending to some 60,659 km2. Its uniqueness from the rest of the Caribbean 
Islands is reflected in its mineralogy and biodiversity which mirrors those of the neighbouring South 
American mainland. 
 
 
 
In the development of Trinidad and Tobago, the livelihoods of its people and their cultures are 
based primarily on the utilisation of its natural resources.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 35
text:
8 
 
 
Despite its limited land space, Trinidad and Tobago is endowed with a wide natural resource base 
compared to other small island developing states (SIDS) of similar size elsewhere in the tropics. Its 
minerals, soils, waters, flora, fauna, ecosystems and biodiversity are all utilised for the socio-economic 
development of the country. This combination of natural resources is all captured in diverse landscapes 
and waterscapes, which add to Trinidad and Tobago’s value as an emerging tourist destination. With a 
combined oil and natural gas reserve of 9.2 billion barrels of oil equivalent in 2000 (Williams 2003), the 
energy and petrochemical industries play a dominant role in the development of the national economy. 
 
 
 
Without a doubt, the country’s progress and its natural resources are closely inter-related and inter-
dependent. With the strength of the economy closely tied to its natural resource base, monitoring and 
reporting on the health of the environment and the individual natural resources is a key strategy for 
sustainable development. 
2.2 Main Drivers Influencing Environmental Change 
The arrival of the Europeans to the shores of Trinidad and Tobago in 1498 was the beginning of significant 
changes in the country’s environment. As a settled colony, its mixture of mountains, valleys, plains and fertile 
soils made the islands an ideal location for production of export crops. Today, the importance of agricultural 
production to the economy of Trinidad and Tobago has declined considerably and so the sector is no longer the 
prime economic driver. Instead the economy is now centred on the exploration of the country’s oil and natural gas 
resources. Over the years due mainly to increasing and inappropriate human activities, the country’s ecosystems 
and natural resources have been altered considerably, resulting in a number of negative impacts, some of which 
are irreversible. 
The international community has long recognised the relationship between environmental change 
and socio-economic activities and the need to understand the factors, both direct and indirect, that can lead 
to such change. Knowledge of these factors is important in order to better grasp the state of Trinidad and 
Tobago’s environment, and to provide appropriate interventions geared at mitigating against environmental 
problems, reducing negative impacts and maintaining the environmental health of our natural resources for 
Map of Trinidad and Tobago 
Source: 
www.hydrocarbons.technology.com/p
rojects/pointfortin/images/image5

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 36
text:
9 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
present and future use. 
Such factors or driving forces, which can be numerous and their interactions complex, are the 
consequences of our past and present choices as a society. They result from our patterns of behaviour over 
time, some of which are steeped in our cultural norms and practices. The MA (MA 2005) provides a list of 
factors or drivers, some of which are direct in nature (physical, biological and chemical), and those that are 
indirect (demographic, economic, socio-political, scientific and technological, and cultural and religious). 
This report focuses mainly on the direct pressures or factors to environmental change. However, it 
is also important to examine some of the indirect or underlying factors leading to environmental change. 
This is done in the following sections of this chapter.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 37
text:
10 
 
 
Experience has shown that there are several underlying factors in Trinidad and Tobago which lead to 
negative environmental change. Among the major ones identified in the MPUE's State of Land Resources 
of Trinidad and Tobago (MPUE 2005), are: 
• 
Population pressures; 
• 
Rising affluence; 
• 
Poverty; 
• 
Approach to development; 
• 
Incompatible land and marine use; 
• 
Insufficient institutional capacity; 
• 
Inadequate legislation; 
• 
Insufficient monitoring and enforcement of existing laws; 
• 
Inadequate stakeholder collaboration (among State agencies and the private and NGO sectors); 
and 
• 
Negative elements of popular culture. 
2.2.1 
Population Pressures 
Urbanisation and rural to urban drift have resulted in population concentrations in the built up areas in 
the East-West and North-South corridors of Trinidad and in southwest Tobago. Such population pressures 
are leading to increased densification of traditional urban neighbourhoods (for example, St James, 
Woodbrook, Maraval, Diego Martin, Tunapuna, Maracas and St Augustine), where there is an increasing 
trend to replace single family units with apartment buildings and townhouse complexes. Chaguanas is the 
fastest growing urban centre in the country, with an increasing number of new satellite communities 
emerging in the region (CSO 2000). This densification of traditional neighbourhoods and the expansion of 
settlements into new areas have not always occurred with a corresponding expansion and upgrading of 
infrastructure and amenities. The resulting situation is inadequate drainage systems, sewerage systems, 
and local road networks in commercial and built up areas. 
Urban centres such as Port of Spain, San Fernando, Chaguanas and Arima support a large transient 
population, but nowhere is this population greater than Port of Spain where during weekdays its transient 
population is at least five times the size of the city’s resident population of about 50,000 (CSO 2000). This 
high transient population places a burden on existing infrastructure and sanitation services, which leads to 
environmental degradation. 
2.2.2 Rising Affluence 
For many societies, decoupling the link between affluence and consumption remains the key 
challenge as increased consumption is often linked to increased waste generation and other 
environmental problems. For Trinidad and Tobago, economic growth has created increased demand for 
the country’s resources, rising levels of expectation and increased consumption of goods and services. 
This is particularly evident in the expansion of the vehicle population on the nation’s roads. Figures 
indicate that during the period 1995 to 2005, the number of registered vehicles in the country had almost 
doubled to just over 400,000 cars (CSO 2007). This has implications for carbon dioxide emissions and 
declining air quality. It is reported that global carbon dioxide emissions from motor vehicles (of which 
emissions from cars make up 74%), increased during 1990 to 1997 by 26% and at a rate four times 
greater than the growth of carbon dioxide emissions overall (Myers and Kent 2003). Research on the

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 38
text:
11 
 
 
relationship between affluence, consumption and the environmental impacts of consumption in a local 
context needs to be undertaken in detail in order to formulate workable solutions to this challenge. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.2.3 Poverty 
While Trinidad and Tobago has made significant strides in socio-economic development, a 
considerable proportion of its population live below the poverty line. A 2004 report put the level of poverty 
in Trinidad and Tobago at 16.7% of the population, while the level of indigent poverty was at 1.2% of the 
population. The poverty line was defined as TT$665 per month, per capita (Inter-American Development 
Bank 2004a). Although not all economically poor communities engage in activities which lead to 
environmental degradation, some low income groups lack the means and resources to provide for some 
of their basic needs and are known to resort to the unsustainable exploitation of natural resources. 
In Trinidad and Tobago, unauthorised occupation of land is not restricted to the landless and poor. 
However, historical patterns of lack of access to affordable land and housing by some low income groups 
has exacerbated the problem of squatting and has generated a myriad of environmental problems 
including: poor sanitary conditions; inadequate liquid and solid waste disposal; and deficient drainage 
systems and road networks. For example, the IADB report showed that about 62% of the lowest quintile 
(the poor) was dependent on pit latrines while appropriately 72% of the indigent was dependent on pit 
latrines (Ibid.). 
2.2.4 Approach to Development 
Over the last decade or so Trinidad and Tobago has pursued a path of industrialisation focused 
on gas-based heavy industry located for the most part on the western coast of Trinidad. The first 
industrial complex at Point Lisas is currently being used as the blueprint for further industrial 
development and the Government is in the lead, as with Point Lisas, in acquiring mostly foreign-based 
partners to locate their plants in Trinidad. 
With their large demand for land, water and electrical energy, some of these new industries 
have the potential to negatively impact the natural environment, unless carefully planned and 
monitored. Already the concerns for relocation of residents, displacement of fisher-folk and decline in 
coastal water quality are being expressed in many quarters. Thus, the competition for scarce space in 
a SIDS such as Trinidad and Tobago, coupled with small margins for environmental errors, will require 
monitoring programmes that offer very early warnings to vigilant environmental groups and authorities 
to avert disaster. 
Aerial view of the built up sections of 
greater Arima

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 39
text:
12 
 
 
2.2.5 Incompatible Land and Marine Uses 
The intrinsic characteristics of renewable natural resources are those properties which allow 
them to regenerate. Where the use to which the resource is put disrupts or destroys its intrinsic 
 
 
 
 
 
 
qualities, degradation occurs. Incompatible land and marine uses are not sustainable because the 
resources associated with these ecosystems are unable to regenerate themselves. The examples 
of inappropriate uses in Trinidad and Tobago are many, ranging from: inappropriate land 
clearance methods which leave landscapes denuded; the construction of large houses and 
conducting agricultural production on steep hillsides without the use of suitable soil conservation 
practices and guidelines; the proliferation of illegal waste dump sites around the country; and 
squatting in forested areas. 
2.2.6 Inadequate Institutional Capacity 
Experience has shown that where there is insufficient or limited institutional capacity to safeguard 
against unsustainable practices and behaviours, environmental degradation follows. Environmental 
improvement requires an alignment of legislation with the country’s principles of environmental 
sustainability, as well as the effective implementation and strengthening of capacity of environmental 
agencies, including rationalisation and improving inter-institutional collaboration. The natural resources 
and environment of Trinidad and Tobago are governed by some 40 pieces of legislation and managed by 
about 28 State agencies, with the EMA playing a regulatory and coordinating role. However, this 
arrangement has had limited success in meeting the environmental degradation challenge. Instead the 
sector is currently characterised by deficiencies in its institutional structure and capacity. 
2.2.7 Poor Legislation 
The absence or inappropriateness of legislation limits punitive measures to preventive action, or 
cessation of those activities that can lead to environmental degradation. 
While it is recognised that regulations alone cannot protect all aspects of the environment, there is 
need for legal regulatory efficacy. Some legislative frameworks for various aspects of environmental 
management are ineffective and the penalties for non-compliance are not stringent enough to act as 
deterrents.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 40
text:
13 
 
 
2.2.8 Inadequate Monitoring and Enforcement of Existing Laws 
Environmental monitoring and the establishment of sound environmental information management 
systems are critical to the national sustainable development of any country. Environmental monitoring 
affords humans the opportunity to: better understand the interactions among the terrestrial, marine and 
climate components of the Earth’s environment; assess the impact of changes on the provision of 
ecosystem services and processes; and highlight the consequences of development policies on the 
environment. 
 
 
There are various types of monitoring activities in place today (baseline monitoring, impact 
monitoring and compliance monitoring), which together can provide sufficient and useful data and 
information to inform decision making processes. While there may be the institutional commitment by 
many state agencies responsible for monitoring various aspects of the environment, oftentimes there are 
insufficient resources available to ensure continuity and to maintain the integrity of these monitoring 
programmes, including the availability of technical expertise, and adequate information management 
systems. 
The absence of baseline data and the lack of appropriate ecological monitoring are serious 
shortcomings. Another shortcoming is the lack of impact monitoring during the construction and operation 
of large development projects particularly since the country is experiencing a proliferation of construction 
and infrastructural projects. 
For some, the idea of enforcement of legislation is the only way to elicit compliance. However, most 
agencies lack adequate capacity to enforce existing laws or to monitor mitigation measures, while court 
cases can be long, drawn out processes in which the plaintiff (the State) seldom ever wins. 
 
 
Known as fragile wonders, the coral reefs of 
Tobago must be protected

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 41
text:
14 
 
 
2.2.9 Negative Elements of Popular Culture 
Popular culture tends to encourage conspicuous consumption and ‘throw-away’ attitudes which 
generate pollution and insensitivity to the natural environment. Elements of popular culture enjoin society 
to get the new and larger things and the most advanced systems in order to make life easier. As a result 
the population’s lifestyles support the continuous upgrade of material assets often to the detriment of the 
traditional values and norms. Whilst modern day living drives society and people to consume more and 
more, this is done often without regard for the consequences to human health, the natural environment 
and to the needs of future generations.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 42
text:
15 
 
 
Chapter 3: Atmosphere 
 
3.1 Overview 
The Earth is surrounded by a blanket of gases (the atmosphere), which starts at ground level and 
stretches some 800 km above the planet, becoming thinner until it gradually reaches space. Essentially, the 
atmosphere is comprised of several layers which are differentiated by their thermal characteristics, chemical 
composition, and density (Figure 3.1). Life on Earth is supported by the atmosphere, solar energy, and the 
planet’s magnetic fields. The atmosphere absorbs the energy from the sun; recycles carbon, nitrogen, water 
and other chemicals; and works with the electrical and magnetic forces to provide a moderate climate. The 
atmosphere also protects humans, animals, and plants from high-energy radiation; the frigid vacuum of 
space; and from drifting rocks, chunks of metal and other bits and pieces of space debris that occasionally 
collide with Earth. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure 3.1: Layers of Earth’s Atmosphere 
Source: NOAA 2006

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 43
text:
16 
 
 
 
The Earth’s atmosphere consists of a complex system of gases and airborne solid and liquid 
particles. According to the National Atmospheric and Oceanographic Administration (NOAA), about 99.96 
% of dry atmosphere consists of three gases, namely nitrogen (8.08 %), oxygen (20.95 %) and argon 
(0.93 %). The remaining proportion (0.04 %) consists of carbon dioxide (0.035 %) and traces of other 
gases (NOAA 2006). Finally, water vapour usually exists in varying amounts (up to 7 %). Table 3.1 and 
Figure 3.2 show the composition of the earth’s atmosphere. 
Table 3.1: Constituents and Composition of Earth’s Atmosphere 
(Source: Environmental Protection Authority & Queensland Parks & Wildlife 2003) 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The troposphere, the most dense and closest layer to the Earth’s surface, is the layer where 
people, plants and animals live, and where emissions from anthropogenic and natural sources are first 
discharged. It is also the region where almost all weather occurs. 
The next layer is the stratosphere which contains ozone which blocks large amounts of solar 
ultraviolet (UV) radiation from entering the troposphere and thus protects Earth and its inhabitants from its 
harmful effects. The other outer layers include the mesosphere, the thermosphere, and the exosphere. 
 
The atmosphere, particularly the troposphere and stratosphere, plays a central role in maintaining 
environmental conditions on Earth and sustaining life as we know it. However, within recent times it has 
become clear that humans are significantly influencing the chemical composition of the troposphere and 
stratosphere in ways in which can affect the dynamic workings of the atmosphere and negatively impact 
humankind. In recent times, global issues such as climate variability and change, enhanced greenhouse 
effect and stratospheric ozone depletion have become important to the development of Trinidad and 
Tobago, particularly in light of the fact that we can be classified as a SIDS, which makes us extremely 
vulnerable to vagaries of climate change, sea level rise and the impacts of natural disasters. In addition,

local air quality continues to be a growing concern given the increased industrialisation of the country and 
ever-increasing number of vehicles on the nation’s roads. 
 
Figure 3.2: Composition of Dry Atmosphere 
 
(Source: http://rpmedia.ask.com/ts?u=/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Atmosphere3.jpg/400px-Atmosphere3.jpg) 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Climate variability, enhanced greenhouse effect/global warming and stratospheric ozone are global 
issues of relevance to Trinidad and Tobago which are discussed in this chapter. There are now very few 
sceptics to the ‘reality of global climate change’ and there is strong evidence that human activities are the 
reason why this is happening. Changes in rainfall, temperature and sea level rise are the main concerns for 
Trinidad and Tobago. Collected data suggest that air temperature has been on the increase at least for the 
last 31 years. Total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions are small in the context of global outputs, but per 
An aerial view of the Northern Range 
Sunset at Crown Point, Tobago

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 45
text:
18 
 
 
capita emissions place Trinidad and Tobago among the highest contributors. The country’s per capita rating 
is expected to climb with the expansion of its industrialisation process and the ever-increasing vehicle 
population. The concerted international response to stratospheric ozone depletion has controlled the 
discharge of ozone-depleting substances (ODS), and as a result the ‘ozone hole’ observed annually in 
Antarctica has started to shrink and is predicted to close by 2050. On the home front, Trinidad and Tobago 
has done its share to reduce the use of ODS, and more efforts are expected. 
Outdoor air quality issues, such as vehicular emissions and particulate pollutants are examined 
based on available data, and the information points to the fact that these are worrying concerns. One of 
the main sources of air pollution, which negatively affects outdoor air quality, is motor vehicles, which is 
also a key contributor to GHG emissions. Industrial activities and bushfires are major contributors to 
particulate pollution. Though there are plans for continuous measurement of air pollutant levels, 
monitoring is mostly confined to individual isolated studies. Public awareness about the health (as well as 
other) effects of air pollution and ‘air toxics’, is important in ensuring control of the problem. 
Sahara dust is increasingly becoming a threat which poses health concerns for those affected 
by asthma and other respiratory ailments. An ongoing research programme has been established at 
the St Augustine Campus of the University of the West Indies (UWI) to examine the composition and 
impacts of Sahara Dust in Trinidad. 
There are a number of emerging issues such as indoor air quality, exposure to cigarette smoke, 
etc. for which there is limited information but which may need future attention. 
3.2 Climate Variability and Change 
3.2.1 Background 
Weather refers to the daily and weekly changes in the atmosphere experienced in a given location 
as it relates to rainfall, temperature, air pressure, relative humidity, cloudiness, wind, and visibility. On the 
other hand, climate refers to the long term description of weather patterns. Historically, a 30-year period 
has been taken to represent the long-term or normal climate of a country. The current standard 30-year 
climatology as defined by the World Meteorological Organisation (WMO) to assess changes in climate is 
from 1961 to 1990. Both short term weather patterns and longer term climate can be variable. 
Climate variability can be defined as the way climatic variables, such as temperature and 
precipitation, depart from some average state, either above or below the average value. Climate change, on 
the other hand, can be defined as a trend in one or more climatic variables characterised by a fairly smooth 
continuous increase or decrease of the average value during the period of record. While there is continued 
debate about what constitutes normal climate variability as opposed to actual climate change, only a few will 
argue that current global climatic trends are not a worrying concern. There is also the incidence of extreme 
weather events that depart from the average in some significant way. Global climate change is expected to 
result in an increase in the occurrence of these extreme events.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 46
text:
19 
 
 
 
 
 
 
In dealing with climate variability and change, two climate variables of critical importance are rainfall and 
temperature. Global temperature has predictably been on the rise with an increase in the number of extreme 
temperature days observed. Figure 3.3 shows the combined land-surface, air and sea surface temperatures 
(in ºC) from 1861 to 1998, relative to the average temperature of 15.08 ºC recorded between 1961 and 
1990. 
 
Figure 3.3: Trend in Average Global Surface Temperature 
 
 (Source: School of Environmental Science, Climate Research Unit, University of East Anglia, UK 1999) 
 
 
Rainfall in northeast Trinidad

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 47
text:
20 
 
 
According to the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), the mean global surface 
temperature has increased by about 0.3 to 0.6 ºC since the late 19th century and by about 0.2 to 0.3 ºC 
over the last 40 years, which is the period with the most reliable data. Recent years have been among the 
warmest since 1860 - the date from which instrumental recording of sea surface and land-based surface 
air temperatures began. Warming is evident in both sea surface and land-based surface air temperatures 
(UNEP/Grid-Arendal 2006a). In fact, 10 of the last 11 years (spanning the period 1995 to 2005), have 
been among the warmest years since instrumental record keeping of sea surface and land-based surface 
air temperatures began (IPCC 2007). 
Rainfall patterns which deviate from the norm can result in drought and flooding. Though regional 
differences have led to increased rainfall in certain locations and decreased rainfall elsewhere (Figure 
3.4), precipitation averaged over the Earth’s land surface increased from the start of the century up to 
about 1960, but has decreased since about 1980 (UNEP/Grid-Arendal 2006a). 
 
Figure 3.4: Precipitation Changes: Trends over Land from 1900 to 1994 
 
(Source: UNEP/Grid Arendal 2006b) 
 
 
The measurement and collection of climatic data for Trinidad began in the 1940s, while such 
measurements in Tobago only started in the mid 1960s. This makes it difficult to determine with some 
degree of accuracy if our local climate changes reflect the global trends. However despite the lack of 
longer term historical data, we can no longer take our climate for granted nor can we wait decades to play 
the odds in deciding whether climate change is “real” and the ways we should respond to the issue. 
The very first notions that human activities might cause climate change on regional scales came 
from British and French island colonies. The administrations of Tobago, St Vincent, St Helena and 
Mauritius were persuaded in the 18th and early 19th centuries that deforestation was linked to declines in 
rainfall. This led to protection of the islands’ forest resources (Sear et al. 2001). Some 200 years later, 
most people would now concur that human activities are accelerating global climate change. According to 
the 2001 Report of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) (IPCC 2001a), global climate 
changes are happening and are having a “widespread and discernible impact.” This 2001 Report also

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 48
text:
21 
 
 
noted that the most widespread direct risk to human settlements is flooding and landslides driven by 
rainfall, sea-level rise, and tropical storms. 
3.2.2 The Climate of Trinidad and Tobago 
Trinidad and Tobago enjoys a humid tropical marine climate with little seasonal or diurnal variation and 
lies within the belt of the Trade Winds, which provide a fairly constant strong wind flowing from the east. The 
islands are affected mainly by the subtropical anticyclone belt and the inter-tropical convergence zone (ITCZ), 
and it is the movement and location of these two systems which give the weather its marked seasonal 
characteristics noted in Trinidad and Tobago and the rest of the Caribbean. From January to May, drier 
conditions are experienced (dry season), and from June to December, wetter conditions dominate (wet 
season). For most years, there is a short dry spell, the Petit Carême, during September and October. 
It is during the wet season from June through to the end of November that there is risk of tropical 
storms and hurricanes. However, Trinidad and Tobago lies at the southern edge of the North Atlantic 
hurricane belt and this makes the islands less prone to being hit by hurricanes when compared with most 
other Caribbean countries. 
3.2.3 Pressure 
The IPCC 2001 Report notes the “dangerous anthropogenic interference with the climate system”. 
Human activities have increased the atmospheric concentrations of GHGs and ozone depleting materials 
since the pre-industrial era. The Report notes that the atmospheric concentrations of key anthropogenic GHG 
(carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, and tropospheric ozone) reached their highest recorded levels in the 
1990s, primarily due to the combustion of fossil fuels, agriculture, deforestation, and land-use changes. The 
concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere has increased by some 31 % from 280 ppm to 368 ppm 
since the pre-industrial era, while the concentration of methane has more than doubled. Table 3.2 shows the 
changes in atmospheric concentrations which have taken place since the pre-industrial era to today.

These atmospheric changes are likely to result in predicted climate change for Trinidad and 
Tobago as well as the rest of the Caribbean. The IPCC-endorsed projections equate a likely increase of 
between 1.0 to 3.5 ºC by 2100, a sea level rise of between 15 and 95 cm and a rainfall deficit of about 15 
%. 
3.2.4 Current State and Trends 
In Trinidad, the 30-year average annual rainfall for the period 1975 to 2004 is 1,870 mm. However, 
depending on location and topography, there can be wide variation among different areas on the island. 
In the eastern section of the Northern Range rainfall can reach as high as 3,800 mm, and for the same 
period be as low as 1,200 mm both on the off-shore islands of the northwest peninsula and in the 
southwest peninsula of Trinidad. The pattern for Tobago is comparable with that of Trinidad in quantity, 
seasonality and spatial distribution where rainfall ranges between 3,800 mm in the northeast and 1,400 
mm in the southwest. 
For Trinidad, the 30-year mean annual average air temperature varies between 32.6 ºC (in the day) 
and 20.2 ºC (at nights). Average relative humidity is approximately 80 %, but ranges between 50 % in the dry 
season and 100 % in the rainy season. During the dry season, there is wide diurnal fluctuation in relative 
humidity. The Northeast Trades dominate the wind regime, showing great steadiness throughout the year. 
The average wind speed ranges between 20 to 28 km per hour, with the lowest speeds occurring in the rainy 
season1. There is considerable local variation in wind velocity, related to a range of factors including 
topography, the diurnal cycle and thunderstorm activity. 
Rainfall 
Figure 3.5 illustrates the 30-year (1975-2004) rainfall distribution pattern for Trinidad and Tobago. 
Rainfall varies greatly according to the season, with some 67 to 92 % of the total rainfall occurring during the 
rainy season. The total rainfall varies between 118.9 mm and 854.4 mm during the dry season, and 1,148.2 
mm and 1,999.0 mm during the wet season. Figure 3.6 shows that there were generally drier than usual 
conditions in (a) Trinidad and (b) Tobago during some of the recorded El Niño years (Bourne 2003).
