El Niño/La Niña-Southern Oscillation 
Besides seasonal variations, El Niño and La Niña are the next major factors affecting annual climatic 
variability. Though the occurrence of El Niño and La Niña has only been brought to the forefront as global 
phenomena within the last 30 years, geologic and archaeological evidence suggest that El Niño has been 
around for thousands of years. El Niño involves the warming of some sections of the Pacific Ocean, large 
scale variations in atmospheric pressure between the Pacific and Indian Oceans, and weakening or 
reversing of the east-west trade winds. This large-scale abnormal warming of the surface of the eastern and 
central Pacific Ocean is known to occur every 2 to 7 years, with varying degrees of intensity and duration. It 
is associated with significant changes in climate patterns all over the world, causing droughts and floods in 
parts of the Americas, Africa, Asia and the South Pacific. El Niño was recorded worldwide recently during 
1981/1982, 1987/1988, 1997/1998 and 2002/2003. Among the more recent El Niño episodes, the 
1981/1982 and 1997/1998 were the most severe and had profound effects on the world economy. 
In contrast, there is an abnormal cooling in the eastern Pacific during a La Niña event, which 
produces conditions opposite of those generated by El Niño. The effects of La Niña are most pronounced 
from December to March. 
Analysis of over 100 years of historical climate data shows that El Niño and La Niña are linked 
statistically to worldwide abnormal patterns of rainfall, temperature, and storminess. The Caribbean is no 
exception to these anomalies in climate. During El Niño years tropical cyclone activity in the Caribbean is 
drastically suppressed. During intense El Niño years, Trinidad and Tobago, like the rest of the southern 
Caribbean, experiences deficit rainfall. The reverse is true in La Niña years. However, annual rainfall totals 
may not reflect the impacts of El Niño, since the typical period for El Niño conditions starts in the middle of 
one year and ends in the middle of the following year. This abnormal dry period associated with El Niño is 
usually followed by an exceptionally wet rainy season. Figure 3.8 shows the variation from average annual 
rainfall for the last 30 years for Trinidad and Tobago. The 1997/98 El Niño, which was noted worldwide as 
the second strongest in the past century, did not reflect the significant changes in rainfall amount recorded 
at the Piarco Meteorological Station, though many remember the 1998 fire season to be an aggressive one 
(Figure 3.9). This suggests that the relationship between this phenomenon and the local weather needs to 
be studied in greater detail. 
 
Figure 3.8: Variation in Annual Rainfall (% Change) from the 30-year average (1975-05) 
 
(Source: Meteorological Services Division; pers. comm. 2006)

Flooding 
Since 1989, flooding has become a perennial problem in many communities, particularly in Trinidad 
where heavy rains cause flooding along the floodplains of major rivers, often resulting in damage to home 
and property, and even in the loss of lives, including the lives of children who are caught off guard by the 
rising, raging waters. Over the years, the incidence of flooding has increased significantly and financial claims 
have been substantial. In Trinidad and Tobago, flood damage was estimated at US$2.4 million in 2000 and 
US$8 million in 20022. 
The Meteorological Services Division collects information on the incidence of flooding in Trinidad 
and Tobago. From data collected from 1981 to 2005 it was revealed that the Caroni, Caparo and North 
Oropouche River Basins are notable with regard to the incidence of persistent flooding, with the 
occurrence of two or more flooding events per year. In recent times the most severely affected areas 
have been areas along the Caroni River, with almost twice as many flood events than the Caparo and 
North Oropouche Rivers (Figure 3.10). Moreover, the problem no longer seems to be restricted to the 
traditional October to December period. Instead flooding now extends from June to January, and gets an 
early start when there is above average rainfall during the dry season. Figure 3.11 shows the distribution 
of flood events between 1981 and January, 2005.

3.2.5 Response 
Trinidad and Tobago joined the international community in making a legal commitment to address the 
challenges posed by climate change. The country became a signatory to the UNFCCC in June 1992 and 
ratified it in June 1994. This Convention, which recognises that the global climate system is a shared 
resource, aims to stabilise GHG concentrations in the atmosphere at a level that would prevent human 
activities from influencing the climate systems. As part of this Convention, Trinidad and Tobago is required 
to undertake, periodically update, publish, and make available to the Conference of Parties (COP), its 
national inventory on GHG and its national programmes to mitigate and adapt to global climate change. 
In 2001, the EMA prepared the country’s Initial National Communication which describes the 
national GHG inventory, and provides statements on vulnerability and adaptation, as well as identification 
of technology needs, capacity building, and research (EMA 2001b). However, the report was prepared 
using 1990 data and therefore there may be a need to undertake a new GHG inventory, given the 
dynamic changes that have taken place in the country’s oil and natural gas sector within recent times. 
This new inventory would have to be supported by reliable data, and so the need to establish the relevant 
databases can be considered an urgent requirement. 
The Meteorological Services Division forms the main repository of climatic data in Trinidad and 
Tobago and provides useful data on local weather in both Trinidad and Tobago over the years. In 
addition, other agencies such as the IMA, the EMA, the WRA, the Office of Disaster Preparedness and 
 
 
Management (ODPM)/National Emergency Management Agency (NEMA), Tobago Emergency 
Management Agency (TEMA) and UWI are currently assessing and working on various aspects of climate 
variability and change. However, the Meteorological Services Division along with its partner agencies may 
need to further collaborate in assessing climate change and ensuring that information is made available 
on a regular and timely basis. One of the first tasks in this collaborative effort ought to be the 
development of a set of indicators on climate change for Trinidad and Tobago, for which data can be 
collected, which could be used to measure the effects of climate variability and change. 
The Initial National Communication to the UNFCCC notes that “one of the most important aspects 
of adaptation planning to global climate change is the ability to continuously assess the vulnerability and 
adaptation options of various sectors with some degree of certainty”. Given that the impacts of climate 
change are likely to vary as scientists and technocrats are increasingly able to make more accurate and 
valid predictions, it would be important to establish the necessary institutions to ensure there is a steady 
Traffic congestion on a rainy morning along 
the Uriah Butler Highway

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 56
text:
29 
 
 
flow of information to planners and decision makers. Nowhere is this more critical as in the Caribbean, 
given its disproportionate vulnerability as a region consisting mainly of SIDS. It is, therefore, 
understandable that over the years several regional institutions and projects have been established to 
ensure that this is a reality. Trinidad and Tobago has participated or is participating in the regional project 
Caribbean Planning for Adaptation to Global Climate Change (CPACC), and the subsequent 
Mainstreaming Adaptation to Global Climate Change (MACC) project, is field testing a manual on the 
Clean Development Mechanism, and is developing a National Energy Policy Framework proposed by the 
Caribbean Renewable Energy Development Programme (CREDP). The country is also part of the 
Caribbean Community Climate Change Centre (CCCCC) whose role is: 
a) to protect the climate system of Member countries for the benefit of present and future 
generations; 
b) to enhance regional institutional capabilities for the co-ordination of national responses to 
the negative effects of climate change; 
c) to provide comprehensive policy and technical support in the area of climate change and related 
issues and spearheading regional initiatives in those areas; and 
d) to perform the role of executing agency for regional environmental projects relating to 
climate 
change. 
Behavioural change is seen as key to environmental management and so the EMA has conducted 
lectures and discussions with communities and schools in an effort to inform about the issues of climate 
change. 
With the signs of climate change now validated, sustainable land management is a critical area 
which needs to be addressed if Trinidad and Tobago is to successfully adapt to its effects. 
In recent times, the policy framework relating to the sustainable use and management of land 
resources has been strengthened with the development of the National Action Programme (NAP) to 
Combat Land Degradation (MPUE 2006), the enunciation of a new Forest Policy for Trinidad and Tobago 
in 1998 (Forestry Division, 1998), the Strategic Plan for the Forestry Division (Forestry Division 2001), 
and the adoption of a National Wetlands Policy in 2001 (National Wetlands Committee 2002). The 
rehabilitation of the Nariva Swamp began in 1996 after Government brought to an end large-scale 
intensive agricultural activities taking place in the area. Forestry Division has an on-going and active 
reforestation programme, and several community-based organisations are actively involved in the 
reforestation of denuded watersheds particularly in the Northern Range. Perhaps one of the significant 
activities has been the implementation of the community-based National Reforestation and Watershed 
Rehabilitation Programme (NRWRP) in 2004 which targets the replanting of 33,030 ha of forest over a 10 
year period. If successfully implemented this programme has the potential to substantially increase forest 
cover which serves as sinks for carbon dioxide. Further information on the programme is provided in the 
chapter on biodiversity (see Chapter Seven). 
3.3 Enhanced Greenhouse Effect and Global Warming 
3.3.1 Background 
The average temperature of the Earth is kept at about 15 ºC by the blanket of atmosphere that 
surrounds it. Without this atmosphere, the average temperature would be about minus 18 ºC which would 
be too cold to successfully sustain life. Achieving this average temperature is possible due to the ‘natural 
greenhouse effect’. The Sun’s energy travels to the Earth, most of which passes through the atmosphere. 
This energy is absorbed by the Earth’s surface, warming it and increasing its temperature, and in turn, heat 
energy is radiated from the Earth into the atmosphere. This heat energy is absorbed by certain gases in 
the atmosphere, thus trapping this energy there and increasing the temperature and maintaining the 
Earth’s temperature and energy balance.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 57
text:
30 
 
 
The main GHGs, which occur naturally in the atmosphere, are water vapour, carbon dioxide, 
methane, and nitrous oxide. Increases in these gases enhance the atmosphere’s capacity to trap heat 
energy which leads to increases in the Earth’s surface temperature. The processes and impact of 
increased GHG is known as the enhanced greenhouse effect. Since the industrial era human-induced 
increases in the level of GHGs, particularly carbon dioxide, are reportedly responsible for changes in 
today’s global and regional climatic patterns. In the absence of on-going research, at this stage one could 
only deduce what adverse impacts of climate change would likely be on Trinidad and Tobago. 
The negative climate change impacts due to increased GHG emissions that may affect Trinidad 
and Tobago, some of which are examined extensively in the Initial National Communication for the 
UNFCCC (EMA 2001b), include: 
• 
Sea level rise and extreme rainfall events which will serve to exacerbate the problem of coastal 
erosion, damage to coastal infrastructure, and perennial flooding in low lying coastal areas. Many 
coastal communities are susceptible to coastal erosion particularly communities along the east, 
southeast, northwest and southwest coasts of Trinidad, and southwest Tobago. The IMA noted a 1 m 
per year and higher loss of coastline in the Ortoire region in the 1990s, while Singh (1997) reported on 
an approximate 2 m loss of coastline in Coral Point and Icacos, a 2.5 m loss in Guayaguayare, and 3 
m annual loss in Los Iros. However, accretion was observed close to Punta de Arenal at a rate of 2.3 
m annually 
 Rise in air temperature which increases the risk of crop damage, reduces agricultural production 
and negatively impacts the livelihoods of domestic and export farmers and their families 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Gas flaring releases soot and carbon 
monoxide (among other pollutants) into 
the atmosphere

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 58
text:
31 
 
 
 
• 
Increasing temperatures and increases in days with temperature extremes would affect human 
health, particularly for the elderly and infirm. Variation in rainfall patterns will affect the flowering 
and fruiting patterns of many trees and this, along with increase temperature, will extend the 
activities of vectors and pest 
• 
Extreme storms and increased rainfall, coupled with increased forest denudation, will increase flooding 
and landslide events, soil erosion, and runoff, and decrease aquifer recharge and water management, 
and generally put greater pressure on national and regional disaster management systems 
• 
Coral reefs will be under threat from increased coastal turbidity, sea level rise and increased sea 
surface temperatures, and this can have serious socioeconomic impacts, particularly in Tobago 
where the tourism sector is the main economic driver. Sea level rise and increase runoff and siltation 
will also negatively affect the country’s wetland systems, nearshore fish stocks, riverine fisheries, and 
may ultimately influence the dietary patterns of the population and 
• 
Any change in climate can potentially have an adverse effect on all biodiversity. Sea water 
intrusion, for example, has already impacted on the biodiversity of the South Oropouche river 
system. 
The Initial National Communication for the UNFCCC considers the Caroni Basin the most vulnerable 
to the impacts of climate change and sea level rise. This is because it is the most densely populated area of 
the country and also has a concentration of critical biodiversity which encompasses from the forested 
Northern Range to the coastal mangrove and swamp fringes. Most of the country’s important surface and 
groundwater resources are found in this zone. Current inappropriate land management practices are 
already putting this region under threat of deforestation, annual fires, perennial flooding, siltation of water 
courses and overland and freshwater pollution and loss of biodiversity. Any change in climate is likely to 
further exacerbate the situation. 
3.3.2 Pressure 
With more than 40 % of the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) derived from the energy sector, both 
the economy and lifestyles of the people of Trinidad and Tobago are driven primarily on the consumption 
of the country’s oil and natural gas reserves. The use of these resources extends from primary 
production, transportation, power generation, refining and processing and downstream petrochemical 
production. This reliance on the energy sector is projected to continue in the long term, as the country’s 
future development is based on a further deepening and strengthening of the industrialisation process. As 
of 2005, there were 27 energy-based plants and this number would increase substantially over the next 5 
to 10 years with the proposed establishment of other energy-based industries. The recent upsurge in 
activities in the oil and natural gas sector can only spell increased GHG emissions. 
Emissions of GHG due to road transportation are substantial and are likely to rise with increasing 
numbers of vehicular traffic. Since the mid 1990s, the number of motor vehicles on register has almost 
doubled to just over 400,000 (Figure 3.12). The country’s recent windfall and affluence from the energy 
industry is likely to continue pushing the population’s expectations for owning cars and other motor 
vehicles. At present, the number of registered vehicles per capita stands at 0.312 as compared with 0.117 
in 1995. With the main form of passenger transportation in Trinidad and Tobago being private vehicles 
(about 69 % of all registered motor vehicles and 0.204 vehicles per capita in 2004), the control of total 
vehicle numbers on the whole, and more specifically the private vehicle numbers, will continue to be a 
challenge for the nation’s policy makers.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 60
text:
33 
 
 
processes emissions from private companies. Nonetheless, based on the inventory and as reported in the 
2001 Initial National Communication under the UNFCCC (EMA 2001b), the total anthropogenic carbon 
dioxide emissions are estimated at 14,988 Gg and non carbon dioxide emissions at 2,561 Gg. Thus, carbon 
dioxide is the main GHG being emitted (95.4 % of global warming potential [GWP]), followed by methane 
(4.5 % of GWP). 
According to the 2001 Initial National Communication, the energy sector was the main contributor of 
GHG emissions. The combustion of fossil fuel for the production of energy contributed 66 % of total GHG 
emissions, with industrial processes accounting for the remaining 34 %. Within the energy sector, energy 
industries accounted for 44 % of the total emissions, with manufacturing and construction industries 
contributing 38 %, transportation 15 % and other activities 3 %. The main source of methane emissions came 
from waste, followed by 18 % from agriculture and 11 % from other sources. It was estimated that 62 % of 
nitrous oxide emissions came from agriculture and 34 % from waste. 
The 2001 Initial National Communication also indicated that land use change and forestry (where 
forests serve as greenhouse sinks) removed anthropogenic GHG emissions by 10.2 %. However, this was 
reportedly due largely to abandonment of agricultural holdings and estates. The recent increase in 
deforestation and land clearance may reverse this trend. 
Estimations some four years later (in 1994) painted a picture of increased net GHG emissions and 
changes in the sources of these emissions. An EMA report on the Environmental and Human Health 
Effects of Vehicular Exhaust (EMA unpublished report) showed that carbon dioxide emissions for 1994 
remained the highest, approximately 683 % higher than the 1990 estimates (Table 3.3). Methane

By all counts, individual contribution of the various gases to total GHG emissions from road 
transportation has increased significantly and has become the major source of anthropogenic nitrous

While in 1990 road transport contributed less than 10 % of carbon dioxide and methane emissions, 
by 1994 the sector was responsible for more than 30 % of total emissions. Such emissions can be

3.3.4 Response 
The Kyoto Protocol is the result of negotiations at the third Conference of the Parties (COP-3) for 
the UNFCCC in Kyoto, Japan, in 1997. This Protocol significantly strengthens the UNFCCC by 
committing Annex I Parties to individual, legally-binding targets to limit or reduce their GHG emissions5 by 
the end of the first commitment period (2012). The gases covered under the Protocol include carbon 
dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs), perfluorocarbons (PFCs) and sulphur 
hexafluoride. However, only countries that have ratified this agreement become binding to its 
commitments. To date, 168 countries and one regional economic integration organisation (the European 
Economic Community - EEC) have ratified the Protocol. Of these, 35 countries and the EEC are required 
to reduce GHG emissions below levels specified for each of them in the Treaty. These add up to a total 
cut in GHG emissions of at least 5 % from 1990 levels in the commitment period of 2008 to 2012 
(UNFCCC 2008). 
Trinidad and Tobago ratified the Protocol in January 1999 and as part of its obligations, the country 
is expected to: 
• 
Formulate cost-effective national programmes to improve the quality of local emission factors, 
activity data, and/or models that reflect the socio-economic conditions for the preparation and 
periodic updating of national inventories of anthropogenic emissions of all GHG not controlled by 
the Protocol. 
• 
Formulate, implement, publish and regularly update national programmes containing measures to 
mitigate climate change and measures to facilitate adequate adaptation to climate change with 
respect to the energy, transport and industry sectors as well as agriculture, forestry and waste 
management. Furthermore, utilisation of adaptation technologies and methods for improving spatial 
planning would improve adaptation to climate change. 
• 
Cooperate in the promotion of effective modalities for the development, application and diffusion of, 
and take all practicable steps to promote, facilitate and finance as appropriate, the transfer of or 
access to environmentally sound technologies, know-how, practices and processes pertinent to 
climate change. 
• 
Cooperate in scientific and technical research and promote the maintenance and the development 
of systematic observation systems and the development of data archives to: reduce uncertainties 
related to the climate system; the adverse impacts of climate change and the economic and social 
consequences of various response strategies; and to promote the development and strengthening 
of endogenous capacities and capabilities to participate in international and intergovernmental 
efforts, programmes and networks on research and systematic observation. 
• 
Cooperate in and promote at the international level (using existing bodies where appropriate), the 
development and implementation of education and training programmes. This includes: the 
strengthening of national capacity building (in particular human and institutional capacities) and the 
exchange or secondment of personnel to train experts in this field; and facilitate at the national 
level public awareness of, and public access to, information on climate change. 
• 
Include in their national communication information on programmes and activities undertaken 
under this Protocol. 
 
5 These countries include Australia, Austria, Belarus, Belgium, Bulgaria, Canada, Croatia, Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, 
European Community, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Iceland, Ireland, Italy, Japan, Latvia, Liechtenstein, Lithuania, 
Luxembourg, Monaco, Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Romania, Russian Federation, Slovakia, Slovenia, 
Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States of America.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 64
text:
37 
 
 
Currently Trinidad and Tobago has no national policy on the limitation or reduction of the carbon 
dioxide emissions and other GHGs or on climate change. However, given the world’s current heightened 
awareness and sensitivity to climate change and GHG emissions, many countries and critics to the Kyoto 
Protocol are calling for an alternative which may be more inclusive of both developed and developing 
countries. 
3.4 Air Quality - Vehicular Emissions and Particulate Pollutants 
3.4.1 Background 
Air pollution has become a major concern to the people of Trinidad and Tobago given the adverse 
impacts air pollutants may have on human health and other life-forms. The 2000 State of the Environment 
Report (EMA 2001) defines air pollution as the contamination of the atmosphere by any toxic or 
radioactive gases and particulate matter as a result of human activity. Therefore air pollutants, which can 
be produced from people-made or natural sources, are substances whose presence in the atmosphere 
cause or are likely to cause harm to human, plant and/or animal life; damage human-made materials and 
structures; bring about changes in weather or climate; or interfere with the enjoyment of life or property. 
Table 3.4 provides information on the health effects of various air pollutants. 
In Trinidad and Tobago, air pollution is caused mostly by emissions from motor vehicles and 
energy based industries. Forest and bush fires, the annual burning of the sugar cane crop during the 
harvest, and the burning of landfills are also sources of air pollutants. These air borne particles vary in 
size, with the smaller sized particles remaining suspended in the atmosphere for a considerable period 
of time. The larger particles may eventually settle out, coating trees, soil and hard surfaces. Air 
pollutants may also obscure visibility and create unsightly images as they coat buildings and other 
structures. Low visibility tends to occur during the dry season when there are more frequent bush and 
forest fires and landfill sites are known to be on fire. Bush fires can be more problematic during El Niño 
years. 
3.4.2 Pressure 
Motor vehicle exhaust is a complex mixture of gases, the composition of which depends on the 
quality and type of fuel, design, and engineering of the vehicle, age and population of vehicles, operating 
conditions of the engine, maintenance programmes, and use of emission control devices (EMA 
unpublished report). Most passenger type vehicles and some light trucks are equipped with spark-
ignition engines, which utilise mainly gasoline, although some may be reconfigured to use compressed 
natural gas (CNG). Spark-ignition type engines emit air pollutants including hydrocarbons, carbon 
monoxide, nitrogen oxides and a small amount of particulates. 
Most heavy trucks, buses and maxi-taxis and some cars in Trinidad and Tobago use diesel fuel 
engines, which could be up to 100 % more fuel efficient than gasoline type engines. As a result, lower 
specific emissions of carbon monoxide and hydrocarbons are reported, with greater amounts of nitrogen 
oxides (particularly nitric oxide and nitrous oxide) emitted due to the higher combustion temperatures and 
excess oxygen. There can be a significant amount of particulate matter, as evident by black exhaust 
smoke, which primarily consists of carbon based soot, sulphates and hydrocarbons adsorbed onto the 
soot particles. With the current trend in the increase in the amount of vehicles in the country, one can 
expect greater fuel consumption with an accompanied increase in the emission of pollutant gases, as well 
as associated effects on the environment and human health. These impact projections can be mitigated 
to some extent if cleaner, more efficient fuels are used and if vehicle exhaust standards are enforced. 
3.4.3 Current State and Trends 
At present the EMA has established an air quality monitoring station at the Point Lisas Industrial 
Estate to inform and track trends on particular air pollutants. Figures 3.16 to 3.19 show the levels of

A second ambient air quality monitoring programme was executed by the Town and Country 
Planning Division (TCPD). This project which included measurements of air quality parameters (nitrous 
oxides, sulphur oxides, volatile organic compounds, total suspended particulates [TSP] of aerodynamic 
diameter 10 µm [PM10] or 2.5 µm [PM2.5], nitrates in TSP, sulphate in PM2.5 and metals in PM10) was 
conducted over a four-week period in Point Lisas (two stations) and Chaguaramas (one station). Except 
for a single measurement of TSP in the Point Lisas central area which exceeded the United States 
Environmental Protection Agency's (USEPA's) 24-hour average of 150 µgm-3, the levels of all the other 
reported parameters were within accepted international ambient standards. No explanation was provided 
for this single discrepancy. 
In addition, there have been several pollutant inventory studies done to characterise air pollution 
sources and estimate loads, some of which used simplified models (UNDP/EMA 1998, EMA 2001a) and 
one based on actual consumption data (EMA 2001b). Apart from these specific pollutant inventory 
studies, the EMA has established an investigation and complaints desk since 1995, and has compiled 
and categorised data on air quality complaints from 1995 to 2000. From the 2000 analysis of the data it 
can be concluded that air pollution is an area of major concern for the public, with two activities which 
stand out, namely autobody repair and mechanics, and woodworking (Figure 3.20).

The Case of Diesel Engines 
Diesel engines are generally more efficient, which allows them to emit a significantly lower 
concentration of carbon monoxide and hydrocarbons than equivalent gasoline engines. However, diesel 
engines are a significant source of nitrogen oxides, specifically nitric oxide, which reacts photo-chemically 
with hydrocarbons to form ground level ozone or smog. Data from the recently commissioned ambient air 
monitoring equipment at Point Lisas confirms that ground level ozone is formed and peaks at around the 
same time as peak traffic hours. Additionally, diesel engines emit a significant amount of particulate 
matter in exhaust gases, particularly in urban areas and high traffic areas. Under the same engine and 
load conditions, diesel engines emit 100 times more particulates than gasoline engines. 
Although diesel exhaust contains over 450 different chemical components, the main threats of 
diesel emissions to public health can be classified under three broad categories: fine particulate matter, 
oxides of nitrogen, and toxic compounds. Table 3.5 summarises the main sources of industrial air 
pollution by sector and activity.

3.4.4 Response 
There are several pieces of legislation being considered to deal with the control of some aspects of 
air pollution. One such is the Draft Motor Vehicle and Road Traffic (Air Pollution) Regulations which 
targets vehicles emitting ‘smoke’ and without necessary ‘equipment/device’ that would minimise the 
release of air pollutants. Under these proposed regulations there would be penalties for violators and 
vehicles are expected to be inspected and certified for its road worthiness. To date, it is only under Rule 
38 (13) of the Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Regulations, made under Section 100 of the Motor 
Vehicles and Road Traffic Act, that persons emitting smoke, visible vapours, sparks, or oils, can be 
prosecuted.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 70
text:
43 
 
 
However, enforcement remains a major challenge as institutional capacities remain limited. 
Nonetheless, the MPUE has identified air pollution as a priority area where gaps exists. To this end, air 
pollution rules, initially drafted in 2001, are expected to be laid in Parliament in 2006 along with other 
critical pieces of environmental legislation. The draft Air Pollution Rules of 2001 provide a basis for 
protecting public health from the adverse effects of air pollution, and for eliminating, or reducing to a 
minimum, those air pollutants that are known to be or are likely to be, hazardous to human health and 
well-being and the environment. These Rules provide a mechanism for the establishment of a register of 
air pollution facilities and sources, and a permitting system for the release of air pollutants. In addition, 
they also establish ambient air quality standards for short term and long term maximum allowable levels 
for air pollutants and stack release limits. 
A national ambient air quality monitoring system will need to be developed and implemented so as 
to determine the quality of the ambient air in various key locations in Trinidad and Tobago, the extent of 
air pollution, the significant sources and types of pollutants, and the characterisation or description of air 
pollution. 
3.5 Stratospheric Ozone Depletion 
3.5.1 Background 
Ozone, though considered a pollutant in the lower atmosphere (troposphere), occurs naturally in the 
stratosphere, forming a protective layer against harmful UV radiation from the sun. Constantly being produced 
and destroyed in the stratosphere, ozone concentrations across the globe remains constantly in equilibrium, 
except above the poles where concentrations rise each summer and decline during the winter months. This 
seasonal variation is especially pronounced in the Antarctic. It was discovered in the 1970s that the natural 
order of this naturally occurring layer was threatened by the increased use and release of various human-
produced substances, such as chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), methyl bromide and halon. In the presence of solar 
UV rays, these substances react to destroy ozone molecules. This reaction is pronounced at the North and 
South Poles in winter months, which adds to the seasonal dips observed in ozone concentration. In the 
Antarctic, the arrival of spring serves to worsen the situation such that an ‘ozone hole’ develops as levels 
become low and the layer ‘thins’. 
Depletion of the stratospheric ozone layer reduces its protective function by exposing humans and 
the environment to adverse effects of increased solar UV radiation. Human health impacts include the 
increased incidence of skin cancers, cataracts, blindness, and damage to the immune system. Solar UV 
radiation can disrupt terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems and biogeochemical cycles, and adversely affect 
plant life. 
The Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer was adopted in 1987 to limit 
the production and phase-out the use of ODS and related substances. Evidence suggests that 
international efforts to reduce the release of ODS appear to be working. Research has shown that 
between 1979 and 1997 ozone diminished at a rate of 8 % each decade in the upper stratosphere. 
However, between 1997 and 2002, the rate of depletion slowed to a projected average of just 4 % per 
decade (National Geographic Society 2003). It is predicted that the ozone layer will be back to its pre-
industrial condition within another 50 years. 
3.5.2 Pressure 
Many CFCs and other ODS, such as methyl bromide and halons, are very stable, with long half 
lives and thus remain in the atmosphere interacting with the ozone layer for a considerable period of time. 
One CFC molecule has the potential to destroy as many as 10,000 ozone molecules. The impact of 
certain ODS on the ozone layer is measured as their ozone depletion potential (ODP). Different 
substances have different ODPs.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 71

44 
 
 
The ODPs of CFCs range from 0.6 to 1.0, with most having a value of 1.0. The ODP of methyl 
bromide is estimated as 0.38, whilst methyl chloroform has an ODP of 0.1. Halons, which are the most 
powerful ozone depleters, have ODPs which range between 4.0 and 12.0. Table 3.6 shows the ODP of 
substances under the Montreal Protocol currently imported into Trinidad and Tobago.

3.5.3 Current State and Trends 
Trinidad and Tobago imports all of its requirements for ODS. The State of the Environment Report, 
2000 (EMA 2001a) noted that consumption data is a good guide in determining emissions of ODS since 
discharged or leaked ODS in existing installations are replaced by new consumption. In new installations, 
without recycling, ODS will also be eventually released into the atmosphere, and need to be replaced by new 
consumption. Figure 3.21 and Table 3.7 show the consumption of CFCs and other ODS in Trinidad and 
Tobago for the period 1990 to 2000. Consumption of ODS declined up until 1993, after which a steady rise in 
consumption was observed, reaching a peak of 156 tonnes in 1998. A decrease in consumption was once 
again recorded in 1999 followed by another increase in ODS consumption. However, the country recorded an 
ODS consumption of 52.1 ODP tonnes in 2005. 
3.5.4 Response 
One hundred and ninety one countries worldwide have made the initial legal commitment to 
stop the production and to phase-out the use of all main types of ODS through the Montreal Protocol. 
Annex 4 summarises the Montreal Protocol Control Measures.

Trinidad and Tobago ratified the Montreal Protocol in 1989 and ratified all the subsequent 
amendments and adjustments (London 1990; Copenhagen 1992; Vienna 1995; Montreal 1997; Beijing 
1999).

The EMA has established the National Ozone Unit (NOU) which has established a quota system 
and a reliable licensing and enforcement system for importers of ODS. To support its effort, the EMA has 
developed a communication strategy to inform the public and to bring continuous public awareness about 
ODS and the implementation of the Montreal Protocol. The Government has also taken steps towards 
achieving its compliance targets (Table 3.8). From the beginning of 2008, Trinidad and Tobago has made 
the commitment to no longer import CFCs, which will be two years ahead of most of the Article 5 
countries. 
The National Halon Bank Management Plan was developed in 2001 by the EMA (EMA 2001c) with the 
assistance of the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP). Halons are utilised in fixed and portable 
fire protection equipment by various stakeholders which include insurance companies, fire equipment 
owners, halon users and owners and government ministries (Custom and Excise Department, the protective 
services, etc).

The Plan was prepared to implement the halon phase-out schedule under the Montreal Protocol. 
The use of Halon 1211 has almost been eliminated and so this phased Plan at the time provided a 
strategy which addresses the continued consumption of Halon 1301 and enables Trinidad and Tobago to: 
•

•

• 
Identify essential and non-essential uses of halons while seeking adequate alternatives for

• 
Recover all halons from non-essential uses and distribute only for essential uses. 
• 
Achieve the targeted phase-out programme for halons in Trinidad and Tobago. 
The plan calls for the establishment of the Bank to manage stocks of halons to meet the country’s 
needs up until 2005 when their use will be terminated. Trinidad and Tobago’s 2005 report to the Ozone

48 
 
 
Secretariat indicated that the country has phased out its halon consumption and sustained this phase out 
process for the past three years. The report also revealed that the country has reduced its CFC consumption 
considerably beyond the 50 % CFC consumption reduction target and has almost met the 85 % reduction 
requirement scheduled for 2007. Thus, Trinidad and Tobago is on its way to meeting its planned target of 
complete phase-out of its CFC consumption in 2008. 
2007 will be the last year with CFC imports in the country, and 2008 will be the first year without any CFC 
imports. The NOU is expected to work towards a reduction in the demand of methyl bromide. Therefore, the public 
awareness activities and a good communication strategy will continue to be central elements for achieving 
successful termination of all ODS imports. 
3.6 Other Pollutants: Sahara Dust 
3.6.1 Background 
Each year, massive dust storms from in the Sahara and the Sahel deserts occur in Northern Africa 
creating a reservoir of dust particles over Northern Africa. Strong winds, combined with the rising warm 
air, lift dust as much as 3,048 to 4,572 m (10,000 to 15,000 feet) into the Saharan Air Layer, forming 
dense clouds which are transported across the Atlantic (between 10° and 20° North latitude) to the 
Caribbean, South-eastern USA, and Central and South America. It is estimated that it takes dust particles 
5 to 7 days to reach the Caribbean (Mohammed and Agard 2005). The movement of these dust clouds 
usually coincides with the summer months in the Northern Hemisphere, between February and October. 
Evidence suggests that movement of dust across Northern Africa has been increasing in recent years 
because of increased periods of drought and because of desertification. 
These particles of dust travel across the Atlantic altering air quality and the climate processes, 
changing soil chemistry and depositing loads of minerals in water bodies. These also affect plant and 
animal life and create health problems for susceptible humans. Research has shown that these dust 
particles transport micro-organisms, fungi and chemical contaminants. Sahara dust in the atmosphere is 
known to cause respiratory problems for asthmatics. 
3.6.2 Pressure 
Saharan dust events are unavoidable. In its 2004 special report on the movement of Sahara dust, 
the United State's National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) reported that in July 2000, 
almost eight mmt of Sahara dust reached Puerto Rico and this measurement equaled one-fifth of the total 
year’s dust deposit. The trans-Atlantic transport of Sahara dust is not a recent phenomenon. However, 
the amount of material reaching the Caribbean has increased in recent times as well as the composition 
of the dust is believed to have changed because of human activity. Current data suggest that Africa is 
experiencing an intensive phase of drought and the concentration of dust reaching the Caribbean region 
is estimated to be three to four times higher than that experienced in the 1960s (World Health 
Organisation 2003). 
3.6.3 Current State and Trends 
The presence of Sahara dust in the atmosphere around Trinidad and Tobago has been observed over 
the years and documented in the first air quality study, carried out over a six week period in 1999 to monitor 
suspended particulate matter concentrations along the East-West Corridor (Rajkumar and Siung-Chang 
2000). The results showed that the daily maximum PM10 concentrations approached the USEPA 24-hour 
ambient standard of 150 µgm-3 only on days when Sahara dust was present in the atmosphere. These results 
suggest that Saharan dust events can make up a significant portion of the PM10 concentration in the air and 
can be an important aspect of ambient air quality.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 76
text:
49 
 
 
A study was conducted of paediatric asthma patients who attended the Accident and Emergency 
(A&E) department of the Paediatric Priority Care Facility at the Eric Williams Medical Sciences Complex 
in relation to Saharan dust visibility and other climatic variables for the period 23 May, 2001 to 13 May, 
2002. During the study period, some 2,655 patients visited A&E department with acute asthma and the 
results established a link between increased paediatrics asthma admissions and increased Saharan dust 
cover. 
Apart from the link to respiratory problems in humans, it has been discovered that the toxic fungal 
pathogen known as Aspergillus sydowii was travelling in the dust particles and could be responsible for 
sea fan diseases in the Caribbean. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.6.4 Response 
The response of the Sahara dust challenge has mainly been from a research perspective, looking at the 
cause and effect relationship between the emergency admissions of children with acute asthma and the 
occurrence of Sahara dust episodes. This work is currently being undertaken in collaboration with the US 
Geological Survey (USGS). A station, supplied by the USGS, has been established at the top of a lighthouse on 
Galera Point at the northeast tip of Trinidad, where air coming from Africa is sampled before it moves inland. 
This station provides information on the concentrations of airborne dust, metals, persistent organic pollutants, 
and microorganisms coming across the Atlantic. The results provide background information which can be 
compared with air-quality data from the industrialised areas of Trinidad. It is hoped that the effect of particulates, 
persistent organic pollutants (POPs), metals, and microbes coming from Africa can be separated from the effect 
of locally generated pollutants on emergency admissions for asthma. These data will also be compared with 
similar data from Cape Verde, a group of islands off Africa’s west coast. 
 
 
On July 24, 2003, a Saharan dust storm was draped over 
the Atlantic Ocean and the Canary Islands off northwest 
Africa. Source: NASA. 2003.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 77
text:
50 
 
 
Chapter 4: Land 
 
4.1 Overview 
Land, described by the UNCCD as the “… terrestrial bio-productive system that comprises soil, 
vegetation, other biota, and the ecological and hydrological processes that operate within the system” 
(United Nations, 2007), provides a range of services and products which support human existence. In 
Trinidad and Tobago, land resources are utilised for agricultural and forestry production, regulation of the 
atmospheric and water cycles, mineral supply and mining, human settlement and waste disposal. Indeed, 
land is the foundation of human civilisations and an object of acquisition and conflict over the millennia. 
Human manipulation of the land to provide food, clothing, shelter and transportation has led to a 
growing number of challenges to its capacity to continue to provide services and products in ever 
increasing quantity and variety. The actual changing of aspects of the land and its processes are 
threatening the very survival of humankind and has led over time to a more critical look at the way land 
resources are utilised. As a result, there is increasingly universal recognition of the need for a different 
approach to land utilisation in order to reduce the negative impacts or mitigate those that are already 
threatening the Earth. 
Trinidad and Tobago has accepted the need to act in concert with the countries of the Caribbean 
Region and the rest of the world in finding and implementing solutions to the problems which currently 
challenge the land and its natural processes. To this end, the nation has assented to a number of 
international treaties and protocols that have implications to land management such as the UNCCD, the 
UNCBD (United Nations, 1992), the Convention on Wetlands of International Importance (Ramsar 
Convention) and the Protocol for Specially Protected Areas and Wildlife. 
 The archipelagic Republic of Trinidad and Tobago consists of 5,128 km2 (5,128,000 ha) with 
Trinidad accounting for 4,828 km2 (4,828,000 ha) and Tobago for 300 km2 (300,000 ha) or 5.9% of the 
land mass. 
 
 
 
Trinidad extends 80 km from north to south and 59 km from east to west and contains five 
physiographic regions, namely: the Northern, Central and Southern Ranges and the intervening lowlands 
called the Northern and Southern Basins (Figure 4.1). The highest range is the Northern Range which 
contains the country’s highest peak, El Cerro del Aripo at 940 m, and the second highest, El Tucuche at 
Upper Santa Cruz Valley, 
Northern Range, Trinidad

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 78
text:
51 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
932 m. Tobago, on the other hand, is approximately 42 km long from north-east to south-west and 12 km from 
north to south and consists of two main physiographic regions, namely: the Main Ridge in the northeast, and 
the Coastal Plain in the southwest. The island’s highest point, some 650 m above sea level, is located on the 
Main Ridge which runs for about two thirds of the length of the island in a south-west to north-east orientation. 
Utilisation of the nation’s land resources is organised primarily around: agriculture–including 
forestry; mining; urbanisation; waste disposal; and transportation. 
In 2004, agriculture, excluding forestry, accounted for some 85,000 ha while it was estimated that 
forestry covered some 48% of the nation’s land or approximately 248,000 ha. Thus agriculture and forestry 
combined accounted for 333,000 ha. 
Mining accounted for a substantial but indeterminate quantity of land. Approximately 2,833 ha 
were under license to quarry operators. Data were not available on land licensed to companies engaged 
in land-based oil and gas mining. 
In the case of urbanisation, it is estimated that some 15% or 127,000 ha of the land mass is 
urbanised. However, data are not readily available on the size of the urban centres even though the sizes 
of the individual cities and boroughs are known. The contiguous areas which are separated 
administratively from the city and borough centres are in some instances intensely urban, but have not 
been recently reflected in the available data. 
This chapter deals with the four principal areas of land use and expects to build over time on the 
data that are not readily available. 
4.2 Urbanisation 
4.2.1 Description 
The latter half of the 20th century saw the expansion of some of the traditional towns and villages; 
the densification of old uses; and the development of housing estates, resort facilities and commercial 
and industrial sites on green field sites throughout the nation. Villages strung along main roads and often 
along ridges, began to coalesce into urban centres particularly in western, eastern and southern Trinidad 
Figure 4.1: Physiographic 
regions of Trinidad and 
Tobago

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 79
text:
52 
 
 
 
 
 
and western Tobago. Thus, ribbon development is to be seen along the Western and Eastern Main 
Roads in north Trinidad; the Southern Main Road in central Trinidad; the Naparima/Mayaro and 
Guaracara/ Tabaquite Roads in south Trinidad; and Milford Road in south-west Tobago. The East-West 
Corridor, which in north Trinidad spans a distance of some 48 km, epitomises this continuous form of 
linear urban development. 
 Data from the CSO for the period 1960 to 1990 (CSO 1995) showed decreasing populations for 
the cities of Port of Spain and San Fernando, and an increase in population for the borough of Arima. In 
2000, the population of Chaguanas was the highest of all areas censored (CSO 2000). During 1960 to 
1990 there was movement towards suburban living as populations relocated to the new housing 
subdivisions constructed on green field sites on the periphery of the core urban centres of both Port of 
Spain and San Fernando, leading to the extended urban areas identified as greater Port of Spain, and the 
San Fernando sub-region. Table 4.1 shows the total population and population density for the major 
urban centres for selected years.

Both total population and population densities decreased between 1960 and 1990. In 2000 there 
was an increase in population but a reduction in density for both cities. The geographic expansion of Port 
of Spain and the granting of city status to a redefined San Fernando may have been responsible for the 
continued decrease in density. However, Point Fortin’s population decreased while its density increased.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 80
text:
53 
 
 
 
 
The East-West Corridor in the capital region which extends from Carenage in the west to Arima in the 
east is the country’s major urban centre, and it has grown in population, size and complexity. In 1970, the 
capital region accommodated some 443,100 or 44% of the national population. In 1975 some 691,176 or 
approximately 62% of the national population lived in predominantly urban areas and the capital region 
accounted for approximately 490,512 or some 70% (TCPD 1984)7. The urban population continued to grow 
over the last 30 years with the development of new residential, commercial and industrial areas in the existing 
urban centres as well as new ones. Couva/Point Lisas, Maloney, Canaan/Bon Accord have all been 
significantly populated principally from new residential subdivisions and internal migration. However, there are 
no current data on urban populations by development planning regions. The Population Division of the United 
Nations estimates the urbanisation rate of Trinidad and Tobago to be 75% of the total population (Population 
Division of the United Nations Secretariat 2002). 
The 2000 Census of Population and Housing has identified the five main urban centres as the two 
cities of Port of Spain and San Fernando, and the three Boroughs of Arima, Point Fortin and Chaguanas. 
These urban centres had a combined urban population of 223,217 (Table 4.2) but this number excluded 
the built up areas which are contiguous to these centres and function as integral parts of their social and 
economic activities. When the urban areas are described as Greater Port of Spain, San Fernando sub-
region, Greater Arima and Point Fortin including Cap De Ville, the urban population expands dramatically.

Unfortunately, there are no current standard definitions of these greater urban areas and thus they 
will need to be defined spatially. Nonetheless, there is no doubt that there has been both an expansion of 
the major urban centres as well as densification of the land uses within these centres. 
 
 
 
From casual observation, sections of the Southern Main Road in Chaguanas between Montrose 
and the town centre; the Eastern Main Road between Laventille to Trincity; and Milford Road between 
Canaan and Bon Accord have shown both infilling of vacant lots as well as increase in height and bulk of 
buildings. The construction of new shopping malls and the expansion of the older ones have been 
pursued in North, Central and South Trinidad, and in Tobago. 
Within the last five years or so, there has been an increase in physical development in all of the 
major urban centres of both Trinidad and Tobago. A large number of green field sites have been used for 
new housing subdivisions, industrial estates, sporting complexes, commercial establishments, health 
centres, schools and roads. The demand for land for built development is pushed, inter alia, by an 
expanding population with higher expectations, and a government with a commitment to the provision of 
adequate housing and gas-based industrialisation. 
4.2.2 Pressure 
The tremendous increase in physical development has expanded and densified urban centres with 
increasing space devoted to utilities, transportation, housing, industry and commerce. The escalating 
demand has given rise to land clearance, building on steeper and higher slopes, land reclamation and the 
provision of a range of services and amenities. These actions have led to impacts on the land such as 
clearing of green field sites, modification of the land form, changes in natural drainage patterns, siltation 
of water courses, flooding and traffic jams. In the marine environment, reclamation works are destroying 
mangrove vegetation, feeding grounds and nurseries for fish and other marine life forms. 
Housing estates are being developed on some 10 sites from Caroni’s ex-sugar cane fields. 
According to the Estate Management Business Development Company (EMBD), some 1,375 ha of land 
have been identified for residential subdivision (Table 4.3). The lot yield is still not known but the population 
of these areas will increase upon occupation of these buildings. 
Some 34 housing estates are being developed by the National Housing Authority (NHA)/Housing 
Development Corporation (HDC) (Table 4.4) throughout the country on green field sites, some of which 
were until quite recently, productive sugar cane lands owned by Caroni (1975) Limited. Data are still to be 
obtained from the HDC on the number of housing sites, size and location of each site, number of lots and 
projected scale of development. A sample of the sites is noted below. No information was obtained on 
private sector housing initiatives.

4.2.3 Current State and Trends 
Over the last two decades or so, tremendous pressure has been put on agricultural land for 
residential and industrial uses. Development of housing estates has increased with the Government’s 
stated programme to meet the housing demand of some 100,000 families over the next ten years. Over

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 83
text:
56 
 
 
the last two years, the Ministry of Housing through its NHA/HDC has constructed approximately 4,000 
housing units per year. The private sector has also been engaged in the programme of housing 
construction but its production is not publicly documented. 
Industrial development is also proceeding with the construction of a new industrial park at Union Village, 
La Brea and expansion of the Point Lisas Industrial Estate and the Atlantic Liquified Natural Gas facility at 
 
 
 
Point Fortin. Additionally, the Evolving TecKnologies and Enterprise Development Company Limited (eTecK) 
recently commissioned the design and development of several industrial sites in San Fernando, Couva and 
Piarco while the National Energy Corporation (NEC) is proposing the development of an industrial estate at 
Chatham. Much of these activities are taking place or are planned to take place in the more urbanised western 
section of the country. The heavy gas-based industries in particular are to be located along the western coast 
of Trinidad from Caroni to Point Fortin. 
Densification of residential development on existing urban land is also intensifying as formerly single 
family housing on relatively large plots of land are currently being used for medium and high rise town 
houses, condominiums and apartment complexes. This is to be seen in private developments in areas such 
as The Buoys in Dhein’s Bay, La Riviera in Westmoorings, and Bayside Towers in Cocorite. Hillside 
development is also extending on southern flanks of the Northern Range as they accommodate 
development of apartment complexes and townhouses in addition to palatial single family houses and 
unauthorised single-unit residential structures further uphill. 
4.2.4 Response 
The State enacted the Squatter Regularisation Act to regularise unofficial development on some 
250 sites and its Land Settlement Agency (LSA) is currently attempting to enforce the anti squatting law 
given its publicly stated policy of zero tolerance on squatting. Additionally, the LSA is working to improve 
the squatter communities as its mandate dictates (See Section 4.3.4). 
The State Lands Act and its new amendment are intended to discourage illegal occupation or 
unauthorised use of publicly held land. However, enforcement continues to be sporadic and ineffective. 
Decentralisation has been spoken of over the years and some attempt was made to decentralise 
some Government services to regional locations. More needs to be done to effect a significant reduction 
in demand for transportation services. However, recent publicly stated government policies indicate an 
attempt to solve the traffic problems with the introduction of a rapid rail service between the major centres 
of Port of Spain and San Fernando, 
Land clearance for the Union Industrial Estate, 
southwest Trinidad

text:

with spurs to Diego Martin in the west and Arima in the east. In addition, the old idea of utilising water 
taxis between Port of Spain and San Fernando has re-emerged as another possible solution. 
4.3 Mining 
4.3.1 Description 
Mining in Trinidad and Tobago is pursued for the recovery of hydrocarbons and minerals. Figure 
4.2 highlights the economic geology of Trinidad and Tobago. 
The country has commercial deposits of asphalt, oil and natural gas, which constitute its petroleum 
sector and which have been exploited over the years. Mineral deposits, mostly aggregates and clays, have 
been mined historically for use principally in the construction industry. Mining techniques utilised in the extraction 
of the resources include well drilling operations for oil and natural gas and open pit mining for asphalt and 
aggregate. 
Asphalt and oil have been exploited since 1908, and by the 1960’s were among the most 
significant contributors to the country’s foreign exchange earnings. Land-based exploitation became less 
critical as exploration and operation migrated towards the marine environment, particularly in southwest 
and southeast Trinidad. Only within the last 25 years has natural gas been increasingly exploited as an 
export product and for feedstock for other industries.

Both oil and natural gas are being discovered in new fields off the north and northeast coasts 
of the country, while land-based oil and gas continue to be exploited on a reduced scale, particularly 
in Penal, La Brea and Point Fortin. Between October 2000 and May 2001, land-based crude oil 
production was roughly 23% of the nation’s eight month production of 27.2 million barrels. For the 
period October 2004 to May 2005, some four years later, the share of the country’s land based 
crude oil production was approximately 18% of the 32.2 million barrels. While overall production had 
increased over the same eight month period, the share of land-based production had decreased by 
roughly 4%. Table 4.5 shows crude oil production for selected years and the contribution made by

corresponding demand for aggregate. However, no information was available on the quantity 
produced or the volume of aggregate which was imported. 
Aggregate won through quarrying includes sands and gravels, limestone, porcellanite, clays, 
tar and oil sands and andesite. In 2004 there were 56 quarries (licenced and unlicenced) operating 
throughout the country. Minerals used principally in the construction industry are exploited from 
roughly 5,435 ha spread across the Northern and Central Ranges and the northern and southern 
basins, with the majority of sand and gravel deposits identified in the Arima to Valencia area of the 
Northern Basin in Trinidad’s East-West Corridor (MEEI 2005). 
Impacts of mining are felt in the local communities generally close to the primary source of 
operations and are often due to land clearing, excavation, wind-driven pollution, ruptured oil lines, 
abandoned oil mining or transport equipment and transport of material. These have given rise to 
decreased land capability, reduction in aesthetic/amenity value, modification of landform, land 
contamination, and water and air pollution. 
4.3.2 Pressure 
Quarry Material 
The phenomenal expansion of construction especially in the areas of housing, industry, and roads 
over the last three to four years has led to increased activity in widespread winning of aggregate, such 
that local production of aggregate more than doubled between 2001 and 2004. It moved from a reported 
1.891 mcm to 4.206 mcm over the period. However, mining has modified the landform in large areas of 
Wallerfield, Valencia and Longdenville by land clearance and open pit mining. Additionally, washing of 
aggregate has led to silting of several of the rivers close to the location of these quarries, many of which 
are in the Northern Range while crushing of the aggregate has led to air pollution in Wallerfield and 
elsewhere. 
Illegal quarrying is a major source of difficulties particularly in the Wallerfield and Valencia areas 
where operations have been reported to be executed with intimidation and acts of violence on the 
residents. The quantum of land being illegally mined is not readily available, but the Ministry of Energy

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 87
text:
60 
 
 
and Energy Industries’ Draft Quarry Policy (MEEI 2005) reported that about 12 of the 38 operational sand 
and gravel quarries were illegal operations. Land use has been altered from agriculture to mining, and 
degradation of the land and siltation of nearby water courses have had deleterious effects on the adjacent 
agricultural communities. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The demand for quarry material is likely to continue at least in the short to medium term, 
given the level of construction activities taking place in the country. Indeed, the draft policy 
predicted that local quarry production will grow fourfold by 2020 (Table 4.7). Applications to the 
EMA for Certificates of Environmental Clearance (CECs) between 2001 and 2005 indicate 
intensification in land development. Between 2001 and 2005, 486 CEC applications for land 
development were received, with 159 received in 2005. Thus, roughly one third of all applications 
for the period was received in 2005.

Hydrocarbons 
Hydrocarbon (oil, gas and asphalt) development has impacts on local communities through land 
pollution at drill sites, pipeline routes and processing facilities. Some land-based operations over the 
years have led to contamination of some forested areas in Guapo and Guayaguayare; of local 
communities in Penal and Point Fortin; and of inland waters at Point-a-Pierre and Fanny Village, due to 
abandonment of old and economically unproductive wells and/or lack of maintenance of pipelines, pumps

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 89
text:
62 
 
 
and other infrastructure. No information was readily available on the number and locations of abandoned 
oil wells which are leaking and need to be properly decommissioned. 
4.3.3 Current State and Trends 
Quarry Material 
The State anticipated that beyond 2005 it would be able to provide one year licences to most of the 
quarry operators. Thus some 60 mining operations, 57 in Trinidad and 3 in Tobago, should be awarded 
leases on approximately 2,700 ha of land in north, south and central Trinidad and Tobago for the mining 
of sand and gravel, pitrun gravel and blue limestone. It is expected that few licences will be given out for 
minerals such as silica sand, oil sand and andesite. 
The quarry industry was unable to respond to the increasing demands for its products given its current 
capacity. Thus, new quarry sites are likely to be put on the market to satisfy a demand for aggregate which will 
increase over the next few years as the Government’s road expansion programme and its housing construction 
accelerate. 
There are no reliable data on new quarry sites being opened, size of sites and annual production in 
respect of mining of aggregate and, given the reported widespread operations of illegal quarrying 
operations, accurate data are unlikely to be available in the short term. Additionally, there are no data on 
rehabilitation of quarry lands on which materials have been exhausted. 
Land currently mined is located in the Northern Range and its Northern Basin from Santa Cruz to 
Valencia; in the central plains from Carlsen Field to Longdenville; and from Erin to Guapo in the Southern Basin. 
In Tobago, major mining activity is located in Studley Park. No data are available on the size of the area 
disturbed in the extraction of aggregate. 
Hydrocarbons 
Domestic crude oil production exhibited peaks and troughs over the years but appears to be 
trending upwards (Table 4.9). With sustained high oil prices and “technology optimism”, land-based 
production can improve as once-uneconomic wells are returned to production. Indeed, like the Tax 
Incentive Act of 1976, which was intended to encourage the application of secondary and tertiary 
recovery methods in land-based production of crude oil, new technologies are expected to be utilised to 
extract oil from land-based wells that were once considered uneconomic. But limited oil resources and 
expensive technologies could cause the “Peak Oil” effect, i.e. when the maximum rate of petroleum 
extraction is reached, after which the rate of production enters terminal decline.

Older techniques were applied successfully in some of the old land-based oil areas such as Penal and 
Point Fortin. Unfortunately information is not available on the number of land-based wells that can be

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 90
text:
63 
 
 
The utilisation of natural gas is likely to increase as more gas-based companies are expected to be 
established in Trinidad’s south-west over the short term. Some of these companies will use the natural 
gas as feedstock in their production processes or for the generation of electrical energy. Utilisation of 
natural gas increased from 2,574 mcm in 1999 to 11,789 mcm in 2003 (Ministry of Finance 2004). 
LNG consumption increased from 4,397 mcm in Fiscal Year 1999/2000 to 11,789 mcm in Fiscal 
Year 2002/03. By the first nine months of Fiscal Years 2003/04, consumption had recorded 10,759 mcm. 
Additionally vented natural gas decreased over the period 1999 to 2004 from 930 mcm to 271 mcm. 
While there was fluctuation over the period, there was a discernable downward trend especially from 
Fiscal Year 2001/2002 when 1,017 mcm was vented. 
The demand for natural gas is growing for both industrial and domestic uses. The proposed 
construction of five industrial estates eTecK and three by the NEC as well as expansion of Point Lisas 
Industrial Estate will require tremendous electricity which is likely to be fuelled by natural gas. 
Additionally, the new industries proposed by NEC are described as gas-based industries and are 
expected to utilise natural gas as feedstock in the production processes (Table 4.10).

4.3.4 Response 
Government Initiatives 
With an upturn in economic activities beginning in the late 1990s, the demand for construction 
material increased and demand soon outstripped local supply, according to representatives of the quarry 
industry. By 2004, Government allowed the importation of aggregate from the Dominican Republic and 
Nova Scotia in Canada and this trend is likely to continue as the Government’s construction programme 
accelerates over the next few years. 
The Draft Quarry Policy which was prepared in 2005 identified some of the weaknesses in the

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 91
text:
64 
 
 
existing sub-sector, including the absence of regulations to grant quarry licences, ambiguities in 
application of the procedures for assigning and terminating licences, absence of enforcement measures 
and favouring of large scale operators over small operators, among others (MEEI 2005). This Draft 
Quarry Policy is still under review. 
Based on the Draft Quarry Policy, current operators (i.e. those operating during the period January 
2001 to December 31, 2005), were being offered one year leases during which time they are expected to 
meet new requirements for the operations of quarries. The new requirements include among others, 
submission of an approved survey plan of the licensed area, payment of all outstanding royalties, a mine 
design plan, a restoration plan, an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) report, a CEC and an 
Environmental Management Plan. 
A Bill to amend the State Lands Act was submitted to Parliament to prevent illegal mining on State 
lands. When it becomes law, the State would be in a position to deal effectively with illegal quarrying on 
State lands with sanctions including the closure of illegal quarries and prosecution of illegal operators. 
Private Sector Initiatives 
Based on the inability of the local industry to supply aggregate to the country, several contractors 
are currently importing aggregate. However, the total quantity of aggregate being imported is not currently 
known. 
Many of the major oil companies operating in Trinidad and Tobago have Health, Safety and the 
Environment (HSE) programmes which are intended, among others, to protect employee health and 
safety, as well as environmental health; reduce hazards and risks to employees and the natural 
environment: and comply with relevant legislation. 
4.4 Agriculture 
4.4.1 Description 
Agriculture has been practiced in Trinidad and Tobago over many centuries by the native inhabitants, 
by slave labour on European-owned plantations and subsequently by indentured labour and free workers for 
private companies in which ownership shifted from foreign to State-owned companies. As a result, both the 
type of produce and purpose of production were altered. Shifting cultivation of indigenous crops such as 
manioc (cassava) and maize on relatively small plots for domestic consumption was supplanted by new 
crops such as sugar cane, cocoa, coconut, coffee, and cotton on large estates for export. However, 
cultivation of local crops for domestic consumption was also continued to ensure a cheap supply of local 
food for the labour force. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Hot peppers, grown 
extensively in Trinidad, 
are now being exported 
with promising prospects

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 92
text:
65 
 
 
Externally-propelled economic forces, labour shortages and local pests plagued the agricultural 
sector. Modifications to crops cultivated ensued with sugar cane cultivation giving way in some areas to 
coconuts, the regeneration of cocoa and coffee, and the cultivation of a wider range of local food crops 
including fruits and vegetables. Thus, several of these crops found niches, as different agricultural crops 
were cultivated in different parts of the islands with varying degrees of success. Sugar cane, coffee, and 
cocoa organised on the basis of large plantations and occupying different geographic locations emerged 
as the dominant export crops by the middle of the 20th century. The north-eastern, eastern and southern 
coasts of Trinidad and south-west Tobago produced coconuts, while the valleys and slopes of the Central 
and Northern Ranges of Trinidad and the slopes of the Main Ridge of Tobago produced cocoa. The 
Caroni, Nariva and Naparima Plains in Trinidad produced sugar cane. 
Local food crops including pulses (legumes), vegetables, root crops and rice were historically 
grown by the providers of labour principally on lands marginal to export crop production. However, in 
Tobago, cultivation of food crops occupied less marginal, if not prime, land as a developed peasantry 
acquired and utilised plantation land for food production. Today, the production of sugar cane, the 
country’s major export crop, has declined considerably, while vegetable production for domestic 
consumption has become a viable industry. 
Agriculture’s share to the nation’s GDP declined over the years, moving from 1.42% in 2000 to 
0.80% in 2004, with minor increases in 2001 and 2002. Table 4.11 below shows the contribution of 
agriculture to GDP, as well as the three major components of agriculture, namely: sugar; export 
agriculture (such as cocoa, coffee and citrus) and domestic agriculture.

According to the 2004 Agricultural Census (CSO 2005c), there were 18,022 agricultural holdings in 
Trinidad and 946 in Tobago, and they contained parcel sizes from as small as less than 0.5 ha to as large 
as over 500 ha. In Tobago, the single largest number of holdings was in the category less than 0.5 ha, 
which accounted for approximately 46% of all holdings. In Trinidad, the single largest number of holdings 
was in the category 2 ha to less than 5 ha, which accounted for approximately 35% of all holdings. 
Holdings of less than 2 ha accounted for approximately 52% of all parcels in Trinidad and about 76% in 
Tobago. Thus the majority of holdings are small. 
The 26,131 total agricultural parcels extend over a land mass of 84,990 ha of land of which 
Trinidad accounted for 24,856 parcels on 82,948 ha. Tobago accounted for 1,275 parcels on 2,042 ha. 
The uses to which these parcels are put were identified in the 2004 Agricultural Census under nine 
categories as indicated in the Table 4.12.

Currently, land used for agriculture is under threat since the best agricultural lands – those in 
agricultural land capability I to III – are principally located in the valleys of the Northern Range and have 
been encroached upon by uses such as housing, business places, road construction and sport fields. 
Areas such as Diego Martin, Maraval, Santa Cruz, St Joseph/Maracas for example are all major 
residential areas. 
4.4.2 Pressure 
Pressure has been brought to bear on the land through agricultural practices which are at variance 
with appropriate land uses and sustainable land management practices. In Trinidad and Tobago, these 
practices have taken forms such as squatting; slash and burn land clearance methods; farming in 
inappropriate areas such as on steep slopes and protected forested areas; increasing use of pesticides 
and fertilisers; loss of agricultural land to other uses such as housing, industry and roads; and 
transformation of a large section of former Caroni lands8 into small parcels for intensive agriculture. Some 
of the impacts from these practices include loss of good agricultural land as well as land degradation.

Agricultural Squatting 
The practice of agricultural squatting is widespread. As recently as 1998, the Government had 
identified the existence of some 255 unauthorised settlements on lands owned by the State or other 
government agencies. South Trinidad had 132 squatter settlements of which 71 were located on lands 
owned either by Caroni (1975) Limited or Palo Seco Agricultural Enterprises Limited. Additionally, 22 
squatter settlements were identified in central Trinidad, 98 in north Trinidad and 3 in Tobago. 
According to the 2004 Agricultural Census, some 8.4% or 7,125 ha of the nation’s agricultural land was 
occupied by squatters as of 2004 (CSO 2005c). Table 4.15 which is abstracted from Table 5-13 of the 2004 
Agricultural Census Report, identifies the number of parcels and the size of the areas of government, State 
agencies and privately owned lands currently occupied by squatters.
