title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 96
text:
69 
 
 
Of the 7,125 ha of land under illegal occupation or agricultural squatting recorded countrywide, 
Trinidad accounted for roughly 6,840 ha or 96% of the land, while Tobago accounted for about 285 ha or 
4%. Squatting on agricultural lands was reported to be one of the main causes of deforestation in Trinidad 
(Kairi Consultants Limited 1999). 
Agricultural squatting for the most part leads to poor land management practices due to insecurity 
of tenure, and often results in deterioration of soil and increasing soil infertility. Moreover, as many 
squatters occupy agricultural lands principally for residences, settlements develop which contain little or 
no infrastructure such as sewage disposal, garbage disposal, and running water. 
A variety of long term and short term crops are cultivated on these agricultural squatters’ plots 
ranging in size from <1 to >100 ha on both State and private lands. However, this practice is not restricted 
to subsistence farmers, since commercial producers are also known to illegally occupy land. 
Poor Agricultural Practices 
Slash and burn land clearance methods and/or shifting cultivation are practised in both Trinidad and 
Tobago. In slash and burn, fire is used to dispose of vegetative materials and over time, this practice 
produces fire climax vegetation. Although there are no data on the number and size of fire climax areas, 
evidence is to be found in the Moriah to Plymouth areas in the Courland watershed in Tobago and several 
watersheds (Diego Martin, Cascade and Caura) in the Northern Range, Trinidad. Additionally, the system of 
shifting cultivation, once sustainable, currently does not allow adequate elapse time for the cultivated area to 
fully recover, and with shorter periods of fallow, degradation of the land increases. This technique is prevalent 
in Trinidad’s Northern and Central Ranges. 
 Most of the farmers who conduct illegal agriculture and who use techniques of slash and burn or 
shifting cultivation practice little or no soil conservation, and most farmers cultivating on sloping soils do 
not practice appropriate soil conservation methods. An example of this is noted in central Trinidad where 
farmers are known to till and place crops up and down the slope as opposed to along the contour. 
Additionally, the cultivation of short crops on slopes in the Northern Range and the Courland River Valley 
generally leaves these soils unprotected for long periods. Failure to employ the correct methods 
increases soil erosion and sediment load in runoff water. Unfortunately current data are not available on 
soil loss or rate of soil erosion by soil types since the last report was prepared more than 30 years ago. 
Some farmers indicated that they utilise fires to burn vegetable matter cleared for cultivation and to 
release ash into the earth as a fertiliser. However, some of these fires, which are poorly controlled, 
frequently result in the burning of much larger tracts of land. This practice often leaves the land exposed 
to the erosive power of rainfall before crops are fully established. 
A study conducted by the Forestry Division in the Maracas Valley between 1984 and 1989 (Faizool 
2002), for example, has shown that land under cultivation is 279 times more prone to erosion than land 
under natural forest cover. The same report found that land under burnt forest plantation is 19 times more 
susceptible to land erosion than land under un-burnt forest plantation. 
Inappropriate Use of Agricultural chemicals 
Agricultural chemicals are used for pest and weed control and soil fertilisation. However, pesticides 
are used pervasively in vegetable and tree crop farming while fertilisers are used heavily in the production 
of sugarcane and vegetables. Data obtained from the Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO) indicate 
that fungicides, herbicides and insecticides imported into the country between 1988 and 2003 were 
valued at TT$52,509,000 (Table 4.16). There has been no significant decrease in use of pesticides over 
the years as vegetable farmers continue to rely heavily on their use. The table below shows the quantity 
of pesticides imported by dollar value for selected years.

A socio-economic survey of vegetable farmers’ practices in Trinidad carried out by the Centre for 
Agricultural Bioscience International (CABI) and the Ministry of Agriculture, Land and Marine Resources 
(MALMR) in 1995, reported excessive and unwarranted pesticide applications (Kairo et. al. 2000). Yet, 
the reports also showed that farmers are aware of the negative health impacts of pesticide use on 
humans and animals. 
The scenario remains the same today, with a wide range of pesticides being used, and the same 
pesticides being used on several crops to control different pests. Pesticide run-off has had negative 
effects on freshwater sources and the coastal environment. Some pesticides are known to persist in the 
soil, reaching toxic levels, which can limit soil productivity and fertility. Paraquat, for example, remains one 
of the most frequently used herbicides in Trinidad and Tobago, despite its property of binding strongly to 
clays and its persistence in some soils for up to 13 years (Kairi Consultants Limited 1999). 
A similar picture is painted for fertiliser use. An average of 6,593 tonnes of fertiliser was consumed 
in Trinidad and Tobago during the period 1993 to 2002 (Table 4.17). Existing data show that fertiliser 
consumption declined over the period 1997 and 2001, and this is most probably due to a considerable 
decline in sugar cane production over the same period. However, fertiliser use in vegetable production 
remains high. 
Farmers often do not apply fertilisers on a timely basis in the quantities required and, therefore, 
over-fertilisation continues to be a problem in vegetable production. Fertiliser use in an agricultural study 
showed that nitrate ions (very mobile) and ammonium ions (less mobile) in nitrogen or mixed fertilisers 
can move to a depth of 45 cm in a sandy loam soil within one week after nitrogen fertiliser application. 
Of the three fertiliser groups (nitrogenous, phosphate and potash) imported over the years, the 
country’s average monthly consumption declined from 6,800 tonnes in 1998 to 3,258 tonnes in 2002 with 
the greatest consumption in nitrogenous fertilizers (Table 4.17).

Loss of Agricultural Land 
Agricultural land is being lost to other uses. Housing, industry and associated recreation and roads 
are being developed on a number of sites that were former Caroni lands. These include 1,375 ha for 
housing, 579 ha for industry and an unknown quantity for roads, stadia and other facilities (Table 4.18). 
Of the approximately 25,472 ha of Caroni land once under agricultural production, approximately 
9,862 ha or 39% have been reallocated to small scale farming on one-ha plots and residential, industrial 
and miscellaneous uses, such as road construction and recreation. While non-agricultural uses 
accounted for upwards of 791 ha or about 3% of land alienated from former Caroni lands, the conversion 
of large blocks of sugar cane land into one-ha parcels for vegetable and food crop farming covers 16 sites 
and extends over an area of 8,270 ha. Moreover, given inadequate levels of monitoring of agricultural 
lands and the fact that there is limited enforcement to restrict use of leased agricultural land for other 
purposes, it can be expected that some of these holdings over time will be converted to non-agricultural 
use. 
4.4.3 Current State and Trends 
Land used in agriculture has declined over the years and this is reflected in the total number of 
agricultural parcels, the land area under cultivation and the number of holders of agricultural parcels. Based 
on the 2004 Agricultural Census the number of agricultural parcels was recorded at 26,131, but this was a 
reduction of some 15,611 parcels or 37.4% from 1982 when the number of parcels recorded was 41,743. 
The land area under agriculture moved from 131,572 ha in 1982 to 85,024 ha in 2004. The land lost to

agriculture over the 22-year period is 46,548 ha or roughly 36%. In 2004, holders of agricultural parcels 
numbered 19,143 but this was a reduction from 1982 when there were 30,566 holders, a decline of 11,423 or 
37.4%. 
In addition, agricultural land that was recently abandoned by Caroni is being returned to 
productive use. Thus, the 1,012 ha of abandoned paddy fields, which were once cultivated by Caroni, 
were returned to production in 2005 through government leases to two private operators, one of which 
is the Caribbean Rice Farmers Association of Trinidad and Tobago. 
4.4.4 Response 
Government’s current policy of transforming some 8,250 ha of former Caroni’s sugar cane lands 
into small parcels of 1 ha size, for production of food crops and vegetables, will increase the number of 
parcel holders by approximately 7,000 at completion of infrastructure and distribution of leases. This may 
act to some extent to reverse the trend identified in the 2004 Agricultural Census. 
Training sessions are organised each year for farmers in the use of agro-chemicals, land preparation and 
fire prevention. So far, data are not readily available on the number and type of training sessions held, or the 
number of farmers trained by the Extension Services of the MALMR as well as the impact of these programmes 
on farming. 
The Youth Apprenticeship Programme for Agriculture is one of the programmes in place at the high 
school level to encourage youth to engage in farming. Information was not available at time of reporting. 
The State Lands Act and its recent amendment are in place to restrict illegal access on 
State land for agricultural and non-agricultural land uses. However, it is not enforced and 
appeared to have had little impact on current agricultural squatting. 
The LSA is engaged in the regularisation of squatters and physical upgrading of their communities. 
Between 2002 and 2005, 6 unauthorised housing communities were upgraded and 4 were in the process 
of upgrading. Additionally another 21 sites were in various stages of pre-construction action.

4.5 Pollution 
4.5.1 Description 
Waste generation from the urban centres coupled with dumping of waste into rivers, wetlands and 
other water bodies, surface water runoff from agricultural areas, erosion and landslides and soil removal 
from de-vegetated areas (particularly from hillsides by water), all contribute to pollution of the land and 
water bodies. For the most part, plastics have become a major packaging/container material, superseding 
paper and glass in some industries, and its presence as a waste product is pervasive. Plastic containers 
of all kinds make up a significant percentage of the solid waste generated in Trinidad and Tobago and 
contribute to pollution of the country’s land, water and marine resources. 
Solid Waste Management and Disposal 
Solid waste generation has escalated in Trinidad and Tobago over the past few decades as a 
result of interacting forces such as population growth and urban expansion, accelerated industrialisation, 
increasing commercialism, unsustainable production and consumption patterns (Vision 2020 Planning 
Committee 2003a). Thus both the type and quantity of the solid waste reaching the disposal sites have 
changed, with greater production and consumption of commercial, industrial and household waste. 
 The tremendous gain made by the Chase-Charlie-Away anti-litter campaign and Nag-Charlie 
anti-litter campaign of the 1980s in raising awareness to the issue of waste management and littering 
has long since been eroded, taking the country full circle to the pre-Charlie days when there was 
significant land degradation resulting from ineffective waste management and disposal practices. 
There are nine solid waste disposal sites in Trinidad and Tobago. The three major public landfill 
sites are located in Trinidad, namely the Beetham, which serves the northwest region, the Guanapo 
which serves Northeast Trinidad, and Forres Park which serves the central and southern regions. Three 
other active disposal sites are organised and controlled by the regional corporations, mainly as a result of 
the absence of legislation governing the establishment and operation of landfills and disposal sites (Pan 
American Health Organisation/World Health Organisation 1998). The THA operates the only disposal site 
on Tobago and it is located at Studley Park. Finally there is one privately operated waste disposal facility 
at Guapo, Point Fortin which receives waste from the Point Fortin Borough and the Municipality of 
Siparia. Table 4.19 details the actual operations undertaken at the respective disposal sites in Trinidad 
and Tobago.

challenge for Trinidad and Tobago

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 103
text:
76 
 
 
It is estimated that an average of 1 kg of waste is generated per capita per day, ranging from 0.55 kg 
per capita per day in the rural areas to 1.75 kg per capita per day for a highly commercialised and 
industrialised area such as Port of Spain (Vision 2020 Planning Committee 2003a). Given a population of 1.3 
million people, the average daily total waste should be in the order of 1.56 million kg or roughly 1,560 tonnes. 
Although the Beetham area had long been used for dumping of solid waste, the landfill facility, 
which began accepting waste in 1970, receives about 721.8 tonnes of waste each day, or little less 
than half of the daily tonnage of municipal solid waste delivered to the public landfills in Trinidad. The 
difference in projected generation of garbage and that reaching the three major landfills is 163 
tonnes per day. 
Thus, the remaining landfills should be receiving the difference. However, there are no data for 
quantity of solid waste being received at the remaining six formal dump sites. Moreover, anecdotal 
evidence suggests that much of the remaining garbage is either not reaching the dump sites or much 
more garbage is being generated daily than previously reported. 
 The quantity of waste entering the Beetham facility has almost doubled in the last 10 years (Table 
4.20). A 2004 Inter-American Development Bank study shows the components of municipality solid waste 
reaching the official landfills in Trinidad (Table 4.21). These figures come out from studies done as early 
as 1995. Undoubtedly the composition of solid waste reaching the landfill would have changed over the 
years, with greater consumption of goods in plastic and expanded polystyrene (Styrotex™) packaging 
and containers. Today more than 50% of the waste reaching the landfill could be recycled9. 
A similar situation is noted at the Forres Park and Guanapo Landfills (Table 4.22). The quantity of 
solid waste entering these facilities has increased over the years with expansion of residential areas and 
increased industrial activities in the regions which these landfills serve. 
A 1989 study of the solid waste generation and disposal situation in Tobago reported that about 15 
tonnes of solid waste had to be disposed of on a daily basis (Akili 2005). Some 18 years later the volume 
of garbage has increased considerably with growth in per capita incomes, altered patterns of 
consumption, and the rapid development of the tourism sector. There are no data which record Tobago’s 
daily per capita generation of waste. Over the years, however, it is noted that the quality and composition 
of the waste have also changed significantly, containing less biodegradable materials. 
Much less information is available for the other disposal sites but what is clear is the lower level 
treatment of the waste received at these sites. No consistent compaction or cover is provided nor is 
venting pursued. These disposal sites operate less as sanitary landfills and more like garbage dumps with 
grave implications for public health. 
Hazardous Waste Management and Disposal 
As one of the most diversified and industrialised economies in the English-speaking Caribbean, 
Trinidad and Tobago has a wide variety of manufacturing industries which include wood preserving and 
wood products, textiles, leather/tanning, printing, plastics, polyvinyl chloride (PVC), chlorine, sulphuric 
acid, battery manufacturing, metal fabrication, electrical machinery and components, metal fabrication 
and shipbuilding/repair. There are 19 industrial estates in the country of which the largest is Point Lisas 
with 85 tenants (Weston 2004). In addition, there are 4 major hospitals, 20 private clinics and 40 medical 
laboratories. Coupled with the ever-increasing vehicle population, the agricultural sector and domestic 
activities, the combined impact is the production of a large amount of hazardous waste. 
 
 
9 Alban Scott, SWMCOL, personal communication 2005

Although the country generates a large amount of potentially hazardous waste, there is no 
dedicated hazardous waste landfill or disposal facility. A large proportion of industrial and hazardous 
wastes generated is disposed of in the municipal solid waste stream or even dumped without any control. 
A Solid Waste Management Company Limited (SWMCOL) Report (SWMCOL 2000) provided a list of the 
main hazardous materials generated. These are noted below:

Even to the casual observer, both the type and quantity of the main hazardous waste have 
increased given the country’s expanding industrial and manufacturing base and increasing consumption 
of medical products some of which contain radioactive material. 
Domestic Wastewater 
Domestic wastewater management continues to be a serious and chronic problem. Poor or non-
existent piped sewerage systems in many rural and urban areas make sewage treatment difficult. Since 
many of the existing systems are operated in an unsanitary manner without proper monitoring, they 
threaten human health, either directly through the contamination of water supplies or indirectly through 
the contamination of food and fodder.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 106
text:
79 
 
 
Effective management strategies for wastewater treatment, including reuse are limited. Moreover, a 
lack of technical capabilities to operate and maintain the existing facilities for sewage treatment makes it 
difficult to control wastewater. 
The septic tank has been an effective treatment method for wastewater in areas occupied by a 
small population. A number of problems, however, have been identified, such as limited control over 
design, lack of sludge removal and poor facilities to dispose of sludge. Its extensive use in heavy 
populated areas makes this a serious challenge. 
4.5.2 Pressures 
Pressures on land and water bodies are as a consequence of ineffective methods of solid and 
liquid waste disposal. These include burning of refuse and dumping into rivers and swamps, 
indiscriminate use of agricultural chemicals, absence of appropriate disposal methods and facilities for 
hazardous waste. 
 
 
 
Expanding urbanisation has concentrated a growing proportion of the population into high rise 
structures and dense subdivisions, and this leads to an increase in solid and liquid waste. However, only 
a fraction of industrial and municipal wastewater is treated before being discharged into the surrounding 
land and water bodies. WASA indicated that 30% of the population is connected to sewerage systems 
while septic tank and pit latrines account for 58 and 10% respectively. Two percent of the population has 
no known means of sewage disposal (Grimes 2005). More than 40% of the national population lives in 
the East-West Corridor alone. There is thus a shortfall in sewerage services to residents in the urban 
areas. 
In 2002 solid waste disposal at the three municipal disposal sites accounted for approximately 427 
tonnes (or 33%) of the 1300 tonnes of projected waste generated. There were no data on the quantities 
disposed in the regional corporation facilities or the THA site. However, the quantum of garbage actually 
reaching the dump sites was estimated at 85% in 1995 by SWMCOL. 
There is a plethora of unofficial and illegal dump sites throughout the country, some 300 or so as 
identified by SWMCOL. In the rural areas, they are to be found at abandoned estate traces on former 
Caroni lands, in accessible areas of forest reserves, in swamps and along river courses. In the urban 
centres and particularly in residential areas with difficult terrain, where streets are narrow and sometimes 
access to homes are by steps, the dumps can be seen at several of the intersections of steps and 
streets, in uncovered drains and vacant and unattended lots. 
 
Garbage dumped near waterways often ends up 
in water courses

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 107
text:
80 
 
 
4.5.3 Current State and Trends 
Solid Waste 
The responsibility for solid waste management and disposal is shared by a number of agencies 
and therein lies part of the problem. SWMCOL was established in 1980, to be the national solid waste 
authority with responsibility for management of the solid waste systems in the country. The regional 
governing bodies are responsible for collection of domestic waste within their individual municipality. 
Moreover, because there is no one institution/agency responsible, there is often duplication of effort in 
some areas and neglect in others. 
For various administrative and technical reasons, the collection and haulage of solid waste in the 
municipalities is an inefficient and ineffective operation. Collection scheduling problems are the main 
difficulties. Residents are often unaware of collection schedule changes or simply ignore scheduled 
pickups, opting to place household waste outside of scheduled pickups. 
In addition, all businesses have a legal responsibility for the waste they produce. However, these 
responsibilities are usually neglected, particularly in the commercial sector. The municipal authorities are 
obliged to dispose of the waste of a commercial enterprise within a limited weight. Beyond this weight, it is 
the responsibility of the enterprise to dispose of its own waste. Yet some businesses do not adhere to this 
arrangement. 
There is little or no regulation of the operations of the country’s landfills. The three public 
landfills, all of which have passed the average 20 year lifespan recommended for operation of a 
landfill, operate at low levels of efficiency and pose health and security risks to persons entering and 
operating in the facilities. Their operations are also hampered by low resources. 
While solid materials, surface run-off and leachate from wastes can have devastating effects on 
marine ecosystems and can contaminate freshwater and groundwater supplies, there is no consistent 
and regular monitoring of these waste facilities. In recent times concerns have been voiced about the 
Guanapo and Beetham facilities as sources of contamination which affect the groundwater and 
freshwater supplies. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Safe handling of wastes at the facilities still presents a challenge since salvagers are known to 
indiscriminately sort and handle waste materials, and at times even unearth buried materials. 
At times, garbage is dumped indiscriminately as 
seen in St Augustine Circular, Tunapuna 
From plastic containers to vehicles, all are 
dumped indiscriminately

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 108
text:
81 
 
 
The population’s attitudes towards waste and waste disposal apparently do not lend to effective waste 
management. It has been argued that the country’s problems associated with poor solid waste management start 
with people’s negative perception and hands-off approach to waste. While there has been talk about recycling, 
the citizenry does not actually place value on waste or proper waste disposal, and there is the general perception 
that waste is somebody else’s business once it leaves the home or business compound. 
The existing legal framework does not comprehensively address the issue of inadequate waste 
management. The problem of environmental pollution caused by solid waste is visibly evident throughout the 
island. In 1995, SWMCOL estimated that about 85% of solid waste was disposed of at the public landfills. It is 
likely that this figure has increased given the level of indiscriminate dumping of garbage and debris throughout 
the country, which obstruct drains and clog rivers, particularly those in the urbanised municipalities along the 
East-West and Southern Corridors. Litter abounds in many secluded public spaces – beaches, parks, etc. – 
creating not only land degradation problems but also obnoxious odours and public health hazards. Illegal 
dumping has become a common practice with over 300 informal reported dump sites throughout Trinidad. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Hazardous Waste 
The 2003 National Hazardous Waste Inventory undertaken by the Caribbean Environmental Health 
Institute (CEHI) in 2005/200610 under contract to the EMA, noted that approximately 11,452.5 tonnes of 
hazardous waste was generated in 2003 by 285 companies, in 27 waste streams as categorised under the 
Basel Convention on Trans-boundary Movement of Hazardous Waste and their Disposal (CEHI 2006). 
Seven waste streams generated over 100,000 kg of waste each in 2003 and this is highlighted in Table 
4.24. The single largest waste stream (categorised as Y9 by Basel) was generated across all industrial sectors 
with the largest generator being the oil and gas sector. Companies engaged in the oil and gas sector and the 
chemical and chemicals product manufacturing sector together generated the largest quantity of hazardous waste 
in 2003, representing 85.5% of all reported hazardous waste in 2003. Clinical waste from medical care in hospitals, 
medical centres and clinics) was the second largest quantity followed by waste from organic compounds (Table 
4.23).

In 2003, 96% (or 10,994.4 tonnes) of the hazardous waste generated was distributed among 189 
companies in Trinidad (Figure 4.3). Only 4%, approximately 458.1 tonnes, was generated in Tobago by 
96 companies (Figure 4.3). It should be noted however that several companies, mainly from the tourism 
sector, did not participate in the inventory. The largest companies which were the major generators of 
hazardous waste displayed greater environmental awareness and had systems in place to treat and 
dispose of hazardous waste in a responsible manner. 
4.5.4 Response 
Little attempt has been made to reduce the amount of waste generated. In 1989, SWMCOL 
established waste recovery systems and markets for recyclable materials, including glass, paper, 
cardboard, metals, used-oil and textiles. The establishment of a recycling industry was meant to reduce 
the amount of waste entering landfills, while at the same contribute to the conservation of resources, 
employment creation and income generation, and to create a source of foreign exchange. However, the 
recycling industry remains largely underdeveloped. Most of the sorting is done at the landfills creating 
health problems for the persons who sort through the garbage to collect recyclable materials.

In recent times, the Government has been considering various options for improving management 
of the country’s waste. One such option is the use of plasma-based systems, which utilise ionised gas at 
a high temperature (as high as 10,000 ºC) to convert waste to a vitrified substance with separation of 
molten metal. The technology converts waste into slag and significantly reduces its volume and weight 
with electrical energy as a by-product. However, this technology requires a high level of expertise and 
energy for proper functioning. 
SWMCOL issued a public call for tenders for a waste disposal system for Trinidad. The 
proposer is expected to address the treatment and disposal of waste including some aspects of 
hazardous waste. This tender is currently being evaluated. An earlier contract for design and 
implementation of a waste collection system is currently underway and the successful bidder of the 
waste disposal system will be expected to work with the existing contractor. Meanwhile, the 
municipal authorities are considering extending the term of contract from one to three years for existing 
contractors engaged in the collection and haulage of garbage.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 110
text:
83 
 
 
In 2005, WASA commissioned its waste water treatment plant in Beetham which is designed to 
treat a maximum of 180 million litres of waste water daily, generated by a population of approximately 
365,000 (Water Technology 2006). The facility is described as environmentally friendly, incorporating “the 
latest in ultra violet technology for disinfection and includes anaerobic digestion and biogas removal 
systems” (Grimes 2005). 
A few years ago In response to a government mandate, WASA took control of the maintenance of 
24 waste water systems originally organised by the NHA and subsequently considered taking 
responsibility for maintenance of 150 private wastewater treatment plants that are either malfunctioning or 
essentially abandoned by the private developers (Grimes 2005). Some attempts were made by WASA to 
provide guidelines for repair and restoration of the treatment systems prior to take over. However, this 
process has not realised the desired results.

Chapter 5: Coasts and Oceans 
 
5.1 Introduction 
As an archipelagic state, Trinidad and Tobago’s claimed marine area covers 75,000 km2 with a 370.4 
km (200 nautical miles) EEZ that extends from its territorial sea baseline. The marine environment is 
influenced heavily by its geographic location on the continental shelf of South America. The islands are located 
in relatively shallow waters, and closeness to the Orinoco River’s mouth provides, among other things, a 
nutrient rich marine environment which supports a large fish population. 
The coastal environment consists of substantial areas of seagrass beds, wetlands and coral reefs, oil 
and gas deposits, ports and piers and coastal protection structures. Water quality is affected by 
sedimentation from the rivers of the South American mainland as well as those from inland Trinidad and 
Tobago; natural seepages from oil and gas wells as well as leaks from abandoned and operational 
pipelines; bilge water and oil leaks from marine vessels and wrecks; coastal works which seek to protect the 
coastline or reclaim land; urbanisation; and industrial effluent discharge. 
Wetlands of Trinidad and Tobago consist principally of freshwater swamp forests, mangrove 
swamps, and freshwater marshes. In all, there are 71 wetland sites covering over 25,000 ha. A detailed 
description of the country’s wetlands is provided in Chapter Six. 
Seagrass (Thalassia testudinum) beds are located in the Gulf of Paria along the north-western 
peninsula (with dense beds located in William’s Bay, St Peter’s Bay and Dhein’s Bay) and in Pointe a 
Pierre. In Tobago, seagrass beds are found in Bon Accord Lagoon, Buccoo Bay, Pigeon Point, La Guira 
Bay, Kilgwyn Bay, Petit Trou Lagoon and King’s Bay. These seagrass communities which provide food 
and safe habitat for marine life are currently extending their coverage, yet they either remain under threat 
or are stressed from a range of activities including coastal development, sewage, and damage from 
boats’ anchors and propellers. 
Algal growth and coral reefs are supported on the hard sea bottom in several places such as the 
Manzanilla Bank (running east to Darien Rock), the Drew Shallows (located between Toco and Tobago), 
the Emerald Shoals and Delaware Bank (located east of Toco) and other areas on the north eastern 
coastline of Tobago (Figure 5.1). However, much of the rest of the nation’s marine inshore sea bottom 
varies from mud to sand with patches of shell debris (Van Andel and Postma 1954). Well developed coral 
reefs are found around Tobago and Salybea, Trinidad. Coral reefs are found in Northwest Trinidad in 
Scotland Bay, Chacachacare and Monos Island. 
Sea surface temperature variations are relatively small in comparison to variations in sea surface salinity 
over seasonal cycles. Sea surface temperature ranges from a dry season average of 26°C, to a wet season 
average of 28°C, whereas average levels of salinity can range from 35 parts per thousand during the dry 
season to less than 15 parts per thousand (in the Gulf of Paria for instance) during the wet season. Salinity 
changes are associated with large volumes of fresh water input from the Orinoco River (Van Andel and Postma 
1954). 
Trinidad and Tobago have 704 km of coastline. For the most part, the north coasts of both 
islands are deeply indented with a relatively large number of bays some of which are inaccessible by 
land because of the very high elevations and steep slopes. 
Trinidad’s west coast comprises unconsolidated sediments between Port of Spain and San 
Fernando resulting in low cliffs punctuated with beaches and swamps. The more resistant materials 
make up the promontories at Brighton, Point Fortin and Cedros Point on the south-west coast. 
On the north coast, the western section exhibits a series of cliffs and headlands rising from the

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 112
text:
85 
 
 
Figure 5.1: Marine Offshore Features of Trinidad and Tobago 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
seashore, interspersed with small beaches. Here, though, the bays and beaches of Maracas and Las 
Cuevas are indentations in the soft rock which have eroded over time. On the north-east side, the 
headlands and beaches are smaller and more numerous. The east coast is characterised by low lying 
areas and the longest stretches of beaches and bays such as Matura, Cocos and Mayaro are separated 
by three headlands at Matura, Manzanilla and Mayaro. 
In Tobago, the southwest coast is made up of a flat limestone platform which is approximately 20 m 
in depth and possibly extends seaward to form the coral reefs. This area contains large expanses of 
beaches, a lagoon, and a coral reef complex that is the largest in Trinidad and Tobago. The north coast, 
east of Plymouth, is characterised by a series of headlands and bays with Castara, Parlatuvier and 
Charlotteville being among the most prominent. On the southeast coast the bays at Speyside and 
Roxborough are the most well-known. 
Protection of Trinidad and Tobago’s coasts and oceans is principally the responsibility of the 
Trinidad and Tobago Coast Guard and the MALMR. The former is charged among other things with 
 
 
 
 
North coast Trinidad 
Englishman’s Bay Tobago

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 113
text:
86 
 
 
protection of the nation’s marine environment. The latter, and specifically its Fisheries Division is 
charged, among others, with managing and conserving the nation’s marine resources and executing 
the various regulations consistent with the Fisheries Act (Chapter 67:51), as well as upholding the 
various regional and international conventions on the marine environment to which the Government of 
Trinidad and Tobago is a party. 
The five issues identified as critical are hydrocarbon mining, marine transportation, fisheries resources 
exploitation, coastal erosion, and coastal development. The first three are located within the nearshore and 
offshore marine environment. The other two remaining issues are associated with land contiguous to the 
coastline and within the land–water interface. Each is discussed below. 
Human activities in these areas affect the coastal and marine environments in specific ways. Figure 
5.2 highlights the coastal/marine environmental hotspots as a result of human activities. Use of the 
marine environment is likely to increase in the future and, given the current level of pollution, it is essential 
that monitoring of resources be systematically pursued to mitigate current problems and prevent or 
reduce potential ones.

5.2 Hydrocarbon Mining 
5.2.1 Description 
The exploitation of marine-based oil and natural gas has increased over the years as indicated in 
Chapter Four. However, both exploration and mining in the marine environment are taking place in 
deeper waters, as well as expanding into areas not previously exploited. 
The east and north coasts of Trinidad are now active mining areas while the north coast of Tobago is 
currently being considered for future exploration activity. Blocks 21, 22 and 23 (a), (b) and (c) off the north and 
north-east coasts of Tobago are some of the deepest waters in the nation’s marine environment in which mining 
operators have turned their attention (Figure 5.3). Water depths range from about 250 m on the southernmost 
section of Block 21 to over 1900 m in Block 23 (b). This is in comparison to the 50 to 60 m depth in Block 2(c) or 
the older fields such as Samaan, Poui and Teak on Trinidad’s east coast11. 
Marine crude reserves were calculated at 10 trillion cubic feet in 1995 as reported by the IMA which 
also noted that there were 14 offshore producing oil and gas fields at that time (IMA 1999). The number of 
lease holders increased in 1998 with the auctioning off of new blocks and additional participation by 
companies such as Petro Canada, BHP Billiton and Talisman. In addition, new gas finds were declared 
by companies such as BHP Billiton. Unfortunately, data on current reserves, size of leases and annual 
production figures by companies are not readily available. Table 5.1 lists the companies that are lease 
holders. Note that Petro Canada was included based on information from a public consultation held in 
Tobago in 2006 that revealed that Petro Canada was an active lease holder in 1999. 
There was a general upward trend in natural gas production over the years 1999 to 2004. Annual 
production for the October 2003 to June 2004 and October 2004 to May 2005 periods recorded 21,714 
and 21,819 mcm, respectively. Liquidified natural gas (LNG) utilisation also increased from 7,618 mcm in

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 116
text:
89 
 
 
5.2.2 Pressure 
The marine environment is under pressure from the exploration and utilisation of its resources with 
pollution, resource depletion and physical destruction being major consequences of this exploitation. 
Pollution from mining of oil and gas and refining land-based facilities (including petrochemicals) adds to 
the stress placed on the environment by destroying its resources or reducing the efficacy of the services 
which the resources provide. 
There are anecdotal reports of frequent oil spills on many parts of Trinidad’s coastlines, particularly in 
the southern half of the Gulf of Paria where offshore hydrocarbon winning activity is more than 75 years old. 
While these spills may be small (and many unreported) the net effect may include significant environmental 
loss. Public consultation notes from several EIA reports completed for oil and gas developments reflect 
several comments made by fishermen about the pollution caused by the by-products of oil and gas 
exploitation (i.e., cuttings, muds and produced water). The cuttings and the muds, especially oil-based muds, 
are often toxic and are responsible for the destruction of living organisms. Data were not readily available on 
the quantum of cuttings or muds used in the exploration and subsequent winning of oil and gas, nor the 
produced water generated during operations. 
Two land-based spills were identified in the Ortoire River and Erin River in 1997 and 1998 (IMA 
1999) respectively. Additionally, the Point a Pierre River has been heavily polluted by surrounding 
industrialisation. 
The 1999 IMA Report on State of the Marine Environment indicated that varying quantities of 
hydrocarbon and heavy metal sediments are found in the waters surrounding the islands. 
Concentrations higher than 200 to 500 nanograms of pollutant per gram of water were found in 
surface water runoff proximate to natural oil seepage, drilling and refining operations in the Gulf of 
Paria. The west coast of Trinidad with its long history of marine exploitation, petrochemical plants, 
agricultural runoff, and urbanisation is the most polluted of the nation’s coasts. The east coast of 
Trinidad is less polluted but exhibits “… evidence of pollution by petroleum hydrocarbons where oil 
exploration and production is taking place” (IMA 1999). 
In the case of Tobago, there is evidence of petroleum hydrocarbon contamination in the 
marine environment and this is assumed to originate from sources such as spills or oil seepage 
from Trinidad’s southeast coast, as well as from marine traffic12. 
5.2.3 Condition and Trends 
Exploration and development of the nation’s marine fields are expected to expand into deeper 
waters on Tobago’s north coast and Trinidad’s east coast as the search for oil and gas intensifies. 
 
 
 
 
12 Fisheries Division, THA, personal communication, 2006. 
Fishing versus industrial use of coastal and 
marine resources, southwest coast, Trinidad

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 117
text:
90 
 
 
The nation’s reliance on oil and gas to fuel its economic development thrust is expected to grow as 
more companies set up operations on the west coast of Trinidad. Approximately five new gas-based 
companies are expected to be developed over the next three to five years to add to the 27 already 
in operation. This is elaborated in Chapter Eight. 
5.2.4 Response 
The Government enacted a number of laws to protect the marine environment and acceded to a 
number of international conventions which focus on the marine environment. The relevant local legislation 
includes the Shipping (Marine Pollution) Bill, No. 2, 2004 (this bill lapsed in September, 2005), the Marine 
Areas (Preservation and Enhancement) Act Chapter 37:02, (Act No. 1 of 1970, amended in 1996) and the 
Territorial Sea Act, Chapter 1:51 (Act No. 38 of 1969, amended in 1986). 
International conventions include the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea 
(UNCLOS), Convention for the Protection and Development of the Marine Environment of the 
Wider Caribbean Region, and the International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from 
Ships (MARPOL). 
The Water Pollution Rules, once implemented, are expected to introduce a stricter measure of 
control on effluent discharge into the environment. It is useful to note that the rules will limit effluent 
discharge at the point of discharge only, with no control over cumulative limits in the marine environment. 
In furtherance of protection of its coasts and inshore waters, the Government has updated its 
National Oil Spill Contingency Plan (developed in 1997 and revised in 2004) and established a bilateral 
Oil Spill Contingency Plan with Venezuela (in 1989). 
5.3 Marine Transportation 
5.3.1 Description 
Marine transportation in the nation’s waters includes the movement of goods and 
passengers, both of which are increasing. Figure 5.4 highlights the main shipping routes in the 
Gulf of Paria. It is estimated that there are 10,000 commercial vessels moving through Trinidad 
and Tobago's waters annually13. Ships from 12 shipping lines currently call at the Port of Point 
Lisas and they originate mostly from Europe (Rotterdam, Antwerp and Le Havre) and North 
America (Houston, Miami, New Orleans) 14. 
Special marine goods vessels such as oil and liquefied gas tankers, cement barges and container 
ships, move to and from the various ports on the west coast of Trinidad and through the Gulf of Paria on a 
daily basis. Information was not readily available on ship movements by ports and quantities of goods 
being imported and exported monthly or annually.

The Port of Port of Spain ferry service operates two boats which provide transportation 
between Trinidad and Tobago daily, with exceptions at vacation times when additional services are 
offered. There are also a number of privately-owned vessels operating within the oil and gas 
industry ferrying workers and material from shore bases at Galeota, Point Fortin and Chaguaramas 
to and from platforms distributed throughout the nation’s waters. Data were not available on the 
 
 
 
 
 
Port of Port of Spain 
There is a major international shipping lane off 
the east coast of Trinidad

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 119
text:
92 
 
 
number of vessels currently in use, and the companies engaged in the provision of these services. 
Additionally there are several pleasure crafts moored at Chaguaramas that are used for outings. 
There is a fleet of boats currently plying the Caribbean Region engaging in inter-island trade. This 
trade remains largely unregulated, except for some instances where there are Immigration and 
Customs controls. The number of boats which call at the Caribbean Community (CARICOM) Jetty at 
the Port of Spain Port was not available. 
Both cruise ships and yacht arrivals increased over the period 2004 to 2005 for Trinidad and 
Tobago. The number of cruise ship arrivals increased from 72 in the 2004/2005 period to 89 in the 
2005/2006 period with the majority (55) berthing in Tobago in the 2005/2006 period. 
5.3.2 Pressure 
The marine transportation sector is a source of oil leaks, discharge of ballast water and dumping of 
waste into the environment. While there has been no major dislocation of shipping routes into and out of 
the nation’s ports, vessel movements in some parts of the Gulf of Paria such as Chaguaramas are 
becoming more chaotic as pleasure crafts and supply boats vie for space for manoeuvring and mooring. 
There is an increase in the number of ship movements both within the Gulf of Paria and around the 
two islands, and ship pilots have expressed concern about the absence of a ship movement monitoring 
system15. Unfortunately, data on ship movement by type, draft, cargo, etc are not readily available. 
Discharge of bilge water has been known to be one source of introduction of non-native species of 
flora and fauna into local marine environments, and some of these foreign species pose a threat to 
ecosystems and the survival of native species. 
Marine traffic generated by the oil and gas industry is often responsible for damaging fishing gear 
especially on the south and east coasts of Trinidad. The marine traffic scenario is one of an increase in 
the numbers and types of vessels using the marine waters around Trinidad and Tobago. Associated with 
these increases is the potential for accidents, and particularly collision of oil and gas tankers. The 
problem may be of greater importance within the Gulf of Paria where there are tremendous ship 
movements and no modern marine traffic control mechanisms as mentioned earlier. 
5.3.3 Condition and Trends 
Marine transportation is likely to increase not only within the Gulf of Paria but around the nation’s 
coastal waters with expansion in exploration and development of rigs and platforms. Moreover, traffic in 
the Gulf of Paria is expected sometime in the future to incorporate water taxis operating from San 
Fernando to Port of Spain as an alternative to road transport. 
Pollution from a growing fleet of vessels is also likely to increase. No data were available on vessel 
strength by fleet and port of call. These data will need to be systematically collected. 
5.3.4 Response 
Apart from the local legislation already cited, the Government has prepared a plan for 
modernising ports and port activities in Trinidad and Tobago. This draft Port Plan is currently 
being studied by stakeholders. 
 
 
 
 
15 Chief Pilot, Pilotage Authority of Trinidad and Tobago, personal communication, 2006.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 120
text:
93 
 
 
5.4 Fisheries Resource Exploitation 
5.4.1 Description 
For centuries, the fisheries resources in Trinidad and Tobago’s waters have supported fisher folk. 
Almost all coastal communities were once fishing villages. This has changed over the years because of a 
reduction in the number of coastal residents engaged in fishing, increasing hazard due to piracy in the 
nation’s waters, a decrease in the number of traditional moorings, destruction of some old fishing grounds 
and theft of boat engines. However, based on 1998 data, the FAO indicated that there were a total of 100 
landing sites across the two islands, of which 80 were in Trinidad and 20 in Tobago. However, only about 42 
(12 in Tobago and 30 in Trinidad) were equipped with landing areas, storage rooms and other facilities 
(http://www.fao.org/fi/fep/en/TTO/profile.htm). 
The nation’s fisher folk are mostly artisanal. However, they are generally an aging group with a 
significant number of part time workers. In 1998 they owned and/or operated an estimated 1,417 fishing 
vessels of which 1,216 were registered in Trinidad and 255 registered in Tobago. These fishermen 
operating fishing vessels which spanned four size categories, namely under 9 metres, 9 to 12 metres, 12 
to 15 metres and over 15 metres, are responsible for approximately 80% of the national annual catch 
(http://www.fao.org/fi/fep/en/TTO/profile.htm). 
According to the FAO, in 2000 approximately 6,700 people were employed in the fishing industry, 
including aquaculture but approximately 37% (2,500) were part time fishermen. Additionally, the fisheries 
sub-sector employed 10% of the labour force engaged in agriculture and contributed exports to the 
approximate value of TT$14 million (http://www.fao.org/fi/fep/en/TTO/profile.htm). 
The IMA identified 19 main fish landing sites in Trinidad and three in Tobago and of these, the single 
largest number of sites were located on the north coast of Trinidad (Table 5.2 and Figure 5.5). It is worth 
noting that fishing villages like Grand Chemin and Orange Valley in Trinidad are also consistent producers 
of large catches over the years. Although Tobago has a large number of fishing villages, only three main 
landing sites were listed as shown in Table 5.2 and Figure 5.5. However, villages like Castara, Charlotteville 
and Speyside are particularly active fishing villages supporting large fleets and conducting fish sales right on 
their beaches.

5.4.2 Pressure 
The fishery resources are said to be over exploited in the inshore areas but under exploited 
offshore. Additionally, pollution in the near shore waters is taking a toll on habitats such as coastal 
wetlands, seagrass beds and reefs, which provide protection and food for juvenile organisms. Table 5.5 
provides an overview of the state of some selected species of fish and shellfish. 
Depleting resources are also a consequence of overfishing by fishermen in the inshore waters and 
illegal fishing in the offshore waters, along with problems such as land based pollution from sewage and 
agricultural chemicals. Additionally, physical destruction of habitats such as seagrass and coral reefs is

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 124
text:
97 
 
 
 
 
caused by boat propellers and anchors as well as mechanical damage from visitors walking on the coral 
reefs. 
In a recent survey of fishing practices on the south coast of Trinidad (IMA 1999), fisherfolk generally 
indicated that there was a reduction of fish stock in the Columbus Channel and suggested that one of the 
reasons for this was the destructive practice of members of their fraternity who leave sections of mono filament 
nets in the water and on reefs. This practice causes fish to be ensnared and killed long after the sections of nets 
have been discarded because of damages sustained from marine vessels. 
The marine environment is a multiple use zone providing a range of services and products to different 
users. Non-complementary uses are engendering increasing conflicts among fishermen, recreational visitors, 
operators of marine transport vessels and oil and gas miners. These conflicts will need to be mediated 
otherwise the marine environment will become increasingly difficult for fishermen and recreational users. 
5.4.3 Condition and Trends 
According to the IMA, the nation had an annual average fish catch of between 7,000 and 10,000 
tonnes (IMA 1999). However, based on estimates of total landings by fleet by the Fisheries Division, 
total commercial fish landings ranged from 12,380 tonnes in 1996 to 17,966 tonnes in 2002. Table 5.6 
shows a breakdown by type of fishing fleet for the five year period 2000 to 2004. It should be noted 
though that the data on fish catch provided by the Fisheries Division are essentially estimates for the 
artisanal, industrial, and semi-industrial fleets in Trinidad, and for Tobago. In the case of Tobago, the 
Division noted that a crude estimate for 1991 was used. Nonetheless, the FAO in its 1998 Country Profile 
report for Trinidad and Tobago noted that the data for the artisanal fishermen were generally reliable. 
The information and data on landings adequately cover the artisanal and inshore fisheries, and can 
be considered as being relatively dependable. However, it is somewhat more difficult to obtain information 
and data for the industrial and semi-industrial fleet, since these operations do not currently allow for easy 
monitoring. In addition, there is some reluctance by operators to divulge the required information. A 
similar lack of information makes an assessment of the state of inland/swamp fisheries for crabs, oysters 
and cascadura impossible. 
5.4.4 Response 
The Central Government provides a range of subsidies to the fisheries industry. They include 
subsidies on fuels for fishing vessels and for purchase of new (multi-purpose offshore) and replacement 
of (inshore) old vessels as well as exemptions of duty and tax on engines and spare parts, boats and 
navigation aids. The Fisheries Division carries out its functions as best as it can, but is limited in financial and 
material resources.

The Municipal Government has provided fish markets in several communities in Trinidad and is 
responsible for their maintenance. They are located at Otaheite, Orange Valley, Claxton Bay and Port of Spain. 
5.5 Coastal Erosion 
5.5.1 Description 
The impact of coastal erosion on Trinidad and Tobago’s coastline varies from location to location and with 
spatial changes in geomorphology and exposure to open sea oceanographic conditions. In some areas the 
impacts are easily visible and threaten important infrastructure and property, for example: along the south Western 
coastline of Trinidad at Guapo and Irois Bays; along the eastern coastline of Trinidad at Manzanilla Bay and 
Mayaro Bay; and along parts of the southern coast of Tobago at Rockley Bay. In some instances coastal erosion 
may be part of larger geomorphological changes associated with seasonal high energy conditions (for example at 
Mayaro and Manzanilla). At other locations the coastal change is more abrupt and related to the passage of 
storms and hurricanes (for example on the southern coastline of Tobago). In yet other instances, the phenomena 
are more complex and encouraged by poor or poorly managed coastal construction activity and practices. In 
recent times coastal development activities, including ports, land reclamation and marine dredging along Trinidad’s 
south western peninsula have generated significant concern in the face of perceived exacerbation of coastal 
erosion by coastal residents. 
At any rate, systematic monitoring of the coastline on a national scale is restricted to one limited 
programme by the IMA and other local efforts that are parts of short term projects to support 
environmental impact studies. 
The reality of Trinidad and Tobago is that it continues to rely heavily on coastal construction as part of 
its larger development scheme. The Gulf of Paria in particular remains attractive to port construction and port 
operations as well as significant coastal engineering works to support Trinidad’s strong industrial position. 
While the challenges of managing the risks associated with high energy storm events will always be difficult 
to mitigate, the threat of coastal erosion as a result of poor coastal development is only likely to add to the 
problem of erosion.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 126
text:
99 
 
 
5.5.2 Pressure 
The pressures associated with coastal erosion relate to the loss of property, resource use and 
infrastructure. A useful example of the latter is that observed along the Manzanilla Bay in Trinidad where 
the single coastal road between two towns and connecting several villages, came within immediate threat 
of damage through coastal erosion. The threat was easily observed by passers-by, but remained 
unattended for several years. Similar situations have occurred near Point Fortin and Cedros where, not 
 
 
 
 
 
 
only is infrastructure threatened but so too property including crops and other agricultural supported 
chattel such as machinery and equipment, livestock and/or commodities. Coastal erosion, in some areas, 
has also impacted the use of beaches whether as sea-bathing places (recreational use) or for the 
mooring and landing of small local fishing vessels and their catch (commercial use). To date such loss is 
restricted to Trinidad’s south western peninsula. 
5.5.3 Condition and Trends 
Expanding the land seaward is likely to continue as demand is escalating all types of coastal 
development. These include commercial, residential and industrial development projects. Of these, 
the many high rise luxury apartments stand out. These new coastal developments are expected to 
join the existing ones such as Westmoorings, Gulf View, MovieTowne and Gulf City. Upgrading or 
reconstruction of the main transit through the area, i.e. the Western Main Road from Cocorite to 
Chaguaramas, is expected to be executed soon. 
The expanding demand for more port facilities at Port Point Lisas and the new demand at Union 
Village will continue to encourage reclamation works or pier construction. Both are likely to impact the 
marine nearshore but in different ways. Moreover, the Government’s programme of locating gas-based 
industries along the west coast of Trinidad will require greater quantities of cooling water which will have 
the potential to induce change in the nearshore marine environment. 
5.5.4 Response 
Response to coastal erosion can be thought of as relating to three categories: the preparation and 
institution of regulations; the State’s participation in development projects; and the establishment of 
monitoring schemes. The present situation in Trinidad and Tobago is that regulation is either non-existent 
Erosion is extensive along the southwest coast, 
Pointe-a-Pierre, south Trinidad 
Lady Hailes Avenue, San Fernando

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 127
text:
100 
 
 
or weak except for requirements established through the development planning process and the 
Environmental permitting process. Insofar as development projects are concerned, the State is engaged 
in the erection of coastal defence structures in communities such as Guayaguayare, (where 
approximately 100 m of coastal defence were constructed) and Cap de Ville. 
These efforts are supplemented by the coastal defence projects of private property owners. The 
Ministry of Works, the Government department responsible for the erection of coastal defence structures 
notes that these small projects by private owners are often inadequate both in terms of design and extent. 
Monitoring remains at the limited project level identified earlier with no published attempts to design or 
implement any new national coastal monitoring scheme. 
 
 
 
5.6 Coastal Development 
5.6.1 Description 
Like many developing island states, where land resources and difficult topography are 
significant factors, coastal development levels are high on those parts of the coastline that lie on the 
leeward side of the island. On the island of Trinidad, the western side adjacent to the Gulf of Paria has 
undergone such transformations. On Tobago, the leeward side is the northern side adjacent to the 
Caribbean Sea. Development on Trinidad for traditional reasons is significantly higher because of 
increased opportunities, the presence of a well-developed oil and gas industry and other resources. 
The coastal development form on the West Coast is varied and includes recreational, commercial, 
industrial, residential, and open use. Coastal development on the other coastlines is significantly less, 
due to restriction imposed by lack of access and steep or difficult topography. 
In Tobago recent coastal development is concentrated on the southwestern coast and comprises 
mainly small hotels and tourism facilities. 
5.6.2 Pressure 
Over the last few years, high income residential and industrial use has been at the forefront of 
coastal development. The Port of Spain to Chaguaramas coastline is particularly subject to high levels of 
commercial and high income residential developments that not only reshape the local coastline but may 
be forcing changes to coastal circulation, coastal water quality and nearby drainage. Surveys conducted 
in 200416 indicate that on this 25 km stretch of coastline only 14% is in use as open space while 
approximately 40% is currently used for industrial or commercial use. High income residential use of the 
 
16 Archival data held by CANE Associates 
Coastal defence works, a new addition to the 
landscape of southeast Trinidad

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 128
text:
101 
 
 
coastline accounts for up to 20%. In the same survey, an examination of coastal development projects 
completed during the last 25 years indicates that 55% of the projects undertaken in coastal areas along 
Trinidad’s west coast are of an industrial or commercial nature. 
The size of coastal wetlands along the west coast has decreased over the last 25 years but the 
wetland communities of Caroni and Oropouche have remained in place even though intense physical 
coastal development is within closer proximity. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
5.6.3 Condition and Trends 
The trend in coastal development in Trinidad and Tobago is likely to remain dictated by existing 
conditions: industrialisation on Trinidad’s west coast and tourism on Tobago south west coast. In Trinidad 
the development of new ports and harbours with the attendant engineering works are required to support 
the growing energy industry; and in Tobago small scale tourism is being encouraged by State agencies. 
This tourism form includes a dependence on beaches and water sports as an attraction. 
5.6.4 Response 
 The main administrative approach to physical development remains the development 
planning approval process and the CEC process. Both these processes include the conduct of 
EIAs and other studies to ensure completeness and are supported by various rules and guideline 
set out through legislation or regulation. The coast and coastline remain within the larger 
responsibility of physical development. There are no known plans or policies being considered 
specifically for coastal development.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 129
text:
102 
 
 
Chapter 6: Inland Waters 
 
6.1 Overview 
6.1.1 The Rivers 
Trinidad and Tobago have a myriad of rivers and swamps which have been identified and mapped. 
Less well known are the lakes, ponds, and reservoirs and water impoundment areas. Most of the inland 
waters are located in Trinidad and the river systems are defined by the country’s topography. The 
Northern Range which runs from the north western tip of Trinidad due east across to Toco in the north 
east contains two characteristically different river systems. 
The natural drainage channels on the northern side of the Northern Range that discharge into the 
Caribbean Sea are short, steep, narrow, gorge-like valleys. However, the Northern Range’s southern and 
landward slopes are less steep and many of the large rivers in Trinidad have their sources on the 
southern slopes. The Santa Cruz/San Juan, Maracas/St Joseph, Caura/Tacarigua, Arouca, Oropuna, 
Guanapo, El Mamo, Aripo, Maraval, St Anns/Port of Spain, Diego Martin and Chaguaramas Rivers 
originate on the southern slopes of the Northern Range. 
With the exception of the last four rivers, the other south-draining rivers mentioned above join 
together to form the Caroni River which extends for 40 km and empties into the Gulf of Paria. The main 
drainage channel on the east coast is the North Oropouche River which receives, as tributaries from the 
Northern Range, the Rio Grande, Melajo and Guaico Rivers. 
The Central Range runs diagonally from northeast to southwest of the island and consists mainly of 
hill-ridges radiating from rugged limestone peaks. River systems originating in this range flow northward 
to both west and east and southward to both west and east. The main rivers with their source on the 
northern side of the Central Range are: the Arena, Talparo, Tumpuna and Cumuto Rivers, which are 
tributaries of the Caroni River; and the Cunapa and Sangre Grande Rivers, which are tributaries of the 
North Oropouche River. The L’Ebranche River empties on the east coast and the Couva River on the 
west coast. The main rivers which arise on the southern flanks are the Guaracara and Poole Rivers, 
together with a series of smaller rivers which drain into the Nariva Swamp. 
Like the Northern Range, the Southern Range runs east to west along the southern section of the 
island but is less defined along the eastern and western segments of its length in Guayaguayare and the 
 
 
Couva River, as seen at the Point Lisas 
Industrial Estate 
The Argyle River in Northeast Tobago is a 
popular tourist attraction

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 130
text:
103 
 
 
Cedros Peninsula respectively. This Range is drained on the eastern end by the Pilote River, in the centre 
by the Moriquite/Moruga River and on the west by the Erin River. 
In the case of Tobago, the Main Ridge, an area of highland running for about two-thirds of the 
length of the island in a south-westerly to north-easterly direction, is the source for the island’s main river 
systems. Five of these rivers drain to the north, namely: Bloody Bay, Castara, Englishman’s Bay, 
Parlatuvier and Courland Rivers. Draining south into the Atlantic Ocean are the Richmond, Goldsborough 
and Hillsborough Rivers. 
6.1.2 The Basins 
The two basins known as the Northern and Southern Basins are located between the three 
mountain ranges. The Northern Basin extends from the foothills of the Northern Range to those of the 
Central Range and consists of: the floodplains and alluvial flats of the Caroni and North Oropouche River 
systems; the Northern Terraces (which lies north of the Caroni and North Oropouche rivers and east of 
Arouca); and the Las Lomas Peneplain (which occupies the Manzanilla District in the southeast of the 
region). 
The Southern Basin consists of the Naparima Peneplain. The landscape of this basin is gently 
rolling, reaching its highest point in the San Fernando Hills. The peneplain is dissected by numerous 
small streams which are dry in the dry season and have high runoff rates in the wet season. The main 
drainage systems are the Poole, Guaracara and South Oropouche Rivers. 
The flatlands of Tobago occupy one third of the island and are located in the south west. They 
consist of a flat coral limestone platform that extends seaward to form the offshore coral reefs. 
6.1.3 The Wetlands 
There are several significant wetlands in Trinidad and Tobago (Figure 6.1). The largest and most 
diverse wetland in Trinidad is the Nariva Swamp which is a freshwater swamp located on the southern 
section of the east coast. This wetland is a complex of freshwater marsh, freshwater swamp forest, palm 
forest and an eastern fringe of mangrove forest. Because of its ecological importance, Nariva Swamp was 
declared a Ramsar site in 1992. The Ramsar area, site No. 577, which covers approximately 11,343 ha, 
supports a rich fauna of many threatened species, including the globally threatened manatee Trichechus 
manatus. The site is very important for several species in Trinidad and Tobago, and is critical for the 
maintenance of the biological diversity in the region. Although it has long been an area of small scale 
mixed farming activity and artisanal fishing, in the recent past, it was subjected to tremendous pressure 
from large-scale intensive rice farming which has since been brought under control. 
The North Manzanilla and the Manzanilla Windbelt are both systems of estuarine mangrove also 
found on the east coast and together occupy several hectares. In addition, the North Oropouche Swamp 
occupies a small area at the mouth of the North Oropouche River. On the north coast of Trinidad, the 
largest wetland (approximately 25 ha) is the freshwater swamp forest found at Maracas Bay. 
Mangrove swamps are also dominant on Trinidad’s south coast. The largest of these is Los 
Blanquizales which consists of approximately 840 ha. In general, wetlands along this coast have been 
only slightly modified by human activity. On the west coast the main swamps are: the Caroni in the north; 
the Couva River/Carli Bay in the central; the South Oropouche and Roussillac in the south; and the 
Cedros in the southwest. 
Caroni Swamp, which is the largest mangrove swamp in Trinidad and Tobago, consists of 8,398 ha 
(www.ramsar.org/cda/en/ramsar-pubs-annolist-annotated-ramsar-15817/main/ramsar/1-30-
168%5E15817_4000_0) and is an important critical habitat for a number of bird species, including the 
scarlet ibis (Eudocimus ruber), Trinidad’s national bird. Urban development and industrialisation have

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 132
text:
105 
 
 
which is approximately 1287 ha (www.ramsar.org/cda/en/ramsar-pubs-annolist-annotated-ramsar-
15817/main/ramsar/1-30-168%5E15817_4000_0). This wetland is closely associated with the Buccoo 
Reef and the seagrass community in the Bon Accord Lagoon. It is a critical habitat for various types of 
waterfowl and has been affected greatly by urban and tourism development. 
Inland waters provide sources for the extraction of potable water; ensure appropriate sites for the 
impoundment of surface water runoff; and offer unique and varied ecosystems for the development and 
protection of selected fauna and flora. The extent of inland waters has not yet been properly recorded 
since there appears to be little integrated information on the country’s ponds constructed for fresh water 
fish cultivation or formed from mining activities; weirs, reservoirs and other water impoundment areas for 
the development of potable water; or the traditional recognised swamps and wetlands. 
Given the lack of information on some aspects of the nation’s inland waters, only the issues of 
surface water availability and quantity; groundwater availability and quality; and wetlands health are 
addressed. 
6.2 Surface Water Availability and Quantity 
6.2.1 Description 
Management of the freshwater resources is a critical developmental issue for Trinidad and Tobago. 
Natural availability of the country’s freshwater supply is affected by the occurrence and distribution of 
rainfall; and the occurrence, movement and quality of surface water and groundwater. 
The per capita water availability in Trinidad and Tobago is approximately 2,500 m3 per year 
(GORTT 2003). It is internationally accepted that countries/regions, which have an annual per capita 
water availability level of 1,000 m3 and lower, experience chronic water scarcity, which could hamper 
economic development and human health and well-being (Falkenmark 1997). 
Thus, by international standards, Trinidad and Tobago is not a water scarce country. 
Notwithstanding, surface water availability is strongly influenced by seasonal and spatial variations with 
implications for periodic scarcity and drought-like conditions especially during the dry season. Given an 
anticipated increase in water demand by 2025 (DHV Consultants et al. 1999), water scarcity can become 
an even greater challenge unless corrective action is taken soon. 
About 76% of Trinidad and Tobago’s water supply is derived from surface water sources, while the 
remainder is provided from groundwater sources (MPUE 2003). The major surface water supply sources 
as elaborated in Table 6.1 are the Caroni Arena Reservoir, North Oropouche Intake, Hollis Reservoir, 
Guanapo River, and the Navet Reservoir in Trinidad, and the Hillsborough Reservoir and Courland Intake 
in Tobago. These reservoirs are producing currently at levels just under their natural water availability and 
any excess availability from these catchments should be allocated to satisfy ecological requirements (20% 
of availability) (Vision 2020 Planning Committee 2003a). 
The available surface water in Trinidad is estimated at 3,600 mcm per year (MPUE 2003). For the 
year 2000, water demand was estimated at 336 mcm, 10 times less than the available surface water. Yet, 
in 2000, water supply totaled 300 mcm - an 11% (or 36 mcm) deficit for that year. This means that our 
water resources are not being harvested to their full potential. Such deficit is worsened during severe dry 
seasons when surface water flows are at their lowest, adversely affecting the reliability of raw water 
supply. 
Annual surface water and groundwater availability for Tobago have been calculated at 140 mcm 
and 65 mcm, respectively (Vision 2020 Planning Committee 2003a). The natural water balance suggests 
that there is sufficient water available to satisfy year round demand. However, Tobago continues to

DHV Consultants et al. (1999) reported that the domestic sector is the largest single user of 
water in the country, accounting for approximately 36% of demand; followed by the industrial sector 
accounting for 18%, and irrigated agriculture accounting for only approximately 3% of demand. 
Unaccounted-for water comprises 43% of water demand. Although difficult to quantify, ecological 
demand represents an important user of water (Figure 6.2) since water is necessary to maintain the 
productive ecology of Trinidad and Tobago’s rivers and wetlands. 
6.2.2 Pressures 
 The rivers are under pressure as they receive a range of surface water runoff with a wide array of 
chemicals from agricultural, industrial and sewerage plants, solid waste and urban development. Figure

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 136
text:
109 
 
 
Industrial waste is discharged into waterways primarily from manufacturing and agricultural 
enterprises in the East-West Corridor and the Southern Corridor including the industrial estates of central 
Trinidad. Given the growth in the manufacturing sector, the volume of and number of effluent sources 
have increased. Increased concentrations of heavy metals have been found in several rivers. 
In Tobago, the growth of the tourism sector, which is heavily concentrated in the south–west, 
makes tremendous demand on the water resources and competes with other users, particularly domestic 
users, for an increasingly scarce supply. 
Sewerage treatment plants, some of which are non-functional, are sending raw or almost raw 
sewage into many of the nation’s waterways, and the ubiquitous pit latrine, some of which are constructed 
in inappropriate soils, often leak into nearby waterways. These actions decrease water quality in the 
riverine environment, making them unsafe for recreation and irrigation. 
Urban development is proceeding apace with expansion of housing and other buildings close to or 
on river banks. Even some of the upper reaches of the watersheds are themselves being subjected to 
degradation through deforestation and landslides. Moreover, drainage and river reserves established by 
the Drainage Division of the Ministry of Works are being breached by developers while makeshift bridges 
are placed across some of the rivers to provide access for residents and their vehicles. 
Not only is water quality decreasing as a consequence of pollution, but quantity and availability 
continue to be a challenge. The demand for water is increasing with expansion of the nation’s population 
and their concentrations in urban centres as well as expansion of the industrial base. In the case of 
Tobago, the hotel and resort sector has had tremendous growth and this has been reflected in that 
sector’s increasing demand for potable water. 
Solid waste, including furniture, kitchen and laundry appliances and hulks of vehicles are thrown 
into gullies and rivers clogging drainage channels and changing the rate of flow of some waterways.

Because of its importance as a water source, the Caroni watershed represents one of the key areas 
where detailed studies have taken place. Data from previous reports illustrate the pollution problem in 
northwest Trinidad (Table 6.2). A 31 day study of the Caroni River at the Caroni Arena Water Treatment 
Plant (CAWTP) in 1997 estimated pollutant loads of 5,713 tonnes with a daily average of 184 tonnes. 
Most of the pollutants (88%) were derived from high sediment loads, with organic matter and organic 
contaminants, each accounting for five percent. The Mausica/Manacal and Arima Rivers were heavily 
polluted and were rated fairly poor in water quality, while the Guanapo River was rated as having poor 
water quality (EMA 1997). 
It is estimated that about half the water won by WASA is lost through a combination of leaking 
pipes and fire hydrants, illegal connections, wastage and unregistered use (Water Technology 2006). 
Less than 30% of the nation’s population has an uninterrupted (24 hours/7 days per week) supply and 
approximately 8% has no access to piped water services (Water Technology 2006).

6.2.3 Current State and Trends 
Many of the rivers in the country have lost their vigour as they are being slowly starved of water 
from their sources. They are often filled with sediments which, over time, reduce river widths. 
Moreover, increasing pollution is making the extraction and treatment of raw water costly for WASA 
and will translate into higher costs for users in the future. A report by DHV Consultants et al. (1999) 
estimates a demand for water of 786.66 million litres per day but the current supply is 711.15 million

accounts for roughly 323 million litres per day only receives 313 million litres per day, a 10 million 
litres per day shortfall.

WASA has indicated that it is “…on track to achieve the United Nations Millennium 
Development goals of ensuring environmental sustainability and adequate access to safe drinking 
water and improved sanitation by 2015” (Grimes 2005) and its programme of development includes 
the development of new production wells, rehabilitation and refurbishing of service reservoirs and 
booster stations and installation of new pipes at the rate of 100 km per year. 
A number of complementary projects are being pursued by the Ministry of Agriculture; the Ministry 
of Community Development, Culture and Gender Affairs (MCDCGD); and the MPUE and include: the 
establishment of a national reforestation and watershed rehabilitation programme; a community 
environmental protection and enhancement programme; acquisition of contract services for design and 
implementation of a solid waste collection programme; and the soon to be acquired services for the 
development of a solid waste disposal facility. 
Finally, WASA established a new waste water facility close to the Beetham Landfill and has made 
some gains in water availability with operation of the desalinisation plant at Point Lisas.
