6.2.4 Response 
The EMA promulgated the Water Pollution Rules to guide the quality of effluent discharged from 
industries into public drains, waterways and wetlands. These Rules are being amended and should 
become implementable in 2007. 
A National Hillside Development Policy, which updates and will supersede the Northern Range 
Hillside Development Policy 1988, is being prepared by a specialist Committee under the chair of the 
TCPD to regulate development on hillsides. A draft of this policy has been completed and submitted to 
TCPD for review. 
A programme of reforestation was established to replant areas which have been degraded by 
forest fires, mining, agricultural squatting and encroachment of urban development. The NRWRP is 
intended to replant approximately 33,000 ha of forest over a 10 year period. 
WASA has undertaken a major rehabilitation programme over the last few years to improve the 
output of some of its largest water treatment plants. These include an upgrade of the production capacity 
of the Caroni facility by 25%, expansion of the water impoundment area of the Navet Dam and increase in 
the production capacity of the North Oropouche facility to 20 million gallons per day. WASA is also 
continuing to update some of its operational processes such as making current its consumer database, 
establishing a full metering and leak detection system, and “…upgrading of its control system using 
supporting hydraulic modelling.” 
Water deficit is still experienced in many areas of the country including La Brea, parts of the East-
West Corridor, and in south-west Tobago. 
In many areas of Laventille and San Juan there are constant public complaints about the lack of 
water, sometimes for weeks at a time. In La Brea, residents are opposed to proposed further industrial 
development since it seems likely that such developments will only serve to exacerbate the water shortage 
problem. 
6.3 Groundwater Availability and Quality 
6.3.1 Description 
Trinidad’s aquifers are associated with the variety of gravels and sands located north, central and 
south of the island (EMA 1998b). The major aquifers of Trinidad are the unconfined Northwest Peninsula 
Gravels and the Northern Gravels, and the confined Central Sands and Southern Sands. The minor 
aquifers include the Mayaro Sandstone and Guayaguayare Sandstone. The Northwest Peninsula Gravels 
 
 
Development has progressively crept further uphill on the foothills 
and slopes of the Northern Range, Trinidad

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 140
text:
113 
 
 
extend from Chaguaramas to Port of Spain and are mainly recharged by direct infiltration of rainfall, and, 
to a lesser extent, by streambed infiltration and subsurface flow. Therefore, maintaining a sizeable 
recharge zone is critical. 
The Northern Gravels, which comprise those aquifers east of Port of Spain to just outside Arima 
and southward onto the Caroni Plains, are made up of alluvial deposits and gravel fans along the 
southern foothills of the Northern Range. The rivers in the valleys between Port of Spain and Arima 
recharge the aquifers as they flow over them on their way to the Caroni River. These aquifers are 
connected laterally. 
The Central Sands are located on the southern section of the Northern Basin and extend in 
irregular bands in a diagonal north-easterly direction from Claxton Bay to Cumuto and dip in a north-
westerly direction towards the Gulf of Paria. 
The Southern Sands are multiple-sand aquifers divided into the Erin Formation and Morne L’Enfer 
Formation. While most of the aquifers are exploited, it is the Northern Gravels and Northwest Peninsula 
Gravels that are the top producers. 
Since Tobago is largely composed of igneous and metamorphic rocks, it is not considered rich in 
groundwater resources. However, recent hydro-geological studies in 2000 (Maharaj 2005) have identified the 
presence of substantial water resources in fracture systems of deep bedrock aquifers, which are different 
from the aquifers to which we often refer. 
6.3.2 Pressure 
The aquifers are under pressure from a range of activities, including agricultural chemical usage, 
urban development, vegetation removal and forest fires. The application of agricultural chemicals to pest 
control and crop production has been shown elsewhere to be pervasive. This has generally led to the 
movement of pollutants into the soils and into both surface and ground water sources. 
Urban development on the foothills and slopes of the ranges in Trinidad and Tobago has been 
responsible for the depletion of vegetative cover, significant alteration of the natural drainage system, 
increased waste disposal loads and attendant pollution, increased soil compaction and reduction of the 
area available for water percolation. This has led to reduction in water infiltration rates and therefore the 
recharge of the aquifers is threatened. It also leads to increased surface runoff, especially during periods 
of intense rainfall, leading to a higher incidence of flooding in low lying areas. 
The increase in the development of hard surfaces, such as roads, roofs, concrete aprons and 
parking lots on account of urban development has not been quantified nor even systematically identified. 
From observation of the number and size of green field sites being used in implementation of the 
Government’s housing programme, industrial and commercial building construction and office buildings, 
there has been a phenomenal decrease in the land space available for water percolation and thus aquifer 
recharge. 
While more detailed information is available on forest and bush fires it should be noted that other 
factors contributing to land degradation are dealt with in Chapter Four. Forest and bush fires which also 
aid in de-vegetating large areas of land are a source of stress on the sub-surface water systems. A forest 
fire is an unplanned fire, which burns vegetation on lands under forest cover, including grasslands, which 
were once under forests, or found within forest reserves. The incidence of fires, that is, the number of 
fires and total area burnt, depends on the severity of the dry season and the level of enforcement by 
authorities. Table 6.4 summarises statistics for annual number of fires and total area burnt for the last 17 
years.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 142
text:
115 
 
 
The number of fires and total areas burnt increase during the years of harsh dry seasons 
when drought conditions render both forested and non-forested lands more vulnerable to fires. 
Singh (2001) reported that 41 ha (100 acres) of natural forested land burn annually. However, this 
figure could be closer to 82 to 123 ha (200 to 300 acres), with larger acreage lost during severe dry 
seasons. 
The most severe burning was recorded during the drought of 1987, when it is estimated that 
over 21,000 ha were burnt, with over 4,100 ha estimated to be natural forests. Evidence of this 
damage is still observed in many areas such as Valencia, where many trees were killed and toppled 
by fire and natural regeneration has been quite variable. Recovery also has been set back by 
subsequent fires. Drought-like conditions were again noted in subsequent years with dire 
consequences. 
 The severity of the 2003 fire season resulted in an increase in the number of areas as well as the 
total area burnt (Singh 2003). Yet, when compared with other years of similar rainfall, fire incidence was 
relatively low, most likely on account of better monitoring and enforcement. Large areas were burnt during 
1998, which was recorded globally as an El Niño year. 
Elsewhere, indiscriminate disposal of solid and hazardous waste is threatening the 
environment and specifically the underground water sources since leachate management is not a 
widespread practice. Of the seven well known disposal sites in Trinidad and Tobago, only three 
practice leachate management and such management is absent among unofficial dump sites. The 
extent of toxicity leaching into the soils and the water table is not known and is not routinely 
monitored. 
6.3.3 Current State and Trends 
The country’s groundwater potential is estimated at 611 mcm per year (545 mcm per year for 
Trinidad and 66 mcm for Tobago). Groundwater abstraction for 2000 was recorded as 82 mcm per year 
(77 mcm per year for Trinidad and 4 mcm for Tobago; Table 6.5).

6.3.4 Response 
Ground water provides an important source of the nation’s potable supply and therefore requires 
protection from leachates, creeping development, denuded hillsides in water recharge areas and over 
use. Several of these issues are dealt with elsewhere, including in the section on surface water in this 
chapter as well as chapters on Land and Biodiversity. 
The coastal aquifer in Port of Spain, formed from the Port of Spain Gravels, is approximately 91.4 
m (300 feet) deep and very porous. In this aquifer system, WASA has noted that “… groundwater 
pollution, salt water intrusion and over-pumping continue to be serious concerns” (Water Technology 
2006). 
The reforestation programme is expected to assist in greening the hillsides and thus improve the 
quality of the recharge areas. Implementation of the Water Pollution Rules is expected to discourage the 
indiscriminate discharge of untreated effluents into the nation’s waterways on the one hand, and 
encourage cooperation between environmentally polluting enterprises and the appropriate agencies to 
reduce or eliminate the type and concentration of the effluent to the standards set under the new Water 
Pollution Rules, on the other. 
6.4 Aquatic Ecosystems-Wetland Health 
6.4.1 Description 
Trinidad and Tobago has a large number of wetlands. According to the Wetlands Committee, 
approximately 71 wetland sites have been identified and the inventory is incomplete35. The wetlands 
consist of over 25,000 ha, comprising swamps, lagoons and marshes. Many are located close to or on 
the coasts of both islands. Many wetland sites offer a combination of swamps, lagoons and marshes with 
a range of flora and fauna. 
The IMA identified and mapped 42 wetland sites in Trinidad and 13 in Tobago with a range of sizes 
(Table 6.6). It was found that the wetlands range in type and complexity (IMA 1999). There is the single 
item fringe mangrove in Scotland Bay, the freshwater swamp forest in Maracas, the estuarine mangrove 
in Manzanilla, and the wet pasture in Lucy Vale. The more diverse systems are the freshwater 
marsh/freshwater swamp forest/ estuarine mangrove/palm forest of Nariva, the lagoon fringe/basin 
mangrove/freshwater pond of the Bon Accord Lagoon/Buccoo Bay, and the marshes/mangrove 
swamp/brackish and saline lagoons/tidal mud flats of Caroni.

Most wetlands are located in Trinidad, and the largest proportion of wetlands comprise of those 
that are less than one hectare in size. The two largest wetlands, which extend over 14,600 ha combined, 
are the Caroni and Nariva. Both are internationally well known and along with the Buccoo Reef/Bon 
Accord Lagoon, are protected areas. Their sizes and designation as Ramsar Sites are provided in 
Chapter Seven. 
6.4.2 Pressure 
More than 50% of the nation’s original wetlands have been lost over the years and the current 
stock is under severe pressure from over exploitation36 from a variety of sources. For the most part, 
wetlands are perceived as unimportant and without value. They are therefore seen by many as 
appropriate areas for filling and land reclamation, garbage dumps and agriculture. 
The remaining wetlands are directly impacted by rice farming in places like Fishing Pond, South 
Oropouche and Nariva. Along the west coast of Trinidad, land reclamation and consequential urban 
development (residential, commercial and industrial) have occurred at Westmoorings, Invaders Bay and 
Sea Lots in the north-west; Point Lisas in Couva in central; and Atlantic LNG in Point Fortin in the south. 
Apart from the reduction in the sizes of the wetland areas, there has been pollution of the areas because 
of the industrial and commercial activities associated with the built developments in close proximity to the 
wetlands, as well as from land-based sources which may originate miles away from the wetland. 
Agro-chemicals, animal waste from pig and poultry farms, silt from mining of aggregate, and 
industrial effluent discharged from further up the river basin find their way into the rivers and then into the 
wetlands and particularly the coastal swamps. These pollutants including heavy metals and faecal coliforms, 
degrade the wetland ecosystem and reduce the ability to execute their functions and provide their 
ecosystem services. 
 
 
 
 
36 National Wetland Policy of Trinidad and Tobago: http://www.ramsar.org/cda/en/ramsar-pubs-annolist-national-wetland-
21182/main/ramsar/1-30-168%5E21182_4000_0__

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 145
text:
118 
 
 
6.4.3 Current State and Trends 
Wetlands are in varying stages of degradation and, until recently, were essentially left unmanaged 
and minimally resourced. There is little history of wetlands protection or management to the extent afforded 
to protected areas such as forest reserves and game sanctuaries. Thus wetlands have largely suffered from 
neglect; although some portions within the Caroni and Nariva Swamps were provided legal protection when 
prohibited areas like the game sanctuaries and forest reserves were located within their boundaries. 
6.4.4 Response 
To some extent, the Marine Areas (Preservation and Enhancement) Act; Act 1 of 1970 brought 
protection for the wetlands under its umbrella. Here the Act did not name coastal wetlands but described 
them as “… submarine areas within the Territorial Seas and includes any adjoining land or swamp areas 
which form with certain submarine areas a single ecological entity” (National Wetlands Committee 2002). 
More than two decades later (in 1992), the Government became a party to the Convention on 
Wetlands and signalled its intention to protect and restore wetlands in Trinidad and Tobago for the use of 
current and future generations. 
In 1995 a National Wetlands Committee was established and tasked with the preparation of a 
national wetlands policy. A National Wetlands Plan was prepared and eventually was accepted by the 
Government in 2001 (National Wetlands Committee 2002). 
The Plan identifies six policies which are expected to guide wetlands protection and rehabilitation 
and they are noted as follows: 
The Government of Trinidad and Tobago will: 
1. promote awareness and understanding of the wetland resources in Trinidad and Tobago and 
actively encourage participation of landowners, NGOs and institutions in wetland conservation; 
2. develop exemplary practices in support of wetland conservation and sustainable wetland use when 
it designs and implements government programmes on publicly owned land and waters in keeping 
with the wise use principle as defined under the Convention on Wetlands; 
3. preserve outstanding examples of all wetland types in Trinidad and Tobago by including them in a 
system of national parks and other protected natural areas; 
4. be an active partner in cooperative activities, which promote wetland conservation in Trinidad and 
Tobago and the Caribbean region; 
5. support and promote development of the scientific and technological expertise needed for wetland 
conservation and ensure that this expertise is accessible to planners, managers, regulators and other 
decision-makers; 
6. identify weaknesses in the present institutional and organisational structures relevant to wetland 
conservation and will develop activities for their improvement. 
To date some three sites have been designated Ramsar Sites. Nariva Swamp which was illegally 
used for mechanised rice production is currently being brought back to health after the removal of the rice 
farmers and others. 
Agencies with direct responsibility for oversight for environmental matters relating to wetland 
management are Forestry Division; the Land and Water Development Division in the MALMR; the IMA; and 
the EMA. 
A number of other government agencies addressing wetland health include SWMCOL and the

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 146
text:
119 
 
 
MCDCGD. Elsewhere in this document are additional responses to the issue of aquatic environments. 
Restoration and protection of wetlands is likely to be pursued more vigorously in the future as the ethic of 
environmental protection and wise use of resources are adopted by both corporate and individual 
citizens.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 147
text:
120 
 
 
Chapter 7: Biodiversity 
7.1 Overview 
The fauna and flora of Trinidad and Tobago are a natural extension of the ecology of South 
America. Because of its relatively small size, location and the geological relationship to the mainland, the 
country has an abundance and variety of biological resources, and thus a high species diversity to 
surface area ratio. 
The range of terrestrial ecosystems includes several forest types - evergreen seasonal; semi-
evergreen seasonal; deciduous seasonal; littoral woodlands; lower montane rainforests; seasonal 
montane forests; montane rainforests; elfin woodlands; and swamp forests (including mangrove 
woodlands) - as well as palm swamps, marshes and savannahs (Figure 7.1). 
The marine ecosystems include: the water masses; mud bottom; sandy bottom; rocky shores; 
beaches; seagrass beds; coral reefs; and mudflats. By far, the mud bottoms dominate the near shore 
areas. The marine systems support a range of macro and micro biota including a large array of fish 
species of commercial importance. Many fish species may be found common to more than one type of 
sea bottom. The greatest diversity is associated with coral reef systems. 
One main drawback is that there is no comprehensive listing of many plant and forest species, 
particularly in the case of the Main Ridge, Tobago. Although the review of taxa in Trinidad and Tobago is 
far from complete, at latest count the terrestrial fauna for the archipelago exceeds 1,000 species and the 
flora, 2,500 species (Forestry Division 2005). 
Many marine species are important food species. These include fishes, shrimp, crabs, lobsters, 
and molluscs. Widespread concern continues to be expressed for the decline in numbers of some 
species and potential extinction of others. Manatees (Trichechus manatus), for example, were previously 
known to live in both the South Oropouche and Caroni drainages, but only a small herd remains extant in 
Nariva. 
The ecological status of many plant and animal species is not clear. However, what is clear is the 
need for Trinidad and Tobago to embark on an assessment of the status of its species for more accurate 
categorisation. 
A number of challenges face Trinidad and Tobago in its task to maintain species biodiversity and to 
conserve the country’s wildlife habitats. The major pressures on habitat conservation and protection are 
loss, fragmentation and degradation due mainly to deforestation. This ultimately affects species numbers. 
The issue of biodiversity and climate change is a critical one given recent predictions of rising ambient 
temperatures, changing rainfall patterns, and sea level rise. 
7.2 Ecosystem Conservation and Protection 
Loss, fragmentation and degradation are the major challenges to the protection and conservation 
of ecosystems and this is due to a number of driving forces including deforestation, bush and forest fires, 
pollution and decreasing freshwater quality. Less is known about the true status of some marine habitats. 
Protected areas in Trinidad and Tobago represent more than 25 % of the country’s total land area. 
However, the status of many of these areas remains questionable.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 148

121 
 
 
7.2.1 Pressure 
There are a number of pressures which degrade, fragment and destroy ecosystems in Trinidad and 
Tobago. Among the major pressures are the expansion of human settlement and continued urbanisation, 
forest degradation, the incidence of forest fires, pollution, and the physical alteration of ecosystems. 
Urbanisation is discussed in Chapter Four of this report.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 149
text:
122 
 
 
Forest Degradation and Loss 
Natural forests support a significant proportion of the country’s biodiversity. Removal of forested 
ecosystems reduces and degrades available habitat, feeding areas and other resources which are required 
to sustain the country’s flora and fauna and, thus, its biodiversity. Given the link between natural vegetative 
cover and biodiversity, loss of forest cover can be used as an indicator of habitat quality and loss of 
biodiversity. At present, there are limited data for assessing the loss of individual forest species. 
Most information generated locally on land use change and forest cover is dated, and thus 
questionable given the dynamic nature of the country’s development over the last 10 years and the high 
accessibility of our forests. Nonetheless, every year forest cover is lost to human activity. The EMA’s 
2001/2002 State of the Environment Report, utilising international databases37, reported an annual loss of 
forested area of 0.8 % between 1995 and 2000 (Agard and Gowrie 2003). This figure was again quoted in 
the 2003 International Tropical Timber Council Report (ITTO) on Forestry in Trinidad and Tobago which 
also reported that deforestation accounted for the annual loss of about 2,200 ha of natural forests per 
year, or 0.8 % per year, during the period 1990 to 2000 (ITTO 2003). Though the level of confidence in 
the estimate was low, Trinidad and Tobago is rated high (at seven) on the Environmental Vulnerability 
Index scale, making it highly vulnerable to the loss of natural vegetated area. Table 7.1 summarises the 
state of some of the key forest reserves in Trinidad and Tobago. 
While in the past agricultural production was primarily responsible for the clearing of natural 
vegetation, today forest loss is attributed to over-exploitation, industrial development, quarrying, 
agriculture, logging, squatting, fires and housing development. It is estimated that there are some 25,000 
squatter households on State lands, with equal numbers on private lands (Vision 2020 Planning 
Committee 2003b) and as such, over 15 % of the households in Trinidad and Tobago are squatting on 
State lands, with a significant proportion of these lands once being forested. 
The State Lands (Regularisation of Tenure) Act (No. 25 of 1998) identifies 254 squatter settlements 
on State lands of which approximately 52 % of the total squatter settlements on State lands are located in 
southern Trinidad. Many of these households are located in forest reserves, with the problem being 
prevalent in the Tacarigua Reserve, the Valencia Reserve, and the Long Stretch Reserve in the north and 
in the Southern Watershed and Rochard Douglas Reserve in the south, where both agricultural and 
housing squatting are common. Forest clearance has made way for formal high-density housing 
developments and other forms of residential development, for example, in Mosquito Creek and Icacos. 
Recent forest loss has been recorded with the expansion of the natural gas industry and 
downstream petrochemical industries in southern Trinidad, namely the development of 154 ha (380 acre) 
Union Estate Industrial Park in La Brea; the construction of the Train IV Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) 
plant; and the trans-island pipeline from Guayaguayare to Point Fortin/La Brea. Further losses can be 
expected with the possible development of 809 ha (2000 acre) heavy industrial estate in Cap-de-Ville. 
The construction of roadways and the laying of cross-island pipelines in forested areas in southern 
Trinidad have not only resulted in deforestation but also the dissection of forest habitats, thus increasing 
environmental stress on fragmented and reduced species populations. The 2001/2002 State of the 
Environment Report noted that there are some 13,832 km length of Class One to Four roads in Trinidad 
and 668 km in Tobago, indicating that both islands have the highest score in respect of habitat 
fragmentation38. 
 
 
37 E.g.: Databases from the FAO; the Forest Stewardship Council; UNEP’s Global Resource Information Database; and the Global 
Land Cover Characteristics Database. 
38 Fragmentation of land and habitat - the percentage of natural cover in fragments of less than or equal to 1,000 hectares, 
excluding those that occupy entire islands

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 150
text:
123 
 
 
 
The incidence of annual forest fires, which impacts negatively on wildlife communities and 
ecological processes, represents a major avenue for forest loss and has tested the capacity of the 
Forestry Division to effectively protect and manage the country’s forest resources. Figure 7.2 shows the 
number of forest fires and the area burnt in Trinidad over a 17 year period 1987 to 2003 (Singh 2003). 
Every year, there is an average of 315 recorded forest fires in Trinidad burning an average of 4,083 ha, 
with a substantial number of these fires occurring in degraded forest lands where fire-climax grassland 
vegetation have been established after these persistent annual fires. 
This trend of above average numbers of fires and acreage burnt was sustained in 6 of the 17 years 
reviewed, of which 4 of them were El Niño years. A disturbing feature of these above average years is 
that a relatively high amount of damage is sustained in the natural forests. Most of the fires are 
maliciously set. Though recent focus has been paid to community-based fire prevention strategies, 
Forestry Division cites public indifference to the values of forests and ignorance and disregard for the 
negative impacts of forest fires as key reasons for the persistence of this annual problem. 
In Tobago, the number of fires averaged 200 per year between 2000 and 2004, with few fires 
occurring in Northeast Tobago where the Main Ridge is located39. Instead these annual fires mainly occur 
on the abandoned agricultural estates in the St David and St Patrick Parishes. No data were received on 
the amount of acreages burnt in Tobago. 
Decreasing River Water Quantity and Quality 
Over the years, human activities have impacted on riverine water quantity and quality, changing the 
habitat and the floral and faunal characteristics of rivers, particularly in urban and built up areas. 
Generally, river water flow and levels have been altered considerably with changes in vegetative pattern 
particularly in the upper watershed region. River water flow in the dry season is down to a trickle or 
virtually non-existent in some rivers where flow was observed previously during this time. Recent work 
conducted by WASA revealed that water flow in some rivers in the East West Corridor during the dry 
season is due mainly to discharges from neighbouring treatment plants. 
The quality of river water has declined considerably in many areas particularly in highly populated 
areas, as evident by high levels of five-day biological oxygen demand (BOD5), bacterial content and 
turbidity, and by the presence of chemical pollutants. This is due mainly to effluent discharges from 
industrial sources; inappropriate flood control measures; indiscriminate dumping of solid waste in water 
courses, including the dumping of construction waste; and the presence of high sediment loads in the 
runoff particularly from hillsides and upstream locations. 
Pollution of riverine water affects the ability of rivers to provide productive habitats for terrestrial 
and aquatic species and may have direct harmful or deadly effects on organisms. In-stream problems due 
to pollution are further exacerbated during periods of low flows when the dilution effect is at its lowest. In 
studies conducted by researchers from the UWI it was found that some freshwater fish and shrimp 
migrate during their life cycles. Thus, pollution at any point within the river can affect them. The studies 
show where mountain mullet or freshwater shrimp are no longer found in upstream tributaries of the 
Caroni River as in the past, since their natural migrations are effectively ‘blocked’ by the polluted lower 
river (Alkins-Koo 2005). 
 
 
39 Obtained from the Annual Fire Dataset collected by the DNRE, THA, 2006

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 153
text:
126 
 
 
“Rivers which enter the Gulf are in general contaminated by domestic, agricultural, and industrial 
waste, whereas those which flow from the north coast into the Caribbean Sea are considered to be 
pristine as they drain forested areas or areas which are relatively undeveloped. Water courses on the 
east coast pass through agricultural, forested and in some cases, swamp lands, whereas those on the 
south coast flow through the oil-producing areas. In Tobago, the condition of the water courses in the 
northern portion of the island can be expected to be better than those in the southwest, since the latter 
flow through more developed areas.” 
The 2003 study by the Harbor Branch Oceanographic Institution on behalf of the Buccoo Reef 
Trust (Lapointe 2003) confirmed the suspicion that Tobago’s coral reefs are being adversely affected by 
nutrient over-enrichment. This had triggered ecological changes in the reefs and led to decreased living 
coral cover and to diseased conditions and declining biological diversity (Box 1). This nutrient over-
enrichment is due mainly to sewage pollution and other land-based pollution sources. 
 
Box 1: Impacts of Land-Based Nutrient Pollution on Coral Reefs of Tobago 
(Source: Lapointe 2003) 
 
Taken from the Study on the Impacts of Land-Based Nutrient Pollution on Coral Reefs of Tobago prepared by the Brian E. Lapointe, 2003. 
Impacts of Land-Based Nutrient Pollution on Coral Reefs of Tobago. Prepared by Harbor Branch Oceanographic Institution, Inc. Florida on 
behalf of Buccoo Reef Trust, Tobago 
 
Main Findings: 
 
 
Nutrient over-enrichment of Tobago’s fringing coral reefs, especially Buccoo Reef, from local nutrient sources has triggered 
ecological changes that have decreased living coral cover and biological diversity. 
 
At Buccoo Reef, reduced coral cover correlated significantly with increased cover of macroalgae and the zoanthid Palythoa both 
of which are indicators of nutrient enrichment on Caribbean coral reefs. 
 
Recent encroachment of the seagrasses Thalassia testudinum (turtle grass) and Halodule wrightii (Cuban shoalweed) into the 
sandy sediments of Nylon Pool are symptomatic of nutrient enrichment. 
 
Coral diseases, including white band disease, yellow band disease, and black band disease, occurred at a number of reef sites 
around Tobago. The dominant coral at Culloden Reef -- Montastrea annularis -- was impacted by an outbreak of yellow band 
disease that has apparently developed in just the past few years following increased deforestation and development of its 
watershed. 
 
Concentrations of dissolved inorganic nitrogen (DIN) increased significantly on reef sites around Tobago from the dry season to 
the wet season. The increased DIN concentrations resulted in significant decreases in concentrations of soluble reactive 
phosphorus (SRP). Low DIN:SRP ratios (< 15:1) year-around indicate N-limitation of algal growth in Tobago’s coastal waters. 
 
High values (> 3 o/oo) of 15N/14N in macroalgae from the Buccoo Reef Complex and other fringing reefs off southwestern 
Tobago occurred during both wet and dry seasons and were indicative of land-based sewage N pollution from the upland 
watershed. 
 
The 15N/14N values of macro algae from Black Jack Hole off Little Tobago Island increased from relatively low values (< 3 
o/oo) in the dry season to high values (> 5.0 o/oo) in the wet season, indicating increased dispersion of sewage N during 
periods of peak runoff. 
 
Levels of phytoplankton biomass, measured as chlorophyll a, increased from the dry season to the wet season at reef sites 
around Tobago that had relatively low impacts of sewage enrichment (e.g. Black Jack Hole and Kelliston Drain off Speyside). 
Reefs that were chronically impacted by sewage pollution showed relatively little effect of the wet season runoff on chlorophyll 
a. 
 
Some of the highest coral cover of the study occurred at Black Jack Hole off Little Tobago Island, the site with the lowest annual 
mean concentrations of DIN, chlorophyll a, and 15N/14N in macro algae.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 154
text:
127 
 
 
Apart from impacts due to declining water quality, wetlands, coral reefs and seagrasses have been 
physically destroyed to make way for changes in resource use and other human activities, for example: the 
removal of seagrass mainly for built development; the in-filling and reclamation of mangroves and swamps for 
residential, commercial and industrial purposes; and destruction of coral by reef walking during visitor tours. 
7.2.2 Current State and Trends 
The extent and quality of the country’s terrestrial ecosystems can be used to define changes in 
habitat, and thus give some indication of their biodiversity status. Trinidad and Tobago is endowed with a 
significant amount of natural forested area, the extent of which was first documented in 1946 and shown 
in Table 7.2.

Most of the terrestrial biodiversity of Trinidad and Tobago can be found in forested areas. In 2003, 
it was estimated that some 248,000 ha or approximately 48 % of the total land area in Trinidad and

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 155
text:
128 
 
 
Tobago is still classified as forest land, although large portions have been under severe pressure for 
alternative uses. 
The accuracy of this 2003 figure and the quality of the country’s forest resources in today’s 
context are questionable. The estimations are based on the last forest inventory which was conducted 
over 25 years ago using 1969 aerial photographs. Nonetheless, over the last 20 years, there has been 
considerable forest depletion in some of the country’s reserves including the Victoria-Mayaro Reserve, 
Morne L’Enfer Reserve, Erin Reserve, Long Stretch Reserve, and Valencia Reserve. Given the 
significant level of deforestation none can argue against the need for a new forest inventory which 
would give a more accurate picture of the status of these invaluable resources. 
Freshwater Habitat 
In Trinidad, the main river systems are the Caroni River in the north, the Caparo River in central 
region, the Ortoire River in the southeast, and the Oropouche River in the southwest. In Tobago, the 
Courland River is the main river system, traversing almost two thirds of the island. These freshwater 
systems directly support human activities as well as plant and animal life. Within the last 10 years, a 
number of studies on the freshwater quality of individual systems have been conducted, all of which point 
to the impact human activities are having on the main river systems, and thus give an indirect measure of 
ecological health. However, the status of these systems as freshwater habitats and the life they support 
have not been recently assessed from an ecological point of view. 
Coastal and Marine Habitat 
Table 7.3 provides some basic information about coastal and marine biodiversity in Trinidad and 
Tobago. Coral reefs, wetlands, and seagrass beds are the most important coastal ecosystems in Trinidad 
and Tobago. They provide breeding grounds, hatcheries, nurseries, and protection for hundreds of 
species of finfish and shellfish of ecological and commercial value (IMA 2006), and protect the coastline 
from erosion, flooding, storm surges and tsunamis. These ecosystems are also habitat to some of the 
country’s endangered species. For example, the beaches on the northeast Trinidad coast at Matura, 
Grande Riviere and Fishing Pond; and on the southwest coast of Tobago at Bon Accord and Courland 
Bay provide nesting habitat for the leatherback and other marine turtle species. Little Tobago and Saut 
d’Eau Island provide habitat for nesting migratory and other marine bird species, while the Nariva Swamp 
is habitat to the endangered manatee.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 156
text:
129 
 
 
Although an inventory of the Nation’s wetlands has not been completed, some 71 sites have been 
identified to date, which are thought to contain over 25,000 ha of swamps, marshes and ponds. The Nariva 
Swamp on the east coast and Caroni Swamp on the west coast are the two largest wetlands in Trinidad with 
a combined area of some 15,632 ha. These wetlands support a variety of plant and animal life. 
The coral reef systems are more diverse in Tobago when compared with Trinidad, with the Buccoo 
Reef, Speyside Reef and Man-o-War Reef in Tobago being the most popular and impressive (Figure 7.3). 
Table 7.4 also summaries the state of the country’s coral reefs and seagrass communities. 
 
 
Figure 7.3: Status of Coral Reefs of Trinidad and Tobago 
(Source: www.reefbase.org) 
 
 
7.2.3 Responses 
Protected Areas System 
In an effort to conserve and protect the country’s biodiversity, there are 36 forest reserves, 13 
wildlife sanctuaries, 1 national park, 3 beaches where entry is seasonally restricted (i.e., during the sea 
turtle nesting season) and 1 marine protected area (MPUE 2009). However the proportion of ecosystems 
represented in the various protected areas is not readily available and therefore should be documented. 
 The forest reserves, first established during the 1920s to 1960s under the Forests Act (Chapter 
66:01) were geared primarily at managing the forest resources for production forestry. Howe ver, the 
shift towards sustainable management and conservation of forest resources came with the 
recognition that existing flora and fauna were intertwined in the lives of humans, and the need for 
increased public awareness and participation in the management of the country’s forests. One of 
the first acts in changing public behaviour towards the forests was declaring a moratorium in the 
1990s on timber harvesting on State lands in both Trinidad and Tobago. Nowadays, trees are 
usually harvested as a mechanism for improving forest health, although there are reports of illegal 
logging activities within some of the forest reserves.

According to the 2003 Annual Report of the Forestry Division (Forestry Division 2004), the 36 forest 
reserves in Trinidad and Tobago occupy 131,432 ha of proclaimed forest reserve, and an additional 
11,652 ha of un-proclaimed forest reserves (Table 7.5), thus representing 27.9 % the country’s total land 
area. Some of these forested lands are severely degraded as a result of years of fires, illegal logging, 
quarrying, residential and agricultural squatting, as well as the practice of slash and burn land clearance. 
There are some 56,000 ha of private forests which also serve the function of biodiversity conservation. 
The 13 wildlife sanctuaries declared under the Conservation of Wildlife Act (67:01), occupying 
17,076 ha in 2003, serve as safe havens for certain threatened and vulnerable species as well as the 
protection of their habitats and food supply. In addition, the Environmentally Sensitive Areas (ESA) Rules 
of 2001 were

system of national parks and other protected areas. Originally developed in the 1980s, the plan

Madamas, Maracas, Matura, and Nariva Swamp in Trinidad; and in Tobago, Buccoo Reef and Eastern 
Tobago), cover almost half the proposed protected areas system. Today, the parks and protected areas 
consist of 12 areas of which 4 are natural areas comprising some 6,904 ha. The management objectives 
for these areas are to protect their ecosystems and natural features and to provide opportunities for 
environmental education, research, and interpretation and some forms of recreation. 
Area Reforested 
The regeneration of degraded forest ecosystems is achieved through the reforestation efforts of 
public sector entities. Prominent among the public sector initiatives is the co-management programme, 
the NRWRP, which stands out because of its mandate to replant 13,367 ha of degraded forest lands over 
a 10-year period (Box 2). Its Status Report for 2004/2006 documents the achievements of the programme 
Box 2: The NRWRP 
(Source: NRWRP 2006) 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
In the past, attempts at community-based forest protection and conservation projects by both public and private institutions 
have been small and localised. However in 2003 the large scale, long term, community-based National Reforestation and 
Watershed Management Programme began to provide a number of ecological and social benefits including “an increase in 
the value of forest capital in Trinidad and Tobago, preservation of biodiversity, enhanced watersheds, increasing aquifer 
discharge, flood avoidance, increase food production through agro-forestry, environmental and aesthetic enhancement and 
the alleviation of poverty through sustainable employment particularly among women and youth.” One of the principal 
targets of this co-management Programme is the replanting of 13,367 ha of state forest lands and reserves over a 10-year 
period, including the replanting of 4,452 ha of watershed which has been denuded or destroyed. The Programme calls for 
the initial replanting of 2,440 ha with 1,214 ha replanted annually thereafter with the anticipated involvement of 80 
communities generating employment for 2,500 persons. It consisted of two main components namely the planting 
component geared at replanting forested areas, and maintenance component designed to strengthen the capacity for 
policing forested areas. The major programme activities included: 
 
 
Identification of and defining denuded forested lands targeted for action; 
 
Implementation of annual plan of action by community groups for their given area; 
 
Preparation of the targeted denuded lands for planting; 
 
Production and planting of seedlings; 
 
Maintenance and protecting restored lands from fires and other hazards; 
 
Implementation and improvement of watershed management practices; 
 
Public education and awareness; and 
 
Facilitating and supporting forest and non-forest management entrepreneurial initiatives within the communities. 
 
Three years later, some 53 community-based organisations under the Programme had employed 1,855 persons and had 
replanted 1,825 ha, constructed 4,000 check dams, maintained 7,135 ha of forests, and protected 14,042 ha of forests. In 
addition, some groups opened and maintained 238 ha of parks and 271 km of trails, and cleared and cleaned 38 km of river 
courses of debris. Working with communities meant that the Programme can tap into indigenous knowledge about flora and 
fauna species in planning and implementing the forest restoration work plans and at the same time provide the necessary 
training in forest management and conservation to shift negative practices and behaviour relating to their interactions with 
the forests. To this end, 11,845 persons have been trained under this Programme on a variety of topics relating to forest 
management and protection and small business management. 
 
In Trinidad, the Programme covers three zones, engaging 19 groups each in the North and the South, and 14 in the East. 
In Tobago the Programme, which falls under the control of the THA, currently works with three communities.

additional 14,042 ha of forest protected over the last three years (Table 7.6). However, there was limited

Private initiatives include the 10-year ‘No Net Loss Forest Loss’ Reforestation project being 
undertaken by the NGC, in collaboration with communities, Government and NGOs, to reforest degraded 
areas in the Rochard Douglas and Mayaro/Victoria Forests and Morne L’Enfer Forest Reserves. In all, 
over 315 ha are being targeted for reforestation and rehabilitation. 
Fire Protection 
Key among the measures for maintaining forests are those geared at protecting the forests from 
fires. The Forestry Division and the NRWMP take the lead in their efforts at fire prevention which include 
the cutting of fire traces, and public education and outreach. 
Coastal and Marine Protected Areas 
The Buccoo Reef Marine Park remains the only marine area set aside for protection in Trinidad and 
Tobago, The reef has an area of 700 ha (0.012 % of Trinidad and Tobago’s total EEZ), suggesting that an 
insufficient proportion of the ecological components of the country’s marine environment are protected, 
and as such they remain highly vulnerable to the impacts of human activities. The EMA plans to 
designate the reef complex as an ESA. The Speyside Reef, though not legally zoned as protected, has 
been receiving special attention, including the preparation of a management plan and monitoring of the 
health of the zone. 
By the end of 2005, three wetlands, one in Tobago and two in Trinidad, had been designated 
as Ramsar Sites (Table 7.7). Altogether, they cover some 15,919 ha. 
Natural Resource Management 
Conservation and protection of species habitat requires the Government and the people of Trinidad 
and Tobago to engage in systems which would allow for sustainable management of biodiversity. A 
strategy and an action plan developed for Trinidad and Tobago as part of its obligations to the UNCBD 
provide a number of initiatives for environmental education, policy rationalisation and reform, legislative 
reform and enforcement, strengthening of research and information management capacity, and for 
institutional strengthening. As part of its package of strategies, the UNCBD action plan also calls for the 
development and implementation of management plans for protected areas, prohibited areas or

country’s natural resources, for others no such plan exists. Moreover, where plans are present, it does 
not necessarily mean that management of the resources will follow recommended guidelines. 
In Trinidad, the UNCBD Programme covers three zones, engaging 19 groups each in the North 
and the South, and 14 in the East. In Tobago the Programme, which falls under the control of the THA, 
currently works with three communities. 
7.3 Species Protection 
7.3.1 Description 
Species protection considers the vulnerability of local flora and fauna to the conditions in Trinidad 
and Tobago. An important aspect of this is the status of species as it relates to their existence in their 
natural habitat - whether or not they exist in adequate numbers, are under threat, or rare. Species are 
classified as endangered, vulnerable or rare. Endangered species are those whose numbers are so low 
that they are close to extinction, whilst vulnerable species can become endangered in the near future if 
steps are not taken to correct causal factors leading to reduction in their population levels. Rare species, 
on the other hand, are those with historically low populations that are at risk. 
7.3.2 Pressure 
The threats and pressures reported in the previous section on Habitat Conservation and Protection 
are also relevant under this section. These include forest degradation and loss, forest fires, pollution, and 
declining water quality. Added to these pressures is the over-exploitation of certain species, including 
timber species, tree ferns, orchids, freshwater fish species and prawns, marine species particularly 
demersal fish species and sea turtles, cage bird species, and game animals including deer, lappe, quenk, 
ducks and waterfowl (EMA 1998a). Some of this over-exploitation is culturally based, for example, there is 
a very high demand for ‘wild meat’ which is consumed locally as delicacy and now also sold 
commercially. The ‘fear’ of snakes means that they are immediately killed whether they are venomous or 
not. 
Some harvesting methods can be destructive, for example the illegal use of trap guns; the setting 
of fires when hunting game species; the cutting and destruction of mangrove to remove oysters; and the 
use of bleach in the harvesting of lobsters. 
Over-exploitation has led to significant reductions in some species population levels, while their 
populations in some areas have disappeared. For example, the manatee was once found in Caroni Swamp 
but is now believed to be absent from this area because of over-exploitation.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 164
text:
137 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
7.3.3 Current Status and Trends 
Little work has been done on the ecological status of many individual species and as such Trinidad 
and Tobago needs to embark on an assessment of the status and numbers of its species for better resource 
management. Nevertheless, a comprehensive revision of the checklist of the flora of Trinidad and Tobago 
was conducted during 2002 by the National Herbarium and Kew Gardens, England. To date, there are over 
2,500 recorded plant species in Trinidad and Tobago in about 175 families (Table 7.8). There are 2,160 
flowering plant species40, 91 of which are endemic to Trinidad and 21 are endemic to Tobago. Of the plant 
species recorded, there are some 845 introduced and naturalised plant species which account for about 24 
percent of the total number of vascular plants. 
Trinidad and Tobago is believed to have as many as 10 times the amount of flora as fauna. To date 
over 1,000 animal species has been discovered. Table 7.8 illustrates the present knowledge about the 
number of faunal species in Trinidad and Tobago. Except in the case of domestic and farmed animals, 
there are limited records of the introduced and naturalised fauna. There are several possible fauna that 
are considered endemic, but further studies are required to conclusively prove that these species do not, 
in fact, exist elsewhere.

Threatened and Vulnerable Species 
A large number of threatened species reflects the vulnerability of a country’s biodiversity. There are 
very few studies on the threatened species in Trinidad and Tobago. Labelling a species as rare or 
threatened is based on local knowledge and not necessary based on scientific assessment. As a result, 
the information on threatened species is highly variable. The World Conservation Union (IUCN) Red List 
of Threatened Species, documents 37 threatened animal species, but carries no corresponding listing for 
plant species41. The manatee or sea cow, green turtle, river otter, pawi, and blue and yellow macaw are 
among those listed as endangered. Table 7.9 shows the current listed threatened animal species in 
Trinidad and Tobago as categorised by the 2006 IUCN Red List in relation to other Caribbean countries. 
In contrast, Agard and Gowrie (2003) noted the total number of threatened species as 147 and 
total number of endangered species as 16. The Wildlife Section of the Forestry Division provides a 2005 
list of 20 endangered, 10 vulnerable and 18 rare species (Box 3). 
Exploited Species 
Trinidad and Tobago’s species are mainly exploited for commercial and recreational purposes and for 
home use. Data on the exploitation and management of some of the country’s species are collected by various 
Government agencies including the Forestry Division and the Fisheries Division, some of which will be

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 170

143 
 
 
Game Species 
The Wildlife Section of the Forestry Division prepares an annual report which provides information 
on the annual sale of game licences, annual number of hunters, and the annual harvest data of wild meat. 
These data can be used to monitor and assess legal hunting. However illegal hunting, which refers to 
hunting without a licence and outside of the hunting season, is a major issue and as such alternative 
methods must be used to evaluate the extent of the problem. Game animals are hunted legally for five 
months, between October and February. 
The number of animal species exploited continues to be small compared to the total number of 
animal species found in Trinidad and Tobago. Game licences were issued for agouti, cage birds, deer, 
lappe, alligator, iguana, quenk (wildhog), armadillo (tattoo) and waterfowl. Over the last 16 years the 
numbers of hunters and State game licences issued have increased steadily (Figures 7.7 and 7.8). In 
the 1990/91 season the number of licensed hunters totaled 5,880 but this figure has increased by over 
50 % reaching 9,133 licensed hunters in the 2004/05 hunting season. Similarly, some 7,428 game 
licences were issued for the 1990/91 hunting season but 16 years later that figure had doubled, 
reaching 14,910. Based on the number of licences issued, agouti hunting continues to be the most 
popular form of game hunting, with the number of agouti game licences sold varying between 34.6 % 
and 45.0 % of the total number of licences. Table 7.11 shows the number of game licences sold by 
type of game animal species. 
Given that hunters are required to fill and return the mandatory data form which provides 
information on harvest statistics, the Wildlife Section is able to estimate the total number of game animals 
caught and their total weight, and thus estimate the total value of the annual legal harvest. For the 
2002/2003 hunting season, sales of state game licences totaled TT$276,500, while the total value of the 
harvested wild meat was estimated at TT$12.3 million (Table 7.12). However, it should be remembered 
that this figure only represents a portion of the total value of the industry, given that there is no estimation 
of other direct and indirect costs associated with hunting and the consumption of wild meat. In addition, 
there are no estimates of the size and value of illegal hunting which is believed to be extensive.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 174
text:
147 
 
 
public education and as pets. Several species of birds, (robins, blue and gold macaws, and yellow-
headed parrots) were the species most requested. However, the section cautions that these figures do 
not reflect the number of protected species in captivity since some persons do not comply with this 
licensing requirement. The species listed under CITES49 require a licence for export, import, re-export and 
introduction into a new area. Under this Convention, the Wildlife Section issued 80 permits in 2003/2004 
to export species, the majority of which included the orange-winged parrots exported for personal use and 
other animals such as lizards, scorpions, spiders, bats and frogs. 
7.3.4 Response 
Prohibited Areas 
Arresting the decline of, or increasing population levels of threatened and vulnerable species and 
securing their habitat and food sources are critical measures towards species protection. 
The declaration of the 13 wildlife sanctuaries to date is geared mainly at prohibiting hunting in 
these ‘sacred’ areas but also presents the opportunity for the protection of habitat and food supply. 
However, the declaration of these areas appears to focus on the protection of bird species. Of the 13 
sanctuaries, 6 are dedicated to the protection of nesting or roosting bird species (Table 7.13), with 5 of 
these areas being small islands, islets and rocks where breeding colonies of seabirds are to be protected 
from poaching and hunting50. 
Of the 7 remaining wildlife sanctuaries, 3 are located in the hilly and mountainous terrain, also 
representing a geographical spread, that is, the Northern Range, Central Range, and Southern 
Range.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 175
text:
148 
 
 
 
 
Environmentally Sensitive Species (ESS) 
The Environmentally Sensitive Species (ESS) Rules of 2001 allow the EMA to designate any 
species of living plants or animals that are endemic, endangered or vulnerable as ESS based on scientific 
evidence or international treaties to which Trinidad and Tobago is a signatory. This designation, along 
with the implementation of a species management plan, is geared at the full recovery of the species to the 
point where the protection of being listed as an ESS is not necessary. 
In 2004/2005, 3 species were designated as ESS, namely, the white-tailed sabrewing hummingbird 
(Campylopterus ensipennis), the West Indian manatee (Trichechus manatus), and the Trinidad piping 
guan or ‘pawi’ (Pipile pipile). Discussion and research were initiated by the EMA in 2005 regarding 5 
species of marine turtles: the loggerhead turtle (Caretta caretta), the leatherback turtle (Dermochelys 
coriacea), the olive ridley turtle (Lepidochelys olivacea), the green turtle (Chelonia mydas), and the 
hawksbill turtle (Eretmochelys imbricata). 
Recovery Plans 
Recovery plans are considered an important response to address the issue of declining 
biodiversity. One objective of the EMA’s Strategic Work Plan for the period 2003-2008 calls for the 
development and implementation of management/ recovery plans for 10 species identified to be 
designated as ESS. These include the 5 species of marine turtles mentioned above, the West Indian 
manatee, the white-tailed sabrewing hummingbird, the pawi, the ocelot (Leopardus pardalis) and the 
golden tree frog (Phyllodytes auratus). 
So far, recovery plans have been developed for the manatee and sea turtles. However, information 
on the outcomes and highlights of the implementation of these plans in the recovery of these species are 
limited at this time. It was expected that information available from current research on the manatee could 
be used to update its recovery plan, and be used in its management. The St Augustine Campus of the 
UWI is currently undertaking research on the white-tailed sabrewing hummingbird and the pawi. It is 
expected that this on-going research will serve as the basis for the development of species recovery 
plans. 
The Cook’s Tree Boa (Corallus ruschenbergerii) 
can be seen during the tour of Caroni Swamp

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 176
text:
149 
 
 
Role of Co-Management in Species Protection 
The NGO and community sector have been instrumental in raising awareness of the value of 
biodiversity in Trinidad and Tobago and the benefits of conservation and protection of natural 
resources. A number of NGOs and community based organisations (CBOs) in north-east and 
south-east Trinidad and in Tobago have worked with State agencies in managing the country’s 
biodiversity through the conduct of community-based conservation, eco-tourism, and research 
projects. 
7.4 Biodiversity and Climate Change 
The biodiversity of Trinidad and Tobago, like elsewhere, is largely characterised by its climate 
regime among other things. Any changes in climate therefore are likely to directly and indirectly affect 
biodiversity. Increased ambient and sea temperatures; changing rainfall patterns and amounts; rising sea 
level; and increased frequency of upset climatic events (such as storms and hurricanes) would adversely 
alter the country’s ecosystems and affect species habitat and food supply. 
7.4.1 Pressure 
The levels of carbon dioxide in the earth’s atmosphere have been on the increase since the 
coming of the industrial age. Along with this increase in carbon dioxide levels are changes in the 
global climate. These predicted changes in global climate and changes in the climate of Trinidad and 
Tobago are discussed in some detail in Chapter Three and summarised in Table 7.14. 
Global climate has changed significantly in the last 150 years. Currently, temperature increase 
is accelerating and this will have direct and indirect, regional and local impacts, with some of the 
most severe impacts predicted to take place on SIDS (IPCC 2001b). A temperature increase as high 
as 6 ºC (worst case scenario) is predicted by 2100, with increasingly severe storms in the Caribbean. 
It is difficult to predict the changes in rainfall that are to be expected since the confidence level is 
low. A report done on behalf of the Department for International Development (DFID) indicates that 
latest projections suggest that rainfall is more likely to be higher in the Eastern Caribbean during 
December to February, and more likely to be lower between June-August. Already there is some 
evidence of increased rainfall during the early dry season period (December – January) but it is too 
early to suggest that this will be a future trend. Moreover, changes in climate elsewhere could affect 
the Caribbean region. For example, the increased occurrence of drought in northern Africa is 
believed to be responsible for the increased frequency of Sahara dust events noted in the Caribbean. 
7.4.2 Current State and Trends 
A number of potential impacts on species habitat and biodiversity have been identified with 
changing climate and sea level rise. Among the most pronounced and relevant in Trinidad and Tobago 
are: 
 
Terrestrial ecosystems species – The survival and distribution of most species are influenced 
by climate. Many species have been able to successfully respond to climate change 
throughout evolutionary time; today the main concern is the impact this accelerated rate of 
change will have on species. The 2001 IPCC Report (IPCC 2001b) notes that impoverishment 
of species has occurred in the past during times of abrupt or rapid climate change. Impacts of 
climate change on species would also likely be from physiological stress and changes and 
loss in habitat, especially from fires and increased storm events.

 
Wetlands and coastal ecosystems and species – Sea level rise will affect wetlands especially 
those in Trinidad through salt water intrusion which would change the vegetation. Already salt 
water intrusion is a problem in some wetlands and coastal lowlands such as Bejucal and Felicity 
in central Trinidad, and in the lower and middle sections of the Oropouche Lagoon in the south. In 
both regions salt water intrusion was exacerbated by the deterioration of irrigation and drainage 
systems associated with the production of rice and other crops, thus resulting in saline soil 
conditions and a change in the ecology of the areas. Sea level rise could severely affect the 
Oropouche Lagoon where the salt water intrusion impacts were greatest, and thus reverse the 
current recovery of the lagoon achieved through major repairs in the area undertaken three to 
four years ago.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 178
text:
151 
 
 
Marine Ecosystems and Species 
While Sahara dust may bring minerals and nutrients that may change the chemical composition 
of the ocean to the benefit of some species, there is mounting evidence to suggest that dust storms 
may be accelerating the loss of coral reefs in the Caribbean. Preliminary work by the USGS and 
others are showing that Sahara dust can bring micro-organisms and chemical pollutants, such as a 
fungus known to cause a coral-reef-killing disease, across the Atlantic to the Caribbean. Some also 
speculate that the increased deposit of iron in the marine waters could increase the populations of 
harmful species of algae, such as those that cause red tides. The increased frequency of Sahara dust 
events could mean increased risks to species and the ecological balance of marine environment. 
7.4.3 Response 
Chapter Three also examined the initiatives taking place to address the issue of climate change 
and therefore would be of relevance to this Section. However, limited feedback was received on the 
initiatives which address specifically the issue of Biodiversity and Climate Change. 
Socio-economic development requires that Trinidad and Tobago take a multi-sectoral approach 
which includes addressing the urgent issue of climate variability and change. This requires the 
adaptation of initiatives which would continuously assess and monitor vulnerabilities to climate 
change. Therefore, the collection of information and its translation into user friendly formats are 
important for easy interpretation by all users. The preparation of a sustainable land use plan for 
Trinidad and Tobago is a great opportunity for the country to take the critical issue of climate change 
on board during a comprehensive national development process. It is also important to develop and 
implement research programmes geared at assessing the impacts of climate change on local 
biodiversity. 
 
 
 
 
Egrets are a common sight in wetlands

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 179
text:
152 
 
 
Chapter 8: Economy, Industry, Resource 
Use 
 
 
8.1 Overview 
The natural resources of Trinidad and Tobago have been and continue to be the foundation for 
socio-economic development. The country was principally an agricultural economy up to the beginning of 
the twentieth century, when economic development was based mainly on sugar production, and smaller 
outputs of cocoa, coconuts, and coffee. 
Today, however, the exploitation of oil and natural gas resources drives the economy. Now on its 
second energy boom, Trinidad and Tobago has developed several energy intensive industries, including 
a range of petrochemicals, thus achieving the rank of being amongst the top five LNG producers in the 
world. The country is the largest exporter of ammonia and methanol, and is the third largest nitrogen 
producer globally. The world’s largest methanol plant can be found in Trinidad and Tobago. Undoubtedly, 
the per capita use and total use of natural gas have increased significantly. Apart from the demand for 
natural gas which is used as a fuel and a feedstock, petrochemical industries create a demand for power 
and water. 
Notwithstanding the contribution of the oil and gas sector to the economy, the utilisation of other 
natural resources has also contributed to recent socio-economic development in Trinidad and Tobago. 
Coastal and marine resources are important for both the fisheries and tourism industries, particularly to 
the economy of Tobago where both are the mainstay. Although Tobago’s main tourism product is based 
primarily on sun, sea, sand and reefs, it has a growing eco-tourism industry based upon the natural 
resources of Little Tobago and the Main Ridge, the oldest forest reserve in the western hemisphere, and 
the peaceful lifestyle of neighbouring villages such as Speyside and Charlotteville. For Tobago, the 
processing of flying fish is an important economic activity, with the operation of three fish processing 
plants. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Gas-based industry, Point Lisas 
Aerial view of the LNG pipelines from offshore 
to onshore, Guayaguayare

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 180
text:
153 
 
 
Urban consumption of natural resources is also considerable. The official target of 10,000 
additional housing units annually has consequences for increased demand for land space, the 
densification of existing neighbourhoods, and the expansion into green areas. Domestic users account for 
the largest freshwater withdrawals, and total electricity use is also high. 
This chapter focuses on three aspects of Economy, Industry and Resource Use, namely energy, 
water, and road travel and transportation. Other issues such as fisheries and forestry have been 
addressed in the chapter on Biodiversity. The issue of quarrying has been dealt with as part of Chapter 
Four. 
8.2 Energy Use 
8.2.1 Description 
The energy sector is the impetus for growth in the Trinidad and Tobago economy accounting for 
40.5 % of total GDP in 2004 and 41.2 % in 2005 with an overall projected growth of 20.6 % in the sector. 
The petrochemical sub-sector, which includes the production of methanol, nitrogenous fertilisers 
(ammonia and urea), and natural gas liquids, continues to expand along with the increased production of 
natural gas (Ministry of Finance 2006). 
This expansion in the energy sector is propelled mainly by the commencement of operations at the 
BHP Billiton oil field in early 2005 and the coming on stream of the Atlantic LNG Train IV in December 
2005. This new train increased Atlantic LNG's overall output by almost 50 %, making this the largest LNG 
train in the world at 5.2 million tonnes per year of LNG. As a result, Trinidad and Tobago is now 
positioned as the fifth-largest exporter of LNG worldwide (Ministry of Finance 2006). 
8.2.2 Pressure 
The Vision 2020 National Development Strategy calls for an expansion of the energy sector into 
value-added oil and gas-based products and services along the value chain (Ministry of Planning and 
Development 2006). Therefore, the country’s economic future remains entrenched in the energy sector as 
further efforts are made to deepen its gas-based industrialisation process. Consequently, in 2005, 2 
additional plants were commissioned, bringing the total number of gas-based plants in the country to 27 
(Table 8.1). This number is expected to further increase with the emergence of new prospects for 
additional downstream gas-based activities. The construction of 2 aluminium smelter plants is proposed 
within the next five years (Box 8.1), along with plans for the establishment of plants for production of 
melamine, urea, ammonia nitrate, acetic acid and polypropylene51. 
This growing industrial capacity and booming economic activities have implications for the 
country’s oil and gas reserves, water resources as well as for electricity generation. Already some experts 
are questioning whether the country has sufficient energy reserves to sustain these industries in the long 
term. In 2004, oil and gas reserves stood at 756 million proven barrels of oil and 18.8 trillion cubic feet of 
proven gas reserves, respectively (Table 8.2). 
With respect to the natural gas exports, the USA remains the main market for Trinidad and Tobago 
LNG, with between 75 and 80 % of all LNG imported into the USA originating from Trinidad and Tobago. 
The 2006 Report entitled ‘Natural Gas: Factors Affecting Prices and Potential Impacts on Consumers' 
(United States Government Accountability Office 2006) 
 
51 Reported during Trinidad and Tobago’s 2005/06 national budget presentation

Box 8.1: Developments in the Iron and Steel Industry 2005
“Agreements were reached between the National Energy Corporation (NEC), on behalf of the Government of Trinidad and 
Tobago and a number of foreign firms for the construction of two aluminium smelters and an integrated iron and steel plant. A 
joint venture company, Alutrint, owned by the NEC (60 %) and Sural, a Venezuela-based company (40 %) was established. It 
was proposed that Alutrint in collaboration with the China Machinery and Equipment Import Corporation (CMEC) would build one 
of the smelters with a capacity of 125,000 tonnes per year. The other smelter with a 325,000 tonnes per year capacity will be 
built by the US aluminium producer, Alcoa. The smelters would source bauxite from Guyana, Suriname and Jamaica. The iron 
and steel complex will be built by S.R. Steel of India. Construction of these plants is carded to begin in 2006 respectively. 
Meanwhile, production of billets fell by 9.9 % to 712,000 tonnes. The decline in production was reflected in export levels of direct-
reduced iron and wire rods which fell by 6.7 % and 19.1 % respectively. Production of hot-briquette iron totaled 130.7 thousand 
tonnes for the first ten months of 2005 following the commissioning of the plant in late 2004. However, teething problems at the 
new facility caused production to be unsteady during the year. In the period January to October the facility exported a total of 
117.4 thousand tonnes of product.”

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 183
text:
156 
 
 
noted that the demand for natural gas in the USA is expected to increase by 60 % by 202052. Trinidad and 
Tobago’s position in the market is likely to remain strong. 
8.2.3 State and Trends 
Domestic Oil and Gas Utilisation 
The 2005 Annual Economic Survey Report by the Central Bank (Central Bank of Trinidad and 
Tobago 2005) shows production of crude oil (including condensates) averaged 144.4 thousand barrels 
per day in 2005 and resulted in a total annual production of 52.7 million barrels, a 27.2 % increase over 
the 2001 figure (Table 8.3). Natural gas production grew even faster than crude oil. Between 2001 and 
2005, natural gas production and utilisation doubled, giving an average of 3,219 million cubic feet per day 
and 3,033 million cubic feet per day, respectively. In 2005, the LNG industry accounted for 49.2 % of total 
usage, followed by the ammonia industry (16.5 %), the methanol industry (12.4 %) and power generation 
(7.6 %). The remainder was absorbed by the iron and steel industry and other small consumers. Table 
8.3 shows the production and utilisation in the oil and gas sector. 
Electricity Generation and Consumption 
Use of energy is important in improving people’s standard of living, and as such, availability of 
electricity is used as a measure of living conditions. At the same time, electricity generation can damage 
the environment, depending largely on how it is generated. More than 99 % of Trinidad and Tobago‘s 
electricity source is natural gas which is considered to be well suited environmentally for power 
generation because of its low carbon to hydrogen ratio. 
While the population size stabilised over the decade (1995 to 2004), electricity generation rose by 
about 59 % to 6.7 billion kwh, with per capita consumption climbing from 2,702.5 kwh in 1995 to 4,543.7 
kwh in 2004. Industrial activities accounted for the highest electricity consumption, averaging 65 % of total 
electricity consumed and 2.5 times the amount consumed by residential users, the second highest 
consumers (Table 8.4). 
8.2.4 Response 
Non-renewable energy use, in the form of fossil fuels, will continue to be the main engine of socio-
economic growth and development in Trinidad and Tobago for as long as supply lasts and its utilisation 
remain viable. Therefore, the use of renewable energy sources is not likely to be given priority at this 
time. 
However, there has been a recent call by the South Chamber of Industry and Commerce and the 
Caribbean Association of Industry and Commerce for the development of a Common Caribbean Energy 
Policy (South Chamber of Industry and Commerce 2006) which should address five critical issues. 
Among them is the issue of the environment and energy conservation. In their argument for such an 
energy policy, the authors of the framework note that there should be a focus on both mitigating negative 
environmental impacts of the energy industry and on encouraging energy conservation, through 
consumer education programmes and development of appropriate renewable technology. These will be 
more cost efficient if undertaken at a regional level.

8.3 Water Use 
Natural availability of freshwater is affected by the occurrence and distribution of rainfall and the 
occurrence, movement and quality of surface water and groundwater. In Trinidad and Tobago, rainfall 
distribution is characterised by seasonal and geographical variation. Agard and Gowrie (2003) quantifies 
renewable water53 at 4.005 billion m3 per year for Trinidad, and 0.206 billion m3 per year for Tobago. 
Using the EVI scoring system it was shown that both Trinidad and Tobago have a very high demand on 
their water resources. This suggests therefore that the water resources should be carefully managed and 
that encroachment into watershed areas should be prevented in order to preserve their integrity. 
 
53 Mean percentage of water usage/year met from renewable and non-declining sources

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 186
text:
159 
 
 
8.3.1 Pressure 
In Trinidad and Tobago water is consumed by mainly industrial and domestic users with small 
volumes consumed for agricultural production. Therefore, unless there is a policy which focuses on 
improving agricultural production through the use of irrigation, the discussion on water demand should 
zero in on domestic, industrial, and unaccounted for water. Figures for long-term trends for water demand 
in Trinidad and Tobago are shown in Table 8.5. These figures reveal that the demand for domestic water 
will continue to dominate, almost doubling by 2025. The loss of water through illegal connections, faulty 
pipelines and treatment process (unaccounted for water) is estimated at 41.8 % and 29.2 %, with less 
because of increased demand. Water demand by industrial users was estimated at 21.6 % in 2005 and 
expected to increase to 26 % by 2025. 
The main non-consuming demand for Trinidad and Tobago relates to demand for water. Rivers 
must maintain a minimum of 20 % of the natural river flow in order to maintain healthy ecosystems. 
Water wastage is another critical issue affecting water demand and use, which places responsibility 
on the customer for reducing water use. While many households receive a limited supply of water or none 
at all, there are others with faulty taps, pipelines, and tanks that go unchecked for considerable periods of 
time. Water conservation is another important area which needs to be effectively addressed, particularly 
with respect to the recycling of water (e.g. use of treated wastewater for the hotel sector and other 
industries); purchase of water saving devices (low-volume shower heads and toilets); and reduction of 
night use of water through the flushing of toilets and other activities. In their presentation on Leak 
Reduction in Point Lisas Industrial Estate, Joseph and Houlder (2003) noted that the UK uses 1.77 
litres/property/hour while WASA uses 6.0 litres/ property/hour. 
8.3.2 Current State and Trends 
Domestic users will continue to be the largest consumers of water in Trinidad and Tobago for some 
time to come. World Bank Statistics estimate annual freshwater withdrawals to be 0.3 billion m3. Domestic 
usage averaged 68 % during the period 1987 to 2002, while industrial activities consumed 26 % of the 
water withdrawn (Table 8.6). The actual abstractions of surface and groundwater for Trinidad and Tobago 
in 2000 were 278 million m3 per year and 81.1 million m3 per year respectively (Table 8.7). 
Unaccounted water is another area which requires further attention, particularly at the district level. 
Table 8.8 shows the proportion of unaccounted water for several districts in Trinidad, which ranged 
between 17.7 % and 90.5 % during 2002 to 2003. 
 At present, there is reportedly an annual 14 % deficit in the supply of water and this figure is 
projected to increase by 27 % by 2015. These deficits persist despite an apparent abundance of water in 
Trinidad and Tobago and recent investments to improve water supply including the installation of a 
desalination plant at Point Lisas. This deficit in water supplied to consumers is due to low surface water 
flows during the dry season and high turbidity of surface water which often lead to increased shutdown at 
water collection facilities. Recent hydro-geological studies in Tobago and Trinidad have shown additional 
potential groundwater capacity which can yield supplies through the application of technologies for 
abstracting deep groundwater in bedrock aquifers. 
Pollution is another issue affecting water usage. Many water sources are now polluted resulting in 
increases to the unit cost of producing potable water.
