8.3.3 Response 
WASA is currently making efforts to improve and upgrade water infrastructure to increase the water 
supply to the population of Trinidad and Tobago. To achieve its objectives, WASA installed 50 km of 
pipeline in 2002 and increased this figure to 100 km annually over the last two years. During 2003 to 
2005, the Authority has also rehabilitated and constructed production wells and booster stations. One 
hundred and fifty thousand customers are reported to have benefited from a recently improved water 
supply. The ultimate goal of WASA is to increase public access to pipe-borne water from 92 % to 95 % 
and increase the number of customers receiving a 24 hour supply throughout the week from 26 % to 36 
% over the next three years (Box 8.2). 
 
Box 8.2: WASA’s Plans for Improving Water Supply 
(Source: Vision 2020 Planning Committee 2003a) 
 
About 92 % of the population has access to drinking water, although this is at varying levels of service. One of the principal aims 
of WASA is the upgrading of the water sector so that water services are delivered to the entire population 24 hours a day, seven 
days a week (24/7). This is part of the National Development Vision to attain developed country status by 2020. At present, 26 % 
of the population gets a 24/7 water supply while water is supplied to some communities on a weekly schedule. However, there 
are many communities without a regular water supply. Consequently, over the next two years WASA plans increase access to 
water from 92 % to 95 % and as such initiatives are being implemented currently to provide a water supply to communities with 
limited or no access to pipe-borne water and to socially depressed areas. 
 
As part of its development plans, WASA also proposes to increase the number of consumers receiving 24/7 supply of potable 
water from 26 % to 36 % over the next two years. This is to be achieved through the upgrading and rehabilitation of water 
infrastructure including the replacement of existing pipelines. At present, new pipelines are being installed at a rate of 100 km per 
annum with a targeted 150,000 customers receiving an improved water supply between 2003 and 2005. 
 
Apart from the replacement of old pipelines, WASA has undertaken a number of initiatives to 
reduce unaccounted water. The implementation of the district metered areas (DMA) is the primary 
method used to address this problem, with 40 DMA established by 2003 (Joseph and Houlder 2003). 
Metering of individual households is also being considered so that domestic customers will pay for water 
actually used. In addition, a public relations campaign is usually conducted each year in the dry season 
focusing on water conservation in that critical period. However, there is a need to extend this campaign 
throughout the year to include other aspects of water conservation. 
One response to shortage and intermittent supply of water, on the part of householders and 
businesses, is the installation and use of high-volume water tanks for storage. Although the use of water 
tanks is pervasive, there is limited data which provide a true picture of their importance and use. 
8.4 Road Travel and Transportation 
8.4.1 Introduction/ Overview 
Transportation is an important element of the economic and social development of Trinidad and 
Tobago economy. With a significant proportion of the population having to travel outside of their 
communities to key urban centres to access jobs, schools and government services, Trinidad and Tobago 
has become a very mobile society. Most travel in Trinidad and Tobago involves private cars and taxis 
(cars, small and large maxi-taxis). However, traffic congestion is a worsening problem throughout Trinidad 
and in downtown Scarborough, particularly during peak periods, as the road network is inadequate to 
handle the volume of vehicles, and no mass transport system exists as an alternative. Movement 
between Trinidad and Tobago is achieved via air and sea travel and in recent times has improved 
tremendously with the introduction of new ferries on the Tobago-Trinidad route.

8.4.3 State and Trends 
Travel Patterns and Traffic Volume 
The predominant movement of vehicular traffic is between settlements/communities and urban or 
town centres. To date, there has been no comprehensive study on travel patterns in Trinidad and 
Tobago. Instead information on travel patterns is derived from sporadic and specific measurements 
geared at addressing targeted issues. However anecdotal evidence suggests that private cars play a 
major role in the journey to work, school, and social activities. The public transportation system consists 
mainly of taxis and small and large maxi taxis. A public bus service operated by the Public Transport 
Service Corporation, also plays a significant role, offering services mainly to and from main urban centres 
and outer districts/communities, for example the East-West Corridor, North-South Corridor, and to and 
from San Fernando to areas such Mayaro, Point Fortin and Siparia. 
The technical report on Greater Port of Spain Local Area Plan (Halcrow Group et al. 2000) 
indicated that in 1996 just over 200,000 people were estimated to be travelling into the city area daily, 
with about 40,000 during peak hours, with an estimated 50 % of these commuters using public transport. 
It was estimated that by 2000, approximately 500,000 persons daily were travelling into and out of Port of 
Spain, which is equivalent to about 100,000 car trips and 20,000 public transport trips. The Port of Spain 
traffic trend therefore seems to be increasing every year. 
Congestion 
The current rate of vehicle ownership and traffic growth has placed great demands on the national 
transportation infrastructure resulting in gridlock in many of the country’s main roadways and access 
roads particularly, along the main East-West and North-South arterial roads in Trinidad, leading to loss of 
productivity and frustration among the travelling public. The once 30 to 45 minute journey from Arima to 
Port of Spain can now take as much as one and a half to two hours during peak hours, and the 45 
minute to one hour trip from San Fernando to Port of Spain can now extend up to two and a half hours. 
Many commuters are now forced to leave their homes at earlier hours than they have done in the past 
traversing back streets and roads to avoid the bulk of the traffic. Moreover traffic bottlenecks are no 
longer a weekday phenomenon — they can now also be experienced on weekends and public holidays.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 195
text:
168 
 
 
8.4.4 Response 
A number of measures are currently in effect or proposed which focus on the supply side of the 
transportation equation. These are geared at improving the current traffic situation and to allow for easier 
movement of people and freight. These measures include the upgrading of the existing road network, 
 
 
 
 
 
 
improvement in the public transportation system and the introduction of a mass transit rail system. 
However, given our land space limitations and the need to ensure environmental sustainability other 
measures are also necessary to curb the growth in demand. 
 Transportation Planning 
A National Transportation Plan is currently being prepared for Trinidad and Tobago. This 
comprehensive study and plan, which was commissioned in 2004, attempts to address the sea, air and 
land transportation needs of the country for the next 30 years30. However, the results of this planning 
process have not yet been made public. 
Upgrading the Nation’s Road Infrastructure 
The policy objective of the Government is to develop a road network to ensure that within Trinidad 
no destination will be more than two and half hours away. As a result, the Budget Statements of the last 
three years proposed a number of major road projects, all geared at extending and improving the East-
West and North-South Corridors to improve capacity and safety and opening up new road arterials: 
• 
Extension of the Solomon Hochoy Highway to Point Fortin; 
• 
Construction of a spur that parallels the SS Erin Road to cover areas from Debe to Erin; 
• 
Construction of a highway from San Fernando to Mayaro, via Princes Town and Rio Claro; 
• 
Construction of a highway from Port-of-Spain to the Chaguaramas Peninsula; 
• 
Construction of an Eco Roadway on the North Coast from Matelot to Blanchisseuse; 
• 
Extension of the Churchill Roosevelt Highway to Manzanilla via Sangre Grande; 
 
30 Reported during Trinidad and Tobago’s 2005/06 national budget presentation

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 196
text:
169 
 
 
• 
Extension of the Diego Martin Highway to Diamond Vale; 
• 
Construction of the interchange at the intersection of the Churchill Roosevelt and Uriah Butler 
Highways; 
• 
Construction of interchanges or overpasses at key intersections along the East-West Corridor, 
such as Aranguez, El Soccoro, Curepe, Macoya and Trincity. 
In addition, a roads concessionaire system is also proposed where developers would design, 
finance, build, maintain, and operate specific highways on behalf of the Government. 
Light Rail Mass Transit System 
Priority is also being given to the development of a Light Rail Mass Transit System which is 
expected to form the foundation of Trinidad’s transport system. A full feasibility study has been completed 
and on this basis, a decision was made to proceed with the system. The system will traverse over 120 km 
and serve over two-thirds of Trinidad’s population, and will link Port of Spain and San Fernando, as well 
as several town centres including Arima, Diego Martin, and Sangre Grande. Some segments of the rail 
system, those from Curepe to Port of Spain and from Curepe to Chaguanas, are expected to be in place 
by the year 2010. 
Sea Transportation 
Sea transportation to and from north and south Trinidad is also seen as an alternative to road 
transportation. Proposals for a water taxi system from Point Fortin to Carenage are currently being 
conceptualised. 
Travel Demand Management 
In 2005, licences were granted for dealers to import 21,860 cars. The MTI has since established an 
allocation system to limit the quantities of used motor cars imported into the country.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 214
text:
187 
 
 
ORGANISATION STRUCTURE 
 
The Authority is governed by a Board of Directors comprising nine members and a Chairman, appointed 
by the President of the Republic of Trinidad and Tobago. The Managing Director/CEO, who is appointed 
by the Board, is an ex-officio member of the Board, while the Manager, Corporate Services serves as the 
Corporate Secretary. The Board reports to the Minister with responsibility for the Environment. The 
Members of the Board and Board Committees are detailed in Appendix 1. 
 
The Authority itself is organized into five (5) responsibility centres: 
 
CEO’s Office 
Oversees the implementation of the policies and priorities of the Board and work programmes of the 
Authority as well as the co-ordination with other governmental entities and regional and International 
organisations. Preparation of the Annual Report also falls under this office. 
 
 
The Human Resource Unit 
The Human Resource Unit falls under the CEO’s office and is responsible for the following: 
• 
Manpower Planning 
• 
Succession Planning 
• 
Recruitment / Selection 
• 
Performance Management 
• 
Training 
• 
Industrial Relations 
• 
Compensation and Benefits 
• 
Employee Relations 
 
 
The Legal Services Department 
Responsible for the following: 
• 
Internal and External Instructing Services 
• 
Advising the Technical Services Department 
• 
Litigation - Preparation of Court Documents 
• 
Mediation/Alternate Dispute Resolution 
• 
Drafting of Legislation/Amendments 
 
A critical aspect of the work of this department involves translating, into legal form, technical instructions 
from the Technical Services Department. Internal instructing services are achieved through promoting an 
effective communication Network within the EMA and specifically between the Legal Services and the 
Technical Services Department. The Legal Services Department, in addition to making recommendations 
for draft legislation, is also actively engaged in the exercise of amending the Environmental Management 
Act and other pieces of legislation with direct bearing on environmental laws e.g. Summary Offences Act. 
 
Corporate Relations And Public Education Department 
Responsible for ensuring that the Authority establishes itself as a continuing presence and influence in 
the nation’s consciousness. In focusing on environmental improvement, the Corporate Relations and 
Public Education Department develops special education initiatives to change attitudes and behaviours 
towards Air, Water, Noise, Waste disposal and our Ecosystem. Its education programmes target various 
populations in schools and communities and often utilize print and electronic media.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 215
text:
188 
 
 
Technical Services Department 
Responsible for environmental improvements focusing on the following: 
 
Pollution Management 
 
Waste Management 
 
Management of Hazardous substances 
 
Emergency Incidents Response 
 
Complaints Investigation 
 
Site Remediation 
 
Biodiversity Protection and Conservation 
 
Market Based Instruments 
 
Environmental Monitoring and Research 
 
Inspection and Compliance Monitoring 
 
The department plays a critical role in the delivery of the Strategic Priorities of the Authority. 
 
 
Corporate Services Department 
Responsible for the following functions: 
• 
Financial Accounting 
• 
Fund/Treasury Management 
• 
Procurement and Asset Maintenance 
• 
Information Technology 
• 
Secretarial Services to the Board 
• 
General Administration 
• 
Health, Safety and the Work Environment 
 
The Corporate Services Department serves as the bedrock for the implementation of good governance 
and accountability. It has the primary responsibility for providing operational support to the organization 
and making available timely, accurate and complete information on the financial performance and status 
of the Authority for sound decision making.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 216
text:
189 
 
 
ACCOMPLISHMENTS FOR 2005 
 
 
CORPORATE RELATIONS AND PUBLIC EDUCATION DEPARTMENT 
 
 
COLLABORATIVE PROJECTS 
 
Anti-Litter Campaign 
 The EMA collaborated with TIDCO, the city and Borough Corporations of Port of Spain, San 
Fernando, Chaguanas and Arima, as well as members of the private sector, to place temporary 
bins along Carnival band routes before the Carnival weekend. The project, “Throw Your Waste in 
a Bin”, was complemented by television and radio advertisements. 
 
Forest Fire Awareness and Prevention 
 The EMA partnered with the Forestry Division to promote its Forest Fire Awareness and 
Prevention programme. A Forest Fire publicity and education programme was undertaken to 
sensitise the public to safe practices during the Dry Season. 
 
Best Village Environmental Competition 
 The EMA served on the Executive Committee of the Best Village Environmental and Sanitation 
Competition, an activity spearheaded by the Ministry of Community Development, Culture and 
Gender Affairs. This competition, which forms part of the overall Prime Minister’s Best Village 
Competition, is designed to encourage community residents across Trinidad and Tobago to come 
together and work towards the improvement, regeneration and maintenance of their local 
environments. 
 
Tidy T&T 
 The EMA continued to work with Tidy T&T, a joint project of the Rotary Clubs in Trinidad and 
Tobago. This is a national environmental improvement initiative intended to benefit all 
communities by capturing the popular imagination and encouraging an increased awareness and 
sensitivity of environmental issues. 
 
 
SCHOOLS’ PROGRAMMES 
The year 2005 marked the seventh year of competition for both the Primary Schools’ ‘Hands-on’ and the 
Secondary Schools’ Public Speaking programmes. The Department has also expanded its programmes 
to include private schools, as well as schools in Tobago. 
 
Lectures and Displays 
 Throughout 2005, the Public Education (PE) unit continued its lectures, workshops and displays 
for the nation’s schools, NGOs, CBOs, Faith-Based Organisations (FBOs), industrial and 
business companies and other civil society groups. 
 
Climate Change Project 
 The EMA participated in Education and Public Awareness initiatives of the Mainstreaming 
Adaptation to Climate Change (MACC) project, which involves 12 participating CARICOM 
member states. The principal objective of the MACC is to build capacity within CARICOM to 
develop adaptation strategies and measures, according to the United Nations Framework 
Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC). 
 
 The PE unit also participated in a Climate Change workshop in Uruguay. This workshop was 
organised for countries in Latin America and the Caribbean to address issues under Article 6 of 
the Convention. (Article 6 on the Convention deals with Education, Public Awareness and 
Training.)

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 217
text:
190 
 
 
VOLUNTEER PROGRAMME 
 
 EMA volunteers continued to work with staff of the Education and Public Awareness department 
on education and awareness projects. 
These volunteers act as arms of the EMA in the dissemination of information, the expansion of 
education and public awareness programmes, as well as support personnel. 
 
 
LEGAL SERVICES DEPARTMENT 
 
Nariva Swamp was finally designated as an Environmentally Sensitive Area pursuant to the 
Environmentally Sensitive Areas Rules, 2001. The effect of the designation is that the Nariva Swamp is 
deemed a Managed Resource Protected Area where there are prescribed limitations on the use and 
activities within the swamp. The Authority can effectively take enforcement action against anyone who 
contravenes provisions in the notice. Such action can take the form of what is termed breach of an 
“environmental requirement” whereby the Notice of Violation process is initiated. It is also important to 
note that under section 70(2) of the Environmental Management Act, No. 03 of 2000 that: 
 
“any person who knowingly or recklessly undertakes or conspires to allow any activity in an 
“environmentally sensitive area” or with respect to an “environmentally sensitive species” designated 
under section 41, which may have an adverse impact on the environment within such area or on such 
species, commits an offence and is liable, on conviction on indictment, to a fine of one hundred thousand 
dollars and imprisonment for two years.” 
 
Therefore, Criminal action can also be effected. 
 
 
 
TECHNICAL SERVICES DEPARTMENT 
 
The Strategic Priorities of the Environmental Management Authority (EMA) are Clean Air, Clean Water, 
Waste Management, Noise Management and Healthy Ecosystems. Based on these, the EMA’s work 
plan for each year is developed. In addition, the Enabling Strategies to support the work of the EMA 
and to facilitate the achievement of the strategic priorities are Communication, Networking, Organization 
Development, Systems/Legislative Review and Coordination /Government Relations. 
 
The EMA’s Technical Services Department (TSD) implements environmental projects of a technical 
nature based on the above strategic priorities. In addition, TSD executes operational functions such as 
processing Certificate of Environmental Clearance (CEC) applications (under the Certificate of 
Environmental Clearance Rules, 2001), processing Noise Variations (under the Noise Pollution Rules, 
2001), designating Environmentally Sensitive Areas and Species (under the Environmentally Sensitive 
Areas Rules, 2001 and Environmentally Sensitive Species Rules, 2001), and responding to 
environmental incidents and complaints. 
 
 
Establishment of EMA – South Office 
The Department was physically split in 2005 in two geographic locations, with the majority of staff in the 
head office in Port-of-Spain and a satellite office established in San Fernando. This was done to allow 
the officers quicker access to the EMA’s clients in South Trinidad and conversely to allow persons living 
in South Trinidad closer access to basic services offered by the EMA such as collecting application forms 
for CECs and Noise Variations, receiving said forms from the public and its help desk services.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 218
text:
191 
 
 
National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Network 
Equipment was procured and set up to measure carbon monoxide, sulfur and nitrogen oxides, ground 
level ozone and meteorological parameters in Point Lisas. Personnel from EMA and PLIPDECO were 
trained to maintain the equipment and collect data with ROSE Environmental Limited providing back-end 
support. 
This project represented the preliminary phase in establishing a National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring 
Network and has enabled technical capacity building in the field of air quality monitoring for those 
involved. 
The result was and continues to be the collection of continuous time-series ambient air quality data from 
the most industrialized part of Trinidad and Tobago. In addition, the EMA acquired a Particulate Matter 
(PM) Analyzer, which allowed for the collection of a more complete data set thus allowing for the 
calculation of air quality indices, which was not previously possible in the absence of PM data. 
 
The EMA was able to do a preliminary assessment of the quality of the ambient air at that location and 
characterise air pollution present. 
 
Vehicle Exhaust Emissions Standards 
One of the EMA’s strategic objectives under Clean Air is the incorporation of vehicle exhaust emission 
standards for the exhaust emissions of the various classes of motor vehicles into legally enforceable 
regulations. 
 
The committee established by the EMA to Phase Out the Use of Leaded Fuel was retained to make 
recommendations for the implementation of the vehicle emission standards. It analysed various 
possibilities and prepared a report, which identified options for the implementation of the said standards in 
June 2005, this was sent to the Ministry of Public Utilities and the Environment for further action. 
 
Draft Water Pollution Rules 
The Draft Water Pollution Rules were developed by the Environmental Management Authority pursuant to 
guidelines contained in the Environmental Management Act, 2000, the National Environmental Policy and 
the Waste Pollution Management Programme. 
 
 
In accordance with the requirements of Section 52(1) of the Act, the EMA conducted the Pollution 
Inventory Study in 1997, which provided a detailed assessment of existing significant sources of water 
pollution in Trinidad and Tobago. The Pollution Inventory Study, together with other reports, provided the 
basis for the development of a comprehensive Register of Water Pollutants in accordance with Section 
52(2); this forms the First Schedule of the Water Pollution Rules, as well as the maximum Permissible 
Levels of water pollutants, which form the Second Schedule of the Water Pollution Rules. 
In 2005, the Legislative Review Committee of the Ministry of the Attorney General requested that 
international benchmarks be included to justify the Second Schedule of the Draft Water Pollution Rules. 
This information was forwarded to the Committee and as at December 2005, the EMA was awaiting 
further instructions.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 219
text:
192 
 
 
Water Pollution Management Programme 
In 2005, a number of procedural documents were updated for administering the Water Pollution 
Registration and Permitting System These included: 
 
User’s Guide Manual for Water Pollution Registration 
 
User’s Guide Manual for Water Pollution Permitting 
 
Public Guide for the Application of Registration of Water Pollutants Sources 
 
Various application Forms along with Instructional Booklet. 
 
Prototype Permits for Quarries and Sewage Treatment Plants 
 
In addition, the EMA revised the development of the management processes and procedures that would 
be associated with the Water Pollution Rules once the Rules are made law. These instructional materials 
and informative exercises included: 
 
 
Development of an implementation strategy and action plan for the impending rules. 
 
Costing for start-up and maintenance of both the Water Pollution Registration and Permitting 
including calculations for application fees and water pollution charges. 
 
Experimental exercises with a select group of reviewers and the Water and Sewage Authority 
(WASA) in using the application forms associated with the draft rules to ensure clarity and 
applicability. 
 
Development and testing of a management information system to store, update, retrieve, track 
and query all data collected from the water pollutant source registration system. 
 
In-house meetings with EMA personnel to update and inform on rules and procedures. 
 
Groundwater quality monitoring for MTBE and BTEX at elected sources in Trinidad and Tobago 
 
In 2001, the EMA began a methodological assessment of groundwater quality in selected areas of 
Trinidad and Tobago. Initial studies involved surveys of temporary monitoring wells adjacent to selected 
service stations in Northern Trinidad. This assessment was initiated out of the need to evaluate the 
quality of ground water supplies used for potable water, with a view to better protecting human health and 
the environment, particularly due to increased use of Methyl Tertiary Butyl Ether (MTBE) as an alternative 
fuel additive coupled with the incidence of leaking underground fuel storage tanks at vehicle fuelling and 
service stations throughout the country. 
From November 2004 to April 2005, the EMA continued with its groundwater assessment programme 
expanding the study to include a wider coverage of Trinidad and Tobago focusing on the assessment of 
MTBE and BTEX in groundwater below selected service stations as well as potable water supplies 
managed by WASA. 
The final decision on which wells were to be included in the monitoring programme was based on 
accessibility to the well site, whether the wells were still intact and the location of the wells in relation to 
sites of known contamination. 
A total of eighteen production wells, seven observation wells and fourteen temporary monitoring wells 
were included in the final sampling programme. Sampling of potable water and ground water was 
performed in accordance with USEPA protocols. The six-month sampling programme of potable water 
and observation wells in WASA’s network and Temporary monitoring wells in EMA’s network allowed for 
some trends to be identified at key locations and for the identification of “hot spots” of pollution, which 
requires further monitoring and/or remediation.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 220
text:
193 
 
 
The shallow groundwater below the NP Service Station at Maraval Road, Port-of-Spain, was still 
contaminated although the Underground Storage Tanks (USTs) at this station were removed in 2003 by 
NP. The shallow groundwater below service stations in Scarborough and Canaan in Tobago as well as 
Charlieville, Central Trinidad, also indicated that the service stations in these areas were in need of 
upgrade and that site remediation is required. 
 
Among the potable water and observation wells in WASA’s network, only three “hot spots” were identified 
at Four Roads in the Diego Martin Producing Field, at Curepe in the Valsayn Producing Field and at Don 
Miguel Road, which is the offtake for the El Socorro Gravels. These wells require further routine 
monitoring and the service stations upgrade of these well should be inspected. 
 
Hazardous Waste Inventory Study 
 
Trinidad and Tobago is a signatory to the Basel Convention on the Transboundary shipment of 
Hazardous Wastes. As at 2005, Trinidad and Tobago has not yet reported on the volumes of waste 
generated. In order to keep up with international obligations, the Government of Trinidad and Tobago 
embarked on an inventory study to meet this requirement. 
 
The EMA is co-managing this project, which was launched in August 2005 and set for completion in early 
2006, and it is expected to generate data on hazardous waste volumes and categories. This information 
will be used to formulate strategies and policies for hazardous waste management. 
 
Designation of Environmentally Sensitive Species (ESSs) 
The Pawi, the Manatee and the White-tailed Sabre-wing hummingbird were identified for designation as 
ESSs in 2005, with the following achievements: 
 
Administrative records for the three ESSs were sent out for public comment from January-
February 2005. 
 
Notices were signed on World Environment Day- June 5, and gazetted on June 17, Legal 
Supplement Part B – Vol. 44, No. 98 - June 17, 2005. 
 
Legal Notices 123 (Manatee), 124 (Pawi), 125 (Sabre-wing hummingbird). 
 
In addition to the above, five species of Marine Turtles were identified for designation as ESSs. The five 
marine turtle species selected, Leatherback, Green, Hawksbill, Olive Ridley and Loggerhead are listed as 
globally endangered species by CITES, the SPAW Protocol and under the IUCN Red List. 
Draft Schedules for the five species were sent out to 30 stakeholder agencies with which the EMA has 
memoranda of understanding. At present, conflicting local legislation on marine turtles is delaying 
designation. 
 
Launch of the Strategic Environmental Assessment Unit to Trinidad and Tobago’s Energy Sector 
 
Energy Sector Stakeholders were presented in February 2005 with the two-year work plan for the 
Strategic Environmental Assessment Unit as it related to energy sector matters. The EMA used this 
forum as an opportunity to solicit feedback from stakeholders for use in future planning. It was also an 
opportunity to network and establish valuable relationships with the Energy Stakeholders.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 221
text:
194 
 
 
PLANS FOR 2006 
 
CORPORATE RELATIONS AND PUBLIC EDUCATION DEPARTMENT 
 
Schools’ Programmes 
 
The 8th annual Secondary Schools’ ‘Hands-on’ Competition and the Secondary Schools’ Public 
Speaking Competition will be launched in January 2006. The Corporate Relations and Public 
Education department will also work with the Tobago House of Assembly to launch these 
programmes in Tobago. 
 
The department will also be expanding its schools’ programme by inaugurating more 
environmental clubs in both primary and secondary schools. 
 
Media Campaigns 
 
Media campaigns will utilise television documentaries, print and radio advertisements and will 
focus on the CEC process, Water Pollution issues, as well as an ongoing Anti-Litter Campaign. 
 
Documentaries as well as features on the secondary and primary schools’ competitions will be 
produced in collaboration with Gayelle - The Channel. 
 
Public Awareness 
 
The department will continue to mount displays that highlight projects under the organisation’s 
strategic plan, as well as on commemorative days. 
 
In 2006, special emphasis will be placed on educating the public about designated areas and 
species under the ESS and ESA Rules. 
 
Collaborative Projects 
 
The department will continue to serve on the Disaster Management Committee of the Ministry of 
Public Utilities and the Environment. The goal of this committee is to ensure that the Ministry is 
ready to deal with disasters through the development of a disaster management programme that 
will outline the roles and responsibilities of all the agencies that fall under its purview. 
 
Collaboration will continue with the Ministry of Community Development, Culture and Gender 
Affairs on the Environment and Sanitation competition under the Prime Minister’s Best Village 
Competition. 
 
Public education and outreach initiatives on Article 6 issues of the UNFCCC. (Article 6 on the 
Convention deals with Education, Public Awareness and Training.) This will be done in 
partnership with the MACC project, under the Caribbean Community Climate Change Centre in 
Belize. 
 
Volunteer Programme 
The Volunteer Programme, which was launched in January 2004, will be expanded in 2006 to include 
additional volunteers. These individuals will act as arms of the EMA in the dissemination of information, 
the expansion of education and public awareness programmes and as support personnel.

title: EMA Annual Report 2005 — Page 222
text:
195 
 
 
TECHNICAL SERVICES DEPARTMENT 
 
Lead Remediation at two sites in East Trinidad 
This will represent follow up to work done by the EMA in 2000 at Demerara Road and La Chance Trace, 
Arima. These activities include compiling detailed information obtained from site investigations, 
assessments and sampling to facilitate decision-making on the mechanism of remediation. Since start up 
in January 2005, a significant portion of work was completed at Demerara Road and due to weather 
conditions and other factors it will be continuing in 2006. 
 
This project has faced challenges including waterlogged conditions due to heavy rainfall, the need to 
remove and relocate of wildlife present at Demerara Road, change in project scope to utilise ex situ 
remediation instead of in situ remediation as was initially proposed, the need to source a disposal site for 
the treated waste and firm decision on the future course of action for La Chance Trace residents. Due to 
the challenges mentioned above, the project remains incomplete. 
 
 
Designation of Environmentally Sensitive Areas (ESAs) 
The Aripo Savannas, the Buccoo Reef and the Nariva Swamp were prioritized for designation as ESAs in 
2005. Procedures for stakeholder and public consultations were executed and the following were 
achieved: 
 Administrative Record for Aripo Savannas sent out for public comment from March-May 2005 
 Draft Schedules for Nariva Swamp was sent out to stakeholder agencies from May-June 2005 
 Nariva Swamp Administrative Record put out for public comment from June–July 2005 
Designation of ESAs faced a significant setback because boundary descriptions have delayed the 
process. There were changes in boundary descriptions for Nariva Swamp and Aripo Savannas. In 
addition, the High Water Mark definition for the Buccoo Reef remains unresolved. 
 
 
Waste Management - Lead Remediation Projects and the Hazardous Waste Inventory initiated in 
previous years are to continue in 2006. 
 
Healthy Ecosystems – Designation of five species of marine turtles, research in the Matura National 
Park, upgrade of the Aripo Management Plan and the development of species recovery plans for the 
Sabre-wing Humming Bird and the Pawi were started in 2005 and are expected to be completed in 2006. 
 
Environmental Assessment Unit - The scoping phase for a Strategic Environmental Assessment for the 
North Coast Marine Area (NCMA) will be initiated in 2006. Capacity building exercises will be conducted 
to boost the ability of the EMA and its team of local external reviewers in reviewing Environmental Impact 
Assessment (EIAs). Developmental work on the CEC Database is expected to occur in 2006. 
 
Geographic Information Systems (GIS) - The EMA expects to complete its GIS Needs Assessment in 
2006. 
 
Structural Changes - In 2006, the EMA expects to establish a formalised unit for the monitoring of CEC 
Certificates. Currently, this function in managed as part of the work carried out by the Compliance Unit 
which deals primarily with environmental incidents and complaints.
